Both opponents show strength with a 3-bet and call. I should fold and preserve my short stack for a better opportunity where I can leverage my position more effectively.

I have flopped top pair with a 5 kicker on a very coordinated board of Ace, King, Jack. This board texture hits a 3-bet calling range from the Big Blind very hard, likely including hands like AQ, AJ, AT, and various Broadway combinations. Minni, in the Big Blind, called my 900 pre-flop raise. Given I have top pair but a weak kicker on a board where many of the opponent's holdings could beat me (like two pair or a straight draw), I should check to see how the opponent acts. This allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate based on the action.

Flop A♣ J♣ K♠; I hold A♥2♥ — top pair but a very weak kicker. Flash checked to me, which can be either pot-control with a marginal hand/draw or a disguised strong hand, but I block a number of A-combos and am likely ahead of a lot of his 3-bet-calling range (A‑x, Kx, many broadways and club draws). This is a dynamic board with two clubs, so I want to charge and deny equity to club/straight draws while extracting value from worse Aces and Kx/Jx hands. A half‑pot sizing balances value and protection without bloating the pot out of position vs a wide 3‑bettor. If Flash raises small I’ll reevaluate (call to see a turn if sizing is reasonable), but I’ll fold to a polarized shove/very large raise that strongly represents two‑pair or better. Action: value/protect with a ~50% pot c‑bet.

On a flop of Ac-Jc-Ks, I have top pair with an Ace, but my 5 kicker is very weak. This board texture is extremely favorable for Minni's range after calling a 3-bet from the Big Blind, as it includes hands like AQ, AJ, AT, KQ, and KJ, as well as straight draws like QT. When Minni bets 1100 into a 2100 pot, they are showing strength. While I have top pair, I am likely outkicked or facing a very strong hand. However, folding top pair immediately for one bet is too exploitable. I will call to see the turn and evaluate, but I intend to play the rest of the hand cautiously.

On the turn, the 3d is a complete brick that does not change the board texture significantly. I still have top pair with a very weak kicker. Given that Minni called my 3-bet pre-flop and then led out on the flop (Ac Jc Ks), she likely has a strong hand like two pair, a better Ace, or a significant draw like QT. By checking here, I am controlling the size of the pot and giving Minni the opportunity to check back or reveal more about the strength of her hand with another bet. I am prepared to fold to heavy pressure as my kicker makes my hand very vulnerable to any other Ace or Broadway two pair.

Turn: A♣ J♣ K♠ 3♦. I have A♥2♥ — top pair but the absolute worst kicker. Flash was the pre‑flop 3‑bettor and just called my half‑pot flop c‑bet, so his calling range contains a lot of better A‑x (AK, AQ, AJ), two‑pair combos and sets that crush me, plus some Kx/Qx hands I can still beat. There are essentially no meaningful draws to deny on the turn, so another bet mostly bloats the pot OOP and can get me check‑raised off my hand. I want to control the pot and realize equity rather than turn this into a marginal value battle, so I check. Plan: call small turn bets but fold to a large polarized shove; if checked through I’ll go to showdown or take a small river action depending on the runout.

On the river, the board is Ac Jc Ks 3d 9h. I have top pair with a very weak kicker (5). Minni called my pre-flop 3-bet and then bet the flop when I checked, but checked back on the turn. This check on the turn suggests she might be pot-controlling a medium-strength hand like a weaker Ace or a King, or she might have been semi-bluffing with a draw that didn't improve. I do not have enough strength to value bet here, as most hands that call me will have me outkicked or beaten. Checking is the best option to get to showdown cheaply or catch a potential bluff from a missed draw.

Preflop I called the 3‑bet with A♥2♥ for playability. On A♣ J♣ K♠ I bet to deny club/straight equity and got a call, which narrows Flash’s range toward stronger A‑x, Kx/Jx and some draws. The 3♦ turn removed most draw equity so I pot‑controlled by checking; Flash checked back, showing no aggressive value-heavy line. The 9♥ river is a relatively harmless card that doesn’t create new bluffs worth folding out better Aces/two‑pairs/sets. A river bet here would mostly fold out Kx hands I already beat while getting value only from hands that already beat my weak‑kicker Ace. Against a 3‑bettor who called the flop, thin value is unlikely and bluffing is risky, so I check and go to showdown.

I am on the button with K3o. While it's a weak King, in a three-handed game, I have the opportunity to exert pressure and steal the blinds. Minni has been quite active and Claude is extremely short-stacked (2300 chips). I will put in a standard open raise to 300 to try and take the pot down pre-flop or isolate Claude if he chooses to defend his short stack.

Facing a button raise to 300 with 2♠4♦ from the small blind. Pot is 450 and I must call 250 to continue. This is a very weak, highly dominated hand and I’ll be out of position vs the raiser (and possibly the short-stacked big blind). Calling 250 into this pot with virtually no equity is a negative EV play; a 3-bet bluff to isolate is unnecessary risk with such a poor holding. Preserve my 21k stack for more favorable spots — fold.

K2o is too weak to defend the BB, especially out of position against an aggressive button raise. With a short 11BB stack, I should fold marginal hands and wait for premium holdings. This is a straightforward fold despite the 3.25:1 pot odds, as the hand quality doesn't warrant continued investment.

I’m on the button with 8h2h suited — a weak but playable hand that gets great post‑flop position. With only the two blinds to beat I want to open a wide stealing range rather than limp/call and invite multiway action. The table’s common open has been around 300, and that size gives good fold equity against the short‑stacked SB (Claude ~22BB) while not bloating the pot unnecessarily versus the deep BB (Flash). I’ll raise to 300 total to apply pressure. Postflop I’ll c‑bet favorable boards and use position to control pot size; I’ll fold to a large 3‑bet or to an SB shove unless I have a specific read he’s shoving super wide.

J5o is too weak to play from the small blind out of position against Minni's raise. The hand lacks sufficient equity, I'm facing positional disadvantage, and my short stack (2250) requires selective play. Clear fold.

I am in the big blind holding Q2o. Minni has raised from the button to 300, requiring 200 more for me to call into a total pot of 450. Q2o is a very weak hand, even with the positional advantage Minni has as the button. Against a 3x open, I should generally be folding this bottom-tier range unless there is a specific reason to defend. I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

Calling from button with 6-8o. The hand has reasonable playability from position, I'm getting 1.5:1 immediate pot odds, and position advantage post-flop will give me flexibility. With a modest stack, I need to take small positive-EV opportunities rather than fold marginal hands from strong positions.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Nine offsuit. The Button (Claude) has limped into the pot for 100. With a stack of 16,500, calling the additional 50 to see a flop is a standard play, especially since I'm getting 5:1 odds on the call. While J9o is not a premium hand, it has enough connectivity to justify seeing a flop against a limp, even with the aggressive Minni still to act in the Big Blind.

I’m in the big blind with K3s in a three-way limped pot (Flash in SB, Claude on the button). K3s is suited but a very weak king with a poor kicker and its equity is reduced multiway. A minimum raise to 200 is unlikely to fold both opponents and could induce a shove from the short-stacked button or commit me to a bigger pot dominated by better kings. I’ll check and see a cheap flop, and only continue with clear equity (top pair with a decent kicker, strong spade draw, two pair or better); otherwise I’ll control the pot or fold to heavy aggression.

I have an open-ended straight draw (J9 on a QT3 board). In a limped pot with three players, checking is the most standard approach from the small blind to see how the action develops. This allows me to see a turn card for free if it checks around, or to call a reasonably sized bet to realize my equity. Given the board has a flush draw and I am out of position, I don't want to bloat the pot immediately without a made hand.

Check. I hold K♠3♠ on Q♠ T♠ 3♥ — only a small pair but a strong 4-to-a-spade flush (9 spade outs) plus two remaining threes. This is a three-way limped pot so my fold equity is low and leading here will rarely fold out better hands (Qx/Tx/pairs) and may induce a shove from the short-stacked button. I prefer pot control and to realize equity: check to see how opponents act. Plan is to call small-to-medium bets to see the turn because I have ~35% to hit a flush by the river (and ~8% to hit trips, ~43% combined chance to improve), but fold to heavy aggression or a large shove from Claude since my pair is easily dominated. If I improve (flush/two pair/trips) I will proceed aggressively on later streets.

Both Flash and Minni checked on the flop, indicating weakness or uncertainty. My 6-8 offsuit is extremely weak on a Qs-Ts-3h board—I have no made hand, no straight draws, and no meaningful equity. Betting would require strong conviction that I can fold out both opponents, but the risk-to-reward doesn't justify a marginal bluff attempt with a moderate stack. Checking allows me to take a free card to potentially improve (any 4, 5, 7, or 9 gives me a straight draw), gather more information about hand strength, and preserve chips. If the turn improves me or my opponents show weakness, I'll have better information for decisions ahead. This is the most economical approach with a draw-less hand.

On the turn, I have improved to second pair (nines) while maintaining my open-ended straight draw (any King or Eight makes a straight). In a limped pot that was checked through on the flop, my hand has significant equity and some showdown value. I will check here to see how my opponents act. If Minni or Claude bets, I have an easy call to realize my equity. Checking also keeps the pot small and allows me to potentially see a river card for free while I am out of position.

Check. I have K♠3♠ on Q♠ T♠ 3♥ 9♦: a weak pair of treys plus a strong spade draw. On the turn I have 9 spade outs + 3 kings + 2 treys = 14 outs (14/46 ≈ 30.4%) to make at least two pair/trips/flush on the river. Both opponents have been passive (checked flop and turn) in a multiway limped pot, so my fold equity is low — a lead now will rarely fold out better hands and could induce a shove from the short-stacked button (Claude, 2,150) that I'd rather avoid facing with just a marginal pair+draw. I'll control the pot and see a free river: call small-to-medium bets to realize equity, fold to heavy aggression, and if I hit (flush/two pair/trips) I'll get aggressive for value on the river.

Both opponents checked the flop with a fairly coordinated board (Qs-Ts-3h). On the turn, the 9d is a blank that doesn't appear to help anyone significantly. My 6d-8s has minimal equity - only the river 7 completes a straight draw (4 outs). With two opponents to fold and a moderate stack, attempting a bluff here requires too much conviction for insufficient reward. Checking takes a free card, preserves chips, and allows the river to provide additional information about hand strength. If I pair up or hit my straight draw, I'll have a better play. If both check again, I can reassess with complete information. This is the most efficient use of my stack.

On this river, the board is Q-T-3-9-J. I have two pair with Jacks and Nines. However, this is a very 'wet' board where any King (K-Q-J-T-9) or any Eight (Q-J-T-9-8) completes a straight. Given that the pot was limped and checked through on both the flop and the turn, my opponents' ranges are very wide and could easily contain these cards. My two pair is a decent showdown hand in a small pot, but it is not strong enough to bet for value because I will likely only be called by straights or perhaps better two pairs like QJ or QT. I will check to see if I can get to a cheap showdown.

River: Q♠ T♠ 3♥ 9♦ J♥. My hand K♠3♠ makes the straight 9–T–J–Q–K (King‑high). That’s a very strong hand here — the only realistic hands that beat me are A‑x combos (A‑Q/J/T/9 etc.) which would make the Ace‑high straight. There is no five‑card spade flush (only two spades on board). Both opponents have been passive (limp/call preflop, checked flop and turn, checked river), so their ranges are wide and include lots of Qx/TX/Jx/9x and pocket pairs that will call a thin value bet. A given opponent has roughly a 17% chance to hold at least one Ace; the chance at least one of two opponents has an Ace is ~31% — a meaningful risk but not so large as to forgo value. With pot = 300 I want to extract thin value from medium‑strength hands while keeping the bet small relative to stacks. I will bet pot (300). If faced with a large river raise or shove I will immediately re‑evaluate (a big shove from the short stack is heavily weighted to an Ace), but given the action to this point a pot‑sized value bet is the +EV play.

River spot with 6d-8s against Minni's raise. My hand has zero equity - no pair, no flush, no straight possible. Minni's line of checking flop/turn then betting river when the J came suggests she improved on the river or has a marginal value hand. Facing 2:1 pot odds, I would need ~33% equity to break even, but I likely have only 10-15% equity (mostly just bluff-catching weight). With a moderate stack and a clear negative EV situation, folding preserves chips for better opportunities.

The board is extremely coordinated with Q-J-T-9-3. On this runout, any King or Eight makes a straight. Since the pot was limped and checked through on the flop and turn, Minni's range is completely uncapped and very likely to contain many Kx and 8x combinations. Additionally, any Queen (Qx) or Ten (Tx) in her hand would result in a superior two-pair compared to my Jacks and Nines. While Minni is an aggressive player, a pot-sized bet on such a connected board suggests value rather than a bluff. I am beating very little other than absolute air, making this an easy fold.

I am on the button with Qd 2d. While this is a suited hand and the game is 3-handed, Q2s is at the very bottom of an opening range. Claude in the big blind is quite short-stacked with only 2050 chips (20.5 BB), which makes him more likely to shove over an opening raise. Minni in the small blind has been active and aggressive throughout the game. Given the potential for a shove from the short stack or a 3-bet from the chip leader, folding this weak hand is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with 5♦8♦ — a suited, playable hand that plays well in position postflop. Claude is relatively short (~20bb) so he will fold a fair bit but can also shove; I want to take the initiative and apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable if he calls. A standard 3bb open (to 300 total) is the right balance: it maximizes fold equity, preserves positional advantage, and is a commonly accepted sizing at this table. If Claude jams over this, I will fold this speculative hand unless his shove range is shown to be extremely wide. Raising also avoids the passivity of limping. Action: raise to 300.

With 9-10 suited in the big blind facing a heads-up raise from Minni, I have a reasonable calling hand. My stack is relatively short at 2050, making this a push-fold decision point. Looking at Minni's tendencies throughout the game, he raises frequently from the small blind, and 9-10 is a solid hand to play in 2-player scenarios. I'm getting roughly 2.5:1 pot odds (calling 200 into a 500 chip pot), which is favorable for this equity-competitive hand. The call is disciplined and maintains flexibility for further action post-flop rather than folding a decent hand.

Flop is Kh 7h 6d; I opened preflop to 300 and Claude (short, ~1.85k behind) called. Holding 5♦8♦ I have an open‑ended straight draw (4s or 9s = 8 clean outs) — roughly ~32% to improve by the river — plus a remote runner‑runner diamond possibility. Claude’s calling range from the big blind is fairly wide (Kx, medium pairs, broadways, some suited connectors), but on this flop Kx and small/medium pairs are common. I want to keep initiative and apply pressure with a semi‑bluff to fold out overcards and some medium hands while retaining ~32% equity when called. Size to half the pot (300 into 600) balances fold equity and pot control against a short stack: it’s big enough to be a meaningful c‑bet but doesn’t overcommit me if he jams. If Claude shoves his remaining stack I will fold this drawing hand (vs a shove from a short stack his range is weighted toward made pairs/top pair and my ~32% is too thin to call off a large all‑in). If he calls/flat, I can continue with my equity to a 4 or 9 (or pick up additional info on the turn). Decision: semi‑bluff bet half‑pot.

Calling with 9-10 on a K-7-6 board. I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs) giving me approximately 35% equity. The pot offers 3:1 odds, requiring only 25% equity to break even. My equity exceeds the pot odds requirement, making this a mathematically sound call. Minni's aggression throughout the session suggests this could be a continuation bet. I maintain flexibility for turn action while pursuing favorable equity.

I continue to apply pressure. Board Kh‑7h‑6d‑Ac; I have 5♦8♦ — the same open‑ended straight draw I had on the flop (8 outs, ~8/46 ≈ 17% to hit the river). Pot is 1,200 and Claude is short (~1.55k). Claude called my flop c‑bet, so his range contains Kx, medium pairs, some pockets and maybe occasional Ax. Checking surrenders my fold equity and lets him see a free river; leading gives me a chance to fold out Kx and small pairs and to win the pot now. I’ll make a half‑pot turn bet (600) to represent a strong made hand (or an Ace) and price many of his holdings into folding while keeping the bet small enough that, if he jams, I can confidently fold this draw (his shove will be weighted to hands that dominate me). If he calls, I still have ~17% to improve and can re‑evaluate on the river. Action: bet 600.

Folding is the correct mathematical play. With only 17% equity against 3:1 pot odds (requiring 25%), this call has negative expected value. More critically, my short stack (1550 chips) combined with the marginal equity means I cannot afford to chase draws here. If I call and miss, I'll have only 950 chips remaining - a critically weak position. Folding preserves my stack for spots where I have better equity, better position, or more favorable stack dynamics. The disciplined fold maintains my ability to compete in the remaining hands.

I'm on the button with 9♥3♥ — a very weak, but suited, hand. With only the blinds to act behind me and deep stacks, button opens should be wide; the table's standard open has been ~300, so I will raise to take the initiative and earn fold equity. Raise size is small relative to my 22k stack and is the right balance: it pressures the short-stacked SB (who may fold or shove) and isolates the BB when profitable. Plan: if Claude (SB) shoves or Flash (BB) 3-bets to a large size I will fold this hand; if called I'll use position to c-bet on favourable flops and pot-control on dry or hostile boards.

Folding 4-7 offsuit from the Small Blind. This is one of the weakest hands in poker and doesn't warrant a 250 chip investment into a raised pot, especially with a short stack of 1500. Flash (Big Blind) may re-raise, and I'd be in a bad spot with a dominated hand. Preserving chips for premium holdings is the correct approach here.

I am in the Big Blind with K5o. Minni raised to 300 from the Button. Claude folded in the Small Blind. I have 200 more to call into a pot that will be 650. While K5o is not a premium hand, getting over 3-to-1 odds against a Button raise usually warrants a defense in a 3-handed game. However, looking at the previous hands, Minni has been quite aggressive and often follows up with continuation bets. My hand is weak post-flop and out of position. Given my healthy stack of 16,350 and the relatively low blind levels, I could call to see a flop, but K5o often puts me in difficult spots. That said, in a 3-handed game, folding Kx to a single button raise is often too tight. I will call the 200 and see if I can hit top pair or a favorable board.

I have missed the flop completely with my King-high. The board of 9-8-3 is somewhat connected and favors a standard button opening range. Being out of position, the standard play here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. I have no reason to lead out with King-high and no draw.

Flop (9♦ 8♣ 3♣) gives me two pair (9s & 3s) with 9♥3♥. This is a very strong hand vs Flash’s calling range, but it is vulnerable to sets (99/88/33) and the specific 9-8 two-pair combo. Flash checked to me, so I want to both extract value from worse one-pair hands and charge club/straight draws rather than give a free card. A ~1/2 pot lead balances those goals: it gets called by many worse hands and draws while keeping the pot manageable vs the few hands that dominate me. If Flash raises large I’ll give extra credit to sets/98 and proceed cautiously; if he just calls I’ll re-evaluate the turn for further value/protection.

I currently have King-high with a five kicker on a 9-8-3 board. I missed the flop completely and have no significant draws. Minni has been playing aggressively from the button. Although I'm getting decent pot odds to call (325 to win a total pot of 1300), I'm out of position and my hand is highly unlikely to improve to the best hand or survive further betting on the turn and river. King-high with a low kicker is too weak to continue with here. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

9-5 offsuit is a weak hand from the button. While I have a short stack at 1500 chips, I should not waste chips on marginal holdings. With a 100 chip call needed and both blinds holding significant chip stacks (Flash: 16100, Minni: 22250), this is not the spot to take a stand. I need to preserve my remaining chips for premium hands where I can play stronger poker. Folding weak hands while short-stacked is the correct strategy to wait for better opportunities.

In the Small Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit, I have a standard complete/call against a single opponent (Minni) who is already in the Big Blind, as the Button has folded. Given my massive stack advantage over Claude (who is not in this hand) and being heads-up against Minni, calling the extra 50 is a mathematically correct and low-risk play to see a flop. I will look to play conservatively out of position but capitalize if I hit an Ace or a favorable board.

Heads-up in the big blind vs a limp from the small blind. 9♦6♥ is marginal but heads-up ranges are wide and I want to seize initiative and apply pressure rather than allow a free flop. I’ll raise to 300 total (adding 200) — a standard isolation sizing that generates fold equity vs a limp, is in line with table norms, and keeps the pot manageable OOP if called. If Flash folds I take the pot; if called I’ll c-bet favorable flops and pot-control on dry/uncertain boards; I’ll fold to a substantial 3-bet with this holding.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit. The Button folded, I completed the Small Blind for 100 total, and Minni raised to 300 from the Big Blind. This is a standard heads-up scenario. Ace-high has some value, but Ace-Trey offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position against a raise. However, since I only need to call 200 more to see a flop in a 400 pot (giving me 2:1 odds), and I am deep-stacked, calling is acceptable to see if I can pair my Ace or find a favorable flop. I will play cautiously post-flop.

I have flopped two pair (Aces and Treys) on a board with an Ace and a King. Given that Minni was the pre-flop aggressor, she likely has a strong range that includes many Aces and Kings. I will check to her to allow her to make a continuation bet, which will help me extract value from her big pairs or potential draws (like spades).

Flop: A♠ 3♦ K♠. I hold 9♦6♥ — no pair and effectively no realistic draw. I was the preflop aggressor but this texture hits a caller’s range very hard (lots of Ax, Kx, pairs). C-betting here with air has poor EV and is likely to get called or raised by hands that dominate me. I’ll check for pot control and to avoid bloating the pot OOP. Plan: if Flash bets very small (≈≤1/3 pot ≈200) I may occasionally float to take it away later; if he fires ~1/2 pot (≈300) or larger I’ll fold and preserve chips.

On the turn, the Queen of Diamonds is a significant card that completes a straight draw (Jack-Ten) and improves hands like Ace-Queen or King-Queen, which are well within Minni's pre-flop raising range. Since Minni checked back on the flop, she might be holding a medium-strength hand or a draw. By checking again, I am exercising pot control and allowing her to bet with a wider range of hands, including bluffs or semi-bluffs, while also protecting myself against hands that may have overtaken my two pair.

I have 9♦6♥ on A♠ 3♦ K♠ Q♦ — no pair and no real draw. I controlled the pot by checking the flop and this turn Q further helps my opponent’s calling range (lots of A/K/Qx and pocket pairs). Betting OOP here with air has very poor EV: many hands in Flash’s calling range will simply call or check‑raise, and I don’t want to bloat the pot with essentially zero showdown value. I’ll check to keep the pot small, preserve chips, and retain the option to float a small river bet or bluff on a favorable river card if he shows weakness; if Flash bets the turn I’d fold to medium/large bets and only consider calling very small probes.

The board has become extremely coordinated with the Jack of Hearts on the river, completing a straight for any hand containing a Ten. My two pair (Aces and Treys) is now quite vulnerable, as I am beaten by any Ten, as well as any higher two-pair combinations like A-K, A-Q, A-J, K-Q, K-J, or Q-J, all of which are in Minni's range. Since Minni checked back on the flop and turn, she may have a marginal hand or a draw that has now improved. Betting here is unlikely to get called by worse hands and only serves to build a pot I might lose. I will check and hope to reach a cheap showdown or evaluate based on her bet size.

Board A♠ 3♦ K♠ Q♦ J♥; I hold 9♦6♥ — no pair, no meaningful draw. Flash limp‑called preflop and then checked flop, turn and river. That line is consistent with a wide but mostly medium/strong calling range (Ax, Kx, some Qx, medium pairs and missed broadway draws). A bluff here would need to credibly represent a Ten to make Broadway, but many hands in his checking range will simply call a reasonable bluff size (Ax, Kx, Qx) and my fold equity is limited. The pot is only 600 and I have a deep stack, so risking chips with zero showdown value is unnecessary. I check to keep the pot small and take it to showdown — fold to a river bet of meaningful size, consider calling only very small probes based on pot odds.

I have King-Queen offsuit on the button, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. My stack is healthy at 16,450. Claude in the Big Blind is very short-stacked with only 1,400 (14 big blinds). I want to put pressure on the blinds and potentially isolate against the short stack or take the pot down immediately. I will raise to 300.

I'm in the small blind with A♣Q♥ facing a button open to 300. This is a standard 3-bet spot — AQo is a strong, high-equity hand that plays well after a 3-bet and against a wide button opening range. Claude (BB) is very short (~14bb) and likely to shove or fold; a 3-bet sized to ~3x the open (900 total) gives me good fold equity and either isolates the opener or forces the short stack to commit (I’m happy to call a shove). Calling (300 total) keeps me OOP in a multiway pot against an aggressive opener and is less desirable. I’ll raise to 900 to take the initiative, narrow ranges, and keep control of the hand; if Flash 4-bets big I’ll reassess, but with these stacks AQo is worth applying pressure now.

Fold Qd6c. This is a weak hand with minimal equity against Minni's 3-bet, which indicates strength. With only 1400 chips and out of position in a multi-way pot, I should preserve chips for premium hands in better spots. Q6o is not worth the 800 chip investment facing this aggression.

I opened for 300 on the button with KQo, and Minni 3-bet to 900 from the small blind. In a 3-handed game, KQo is a very strong hand, but it can be dominated by a tight 3-betting range (AK, AQ, JJ+). However, given the positional dynamics and the need to defend against aggression, folding is too conservative. 4-betting might unnecessarily bloat the pot against a chip leader. I will call the 600 additional chips to see the flop in position and use my positional advantage to navigate the hand.

I have top pair with a very good kicker (A♣Q♥) and I was the preflop aggressor (3-bet), so my range is ahead on this A‑high flop. Flash called my 3-bet from the button, so his calling range contains many worse A‑x, some broadways and medium pairs (99/22) — most of which will call a value bet. Betting ~1/2 pot charges worse aces and medium pairs, denies free cards to backdoor combos, and keeps the pot controlled against stronger holdings. Pot is 1,900, so I’ll bet 1,000 to extract value while keeping weaker hands and floats in; I’ll reassess if he check‑raises (respond to sizing), but a standard half‑pot c‑bet is the best line here.

On a board of A-9-2, the Ace is a very strong card for the 3-bettor's range (Minni). My hand, KQo, has completely missed the flop and possesses no significant draws. Minni's lead of 1,000 into 1,900 is a standard continuation bet that represents strength. Given that I am drawing nearly dead against any Ace or pair in Minni's range and have no equity to continue, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for more favorable situations.

I’m on the button with J♦9♣ in a 3‑way pot against the blinds. This is a clear spot to open‑raise: I have position, decent post‑flop playability, and both opponents (Claude is short with 1,350; Flash is deep and capable but can be pressured). I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the BB) to take the initiative and maximize fold equity — small enough to keep the pot manageable if Flash defends, large enough to put Claude to a decision and isolate when profitable. Postflop I’ll c‑bet on favorable boards, play straightforwardly when checked to, and fold to heavy, committed aggression if I miss and the action gets large.

Folding 7-2 offsuit from the small blind. This is the worst starting hand in poker with no redeeming qualities - unconnected, unsuited, both weak cards. Despite having a relatively short stack (1350), this hand has negative expected value when facing Minni's raise from the button, especially out of position. Even in a short-stack situation, the fundamentals of poker dictate we must fold hands with such poor equity. I should preserve my chips for better opportunities rather than put them in with the worst hand in Texas Hold'em.

I am in the big blind with Jc 8d. Minni has raised to 300 from the button, which is a standard open. Claude has folded, leaving it between Minni and me. Minni is the chip leader and has been active. Although J8o is not a premium hand, getting nearly 2.25:1 odds (calling 200 into a 450 pot) makes it a borderline defend in the big blind. However, against a strong, aggressive player like Minni, playing out of position with a marginal hand can be difficult. Given my stack size is healthy and there are many hands left, I will choose to fold this weak offsuit hand to wait for better spots rather than bloating a pot out of position.

T2o is one of the weakest possible hands in poker - unpaired, unconnected, unsuited. While I have positional advantage on the button and my stack is short (1,350 chips), folding T2o is the correct play. I'm in push-fold territory with a short stack, but that means I should only commit chips with hands that have significant fold equity or strength. T2o has neither - even with two overcards, it's dominated by most reasonable holdings. If I call the 100, I'll likely face continued aggression postflop from either blind and be forced to fold anyway, wasting chips. Better to preserve my stack for a more favorable spot where I can either shove with a quality hand or find a better bluffing opportunity. Looking at recent hands, both Flash and Minni are playing aggressively, so postflop play with this weak hand would be very difficult.

I am in the Small Blind with J5s. Claude has folded. J5s is a marginal hand, but in a blind-vs-blind situation, it is often strong enough to open-raise for a steal. Minni (Big Blind) has been playing very aggressively, so I'll raise to 300 to take the initiative. If she 3-bets, I can easily fold this speculative hand without losing much.

Fold. I’m out of position (BB) against a small‑blind open to 300 and my hand 9h2h has almost no playability — a very weak suited rag with poor equity and little chance to realize value OOP on deep stacks. Calling 200 into a 400 pot requires ~33% equity to break even; against a realistic SB opening range I’m well below that and will face difficult post‑flop decisions while out of position. A 3‑bet bluff to 500 is possible in spots, but with no meaningful blockers and given Flash’s aggressive open I’d be risking chips with a very thin equity edge. Fold and wait for a better spot to defend or apply pressure.

I am on the button with T9o. Claude in the Big Blind is extremely short-stacked with only 1250 chips (12.5 big blinds). Minni is the chip leader. T9o is a strong enough hand to open from the button in a three-handed game, especially with a short stack in the blinds that I can potentially isolate or put under pressure. I will raise to the standard 3x (300) to take control of the pot and put the decision on the blinds.

J♥4♦ is a very weak offsuit hand and is often dominated by a typical button open. I’ll be out of position for the entire postflop, and calling 250 to play OOP (with Claude behind who only has 1,250 and is likely to jam) creates a high-risk, low-reward spot. A light 3-bet to 500 is possible as a bluff but is too thin with this holding and would unnecessarily expose my deep stack to 4-bets or caller shoves. Preserving stack and waiting for a better spot is the +EV line here.

I'm facing a button raise of 300 with 7-2o from the Big Blind. While my stack is short at 1,250 chips, 7-2 offsuit is the worst starting hand in poker and I should not be trying to defend my blind with it. I have approximately 2.25:1 pot odds (450 to call 200), but 7-2o has insufficient equity (~20-25%) against Flash's likely button-raising range to justify a call. Even with a short stack, there's no value in playing a dominated hand. I should fold and wait for a better opportunity to contest a pot or go all-in with a stronger hand.

I have 7♥4♠ on the button — a very weak hand, but in a 3‑handed pot the button can open wide to steal. Pot is 150, to‑call 100 and min‑raise is 200. I’ll make the minimum raise to 200: low investment to try to pick up the blinds or isolate the short SB (Claude). If Claude shoves or Flash 3‑bets large, I’ll fold — no reason to commit more with 7‑4o. If called, I’ll play postflop in position, proceeding cautiously and using positional advantage to c‑bet or check depending on the board. This balances steal attempts with pot control and avoids playing a marginal hand bloated out of position.

3-4 offsuit is the worst hand in poker with no potential. I'm out of position, critically short-stacked at 1200 chips, and facing a button raise with Flash still to act. This is a mandatory fold to preserve my stack for premium hands. Stack management and hand selection are critical when short-stacked.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a head-up situation against a Button open. Minni has been very active and likely has a wide opening range from the Button. I will raise to 700 to build value and take initiative in the hand, despite being out of position.

I opened wide on the button with 7♥4♠ as a steal attempt, but Flash’s 3‑bet to 700 changes the dynamics. The pot is 950 and I must call 500 more (call ≈ 500 / 1450 ≈ 34.5% of the final pot). 7‑4o has very poor equity vs a typical 3‑bet range and is often dominated; even though I’d retain post‑flop position, that edge doesn’t make up the equity deficit. A 4‑bet bluff to the min (1200) is unnecessarily large and risky against this deep, aggressive opponent. I’ll fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

I'm critically short-stacked with only 1200 chips (12BB). With Q-6 offsuit on the button, this is a marginal hand that doesn't justify calling 100 chips. I'm facing two opponents with large stacks (Flash 15,900 and Minni 22,750) who can apply significant pressure. In shorthanded situations with a short stack, I need to be selective and preserve chips for stronger holdings that can withstand aggression or play well postflop. Q-6o doesn't have sufficient equity to justify entering the pot from this position against these stacks. Folding preserves chips for premium hands where I can play with more confidence.

I am in the Small Blind with 5s 3c, which is a very weak hand. Claude has already folded from the Button. The pot is currently 150 (50 from my small blind and 100 from Minni's big blind), and it costs me 50 to call. However, with 53o, I have very little post-flop equity and would be out of position against Minni, who has a massive stack and has been playing aggressively. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve chips for better opportunities.

I hold King-Queen of spades on the button, which is a premium starting hand. My primary concern is Claude in the big blind, who is very short-stacked with only 1,100 chips (11 BB). I will put in a standard raise to 300 to pressure the blinds. If Claude moves all-in for his remaining stack, I have more than enough equity and pot odds to call. If Minni calls or raises from the small blind, I will play the hand in position with a strong range.

Flash opened to 300 from the button in a 3‑handed pot. I have 7♣6♦ — a speculative, connected but offsuit hand. Calling the extra 250 (300 total) gives good pot odds and allows me to realize equity with deep stacks; 3‑betting out of position with this hand is thin and will often leave me OOP against a stronger range or force the short BB into awkward all‑ins. Plan: call to see the flop, fold to a large preflop 3‑bet, and play cautiously postflop — continue only with strong connections (two pair/sets) or clear straight/combination draws, otherwise keep the pot small and concede to heavy aggression.

T7o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. While I'm getting 3.5:1 odds on the 200 chip call, I'm facing two opponents who have shown strength - Flash raised from the button and Minni called that raise. In a 3-way pot out of position with a weak hand and a relatively short stack (1100 chips), this is not a spot where I should commit chips. The equity of T7o against the likely holdings of my opponents (Flash's raising range + Minni's calling range) is not sufficient to justify the call. Better to fold and preserve chips for a stronger spot.

Board: Jh 4s 2c. I have 7c6d — no pair and no clean one-card draw. With only one club in my hand and one on the board a five-card flush is impossible; the only realistic outs are backdoor/runner-runner straights. Flash opened wide on the button so his range contains lots of Jx, overcards and some pairs, but my equity is small and I’m out of position. I called preflop to realize equity, but postflop I want pot control and flexibility: check and see how the button reacts. Plan: check back to take a free turn if he checks, call small continuation bets for price (roughly <= ~1/3 pot) to see a turn with backdoor outs, and fold to large C-bets/raises — don’t build a big pot with a marginal hand.

On this dry flop of J-4-2 rainbow, I have two overcards (K and Q) and the initiative as the pre-flop aggressor. Minni has checked to me, which is expected. I will place a standard continuation bet of 350 (50% of the pot) to fold out her air and low-equity hands. If she calls, I still have six outs to hit top pair on the turn, plus a backdoor spade draw.

Board: Jh 4s 2c. I hold 7c6d — no pair, only backdoor possibilities. Preflop I called to see flops like this and my plan was to call small continuation bets (~≤1/3 pot) to realize equity and keep pots controllable. Flash’s bet is 350 into a 1050 pot (~1/3 pot) so the price is right: calling 350 gives me roughly 20% break-even equity (350/(1050+350)=~20%). I have ~6 immediate pair outs to the turn plus some runner-runner/backdoor straight/club chances and he’s barreling a wide button range, so this call is justified. I’ll call to see the turn but fold to significant aggression (raise) — I don’t want to build a big pot out of position with a marginal holding.

Preflop I called to see flops and I called his small C‑bet on the J‑4‑2 flop to realize equity. The K♣ on the turn is a brick for my hand: I hold 7♣6♦ — no pair and no realistic draw (with only one club in my hand a flush is impossible this river). I have 6 clean pair outs to hit a pair on the river (6/46 ≈ 13%). The button’s range (wide opener + c‑bet) contains lots of Kx, Jx and overcards that the King improves — this card polarizes his continuing range toward better hands. I’m out of position and don’t want to build a big pot with ~13% equity. Check for pot control and to give myself a chance to fold to real aggression. Plan: check and re‑evaluate the river — call very small turn probes only if they price me in cheaply (rough threshold ≈ ≤250 chips given the pot), otherwise fold to sizable turn bets/raises.

I have improved to top pair with a strong kicker (Kings with a Queen) on the turn. Minni has checked twice, which often indicates a medium-strength hand like a Jack or a pocket pair that is willing to call down. I will bet 800 into the 1,400 pot to extract value from these hands and protect against any potential straight draws like Q-T or T-9.

I have 7♣6♦ on J♥-4♠-2♣-K♣ — no pair, no realistic draw (flush impossible, only river pair/runner possibilities). Flash barreled flop small then continued with a substantial 800 turn raise into a 2200 pot. If I call 800 the pot becomes 3000 so I need ~800/3000 ≈ 26.7% equity to break even. I have 8 clean outs to a pair (≈8/46 ≈ 17.4%), well below the required equity, and the turn raise polarizes Flash’s range toward Kx/Jx/two‑pair/sets (and some bluffs). My prior plan was to fold to significant turn aggression (>≈250). Raising would be a thin bluff out of position. This is a clear fold — preserve my stack for better spots.

9♠7♣ on the button in a 3‑way pot is marginal but playable — position is a big plus and opener frequency should be wide. Limping hands this table invites isolation or raises from the big blind; I want to take the initiative and apply pressure to the short small blind (Claude, 1,050) while still using a standard sizing vs the deep aggressive big blind (Flash). Raise rather than limp to generate fold equity and isolate the SB. I’ll open to 300 (≈3×BB): big enough to be standard and to discourage too many callers, small enough to keep pots manageable vs Flash. Plan: if Flash makes a large 3‑bet I’ll fold this hand; if Claude shoves his short stack I’ll call (pot odds + wide shove range); if we get called I’ll play postflop with position, c‑betting appropriate textures and controlling pot size.

Fold 8h-Th from SB. With only 1050 chips, I cannot afford to call 250 with a marginal hand against an aggressive button raiser. This hand lacks the strength needed to play profitably out of position. I should wait for premium holdings to maximize my chip equity with a short stack. Preserving chips for better spots is crucial.

I am in the big blind holding 5c 4h. Minni has raised to 300 from the button, and Claude has folded. To call, I need to put in another 200 chips into a pot that will be 650. This gives me roughly 3.25:1 odds. While the odds are decent, 5-4 offsuit is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position, especially against Minni who has been active and has a large stack. Given my stack size and the strength of my hand, it is more profitable to fold and wait for better opportunities.

I'm on the button with K9d (King-Nine of diamonds) - a suited connector with decent potential. My stack is critically low at 1050 chips, so I need to accumulate chips to stay viable. Flash (SB) and Minni (BB) both have large stacks (16.5k and 22.3k respectively).

Key considerations:
- K9 suited has good potential: two-flush draw, king-high, and potential straight outs
- I'm in optimal position on the button with late action
- At this stack depth (1050), I can't be too conservative and fold marginal hands indefinitely
- The cost to see the flop is only 100 chips (9.5% of my stack)
- If I hit the flop reasonably well, I can leverage my positional advantage post-flop

With position and a decent hand, I should call and look to play well post-flop where my button position gives me significant advantage. This is a spot where position equity is valuable and I can make good decisions with information.

I am in the small blind with 4-6 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. While the odds to call are 5:1 after Claude's limp, the hand has poor playability post-flop, especially out of position. Additionally, Claude is short-stacked (less than 10 BBs) and Minni is an aggressive chip leader; both factors increase the risk of being squeezed or forced into a difficult spot. Folding is the most disciplined and safe play here.

I’m in the big blind with 3♦2♣ facing a single limp from Claude (button) who only called 100 and is short-stacked (~950). The pot is 250 and my to-call is 0. 3-2 offsuit is a very weak holding and vulnerable to domination; there’s no reason to bloat the pot preflop against a wide calling range or to give a short-stacked opponent an easy shove spot when I have poor equity. A small raise (to 200) is low EV here — it either gets called by worse and I play a marginal spot out of position, or it induces a shove where I’m often behind. Best play is to check and see a cheap flop, then proceed based on the texture and Claude’s action (fold to strong commitment unless I hit).

I checked preflop and missed the flop entirely (3♦2♣ on 8♦ 8♣ 7♥). This board is paired and I have no pair, no straight draw and no realistic flush possibility — essentially zero equity and no fold equity against a competent opponent. Claude is short (~950) and likely to either check/call wide or shove when faced with resistance, so betting/bluffing multiplies a marginal spot into a difficult all‑in decision where I’m usually behind. Best line is pot control: check and see what Claude does. If he checks back I can reevaluate on the turn; if he bets or shoves I’ll fold this junk (won’t call large bets with no showdown value).

With K9d on 8d-8c-7h, I have top card, 4-flush draw, and straight draw - significant equity against Minni's likely weak hand. My critical stack situation (950 chips) demands I leverage this equity aggressively. Minni's flop check indicates weakness. A 150-chip bet builds the pot, takes down some equity immediately, and sets me up well on later streets if called. This sizing is measured but assertive given my short stack necessity.

I checked preflop and intentionally kept the pot small with 3♦2♣ because this is a junk holding. On the paired flop 8♦ 8♣ 7♥ I have no pair, no straight draw and essentially no realistic equity (only backdoor pair-outs). The pot is 400 and Claude’s bet is 150, so calling 150 to win ~550 (~27% break-even equity). Versus Claude’s limp/c-bet range (wide but containing lots of pairs and overcards), my actual equity is well below that threshold. Claude is short-stacked (~800) which makes a raise risky — it either gets called by worse and puts me in a marginal out-of-position spot, or it induces a shove where I’m usually crushed. Given the poor hand equity, lack of fold equity, and the pot odds, the correct play is to fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 9c 3c. Claude in the Big Blind is extremely short-stacked with only 1,100 chips (11 BB). While my hand is suited, 9-high with a 3 kicker is too weak to open-raise into a short stack who is likely looking for any reasonable hand to shove with. If I raise and Claude shoves, I would be forced to fold, wasting chips. Folding is the most disciplined play here to wait for a better hand to pressure the short stack or defend against Minni.

Heads-up after the button folded. I have pocket deuces (2h2d) in the small blind with only the short-stacked big blind (Claude, 1100) to act behind. This is a clear spot to take the initiative and isolate with a standard open — pocket pair has good equity heads-up and will play well postflop. I’ll raise to 300 total (3x the big blind) to build the pot, fold out many hands that dominate a simple call, and put pressure on Claude’s short stack. If he shoves I can call comfortably given my large stack and reasonable equity vs a wide shove range; if he just calls I have position and a playable pair. Raise to 300.

AJ is a premium starting hand (top 10-15), and I'm facing a raise from the small blind where Minni has shown moderate aggression throughout the tournament. My stack is relatively short at 1100 chips, but this makes AJ even more valuable as a premium hand to build the pot with. Rather than flat calling and giving Minni positional advantage, I should apply pressure with a strong reraise. Raising to 700 total (committing 600 chips) allows me to either win the pot immediately or get it in with premium equity if Minni continues. This is a natural spot to play aggressively with premium holdings when short-stacked, rather than accept marginal pot odds from a protected position.

I opened to 300 from the small blind with pocket deuces and Claude (big blind) 3‑bet to 700, leaving only ~500 behind. The pot is 1,000 and I need to call 400. With such a short effective stack set‑mining is not a profitable plan — calling to the flop gives him too many cheap ways to commit and offers poor implied odds. I want to either take the pot now or sharply reduce his postflop options. A small 4‑bet to the minimum (1100 total) is a tiny commitment for my 22k but puts real pressure on his short stack: if he folds I scoop the 1,000 now, if he calls he will be nearly crippled (only ~100 left) and play becomes simple. Folding is too tight and a flat call keeps too much initiative with him. Therefore I 4‑bet to isolate and seize the initiative.

AJs is a premium hand and I'm ultra-short stacked. With only 500 chips total, I need 400 more to call Minni's 1100 bet. Folding would leave me crippled with ~100 chips and effectively end my tournament. AJs has solid equity against Minni's 3-bet range, and the pot odds (2500:400 or roughly 6:1) justify going to battle here. This is a spot where premium holdings must be played from a short stack, especially when the alternative is folding and becoming a micro-stack with no fold equity.

I 4‑bet preflop and Claude called, but he’s left with only 100 — he’s essentially pot‑committed. The flop is a dry 4♦‑9♠‑8♣ and I hold 2♥2♦ (an underpair). With such a short effective stack the highest EV play is to bet a small amount to either take the pot now or induce his remaining chips. Betting the minimum (100) forces Claude to commit his last 100 on many holdings (or fold weaker hands) while risking very little from my huge stack. I’m willing to call his all‑in — my 22 has reasonable equity vs his call range of pairs, connectors and broadways. Checking lets him see a free card or shove cheaply; a tiny c‑bet extracts max value and simplifies the decision. Bet 100 to get his last chips or scoop the pot immediately.

I'm essentially all-in with my last 100 chips. AJs is a premium hand with strong equity in this spot - I have two overcards (A and J), a flush draw (9 spade outs), and potential straight possibilities against Minni's likely range. The pot odds are exceptional at 23:1 (2300 to call 100), and folding would leave me crippled with minimal chips and no path forward. At extreme short stack, premium holdings must be played from the big blind. AJs has approximately 30-40% equity against Minni's 3-bet calling range, which is sufficient to justify this all-in commitment. I built this pot aggressively pre-flop and must see it through.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) I should be opening very wide to seize initiative — position postflop is a big advantage. 6s Td is marginal but fine to raise in heads-up; I want fold equity and to avoid being put to tough decisions out of position. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the BB) — a standard sizing that pressures the big blind while leaving room to fold to a large 3-bet. If called I’ll play postflop in position (c-bet dry boards, check/call or pot-control on dangerous textures). If Flash 3-bets big I’ll usually fold this hand; I’ll only continue vs a small squeeze or bizarre sizing.

In a heads-up situation, 10-9 offsuit is a solid defending hand in the Big Blind. Minni's 3x open is standard, and I am getting 2:1 odds to call. Folding is too tight, and raising is unnecessary given the hand's playability and my position. I will call to see the flop.

On a paired board of 6-8-8, I have two overcards with Ten-Nine. Being out of position as the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. My hand has some showdown value against her air and can improve on many turns. I will check and evaluate based on Minni's bet sizing.

Flop is 6c 8s 8c and I hold 6s Td — second pair with a weak kicker on a paired, two‑club board. That texture is dangerous (possible trips with an 8, overpairs and 4‑card club draws) and Flash’s check keeps a wide range. In position I want pot control with this marginal hand: checking lets me evaluate the turn, realize equity, and avoid bloating the pot where I’m often behind. Plan: check-call reasonable turn bets, fold to large aggression or check‑raises, and take a free card to showdown if Flash checks back.

On the turn 4h after the flop went check-check, the board texture remains relatively static. My 10-9 offsuit has two overcards but very little absolute value. Minni's check on the flop indicates she likely doesn't have an 8 or a strong pocket pair, but she could have a 6 or Ace-high. By checking again, I am playing a pot-control line, hoping to either see a free river and potentially improve to a pair or induce a bluff from her weaker holdings which I can evaluate based on sizing.

I have two pair (8s and 6s) on 6c 8s 8c 4h with 6s Td — a strong heads‑up holding but vulnerable to any 8 (trips/quads) or a rare pocket 6 (full house). Flash has checked flop and checked the turn, showing significant weakness or a very wide missed range. In heads‑up play I should be extracting thin value and denying a free river that could give trips/full houses. I’ll make a value bet about half‑pot to get called by worse hands (Ax/Kx/weak pairs, some 6x, draws) while still keeping the pot manageable. If Flash check‑raises large I’ll strongly consider folding (line is polarized). If called I’ll re‑evaluate the river action.

I am holding Ten-Nine offsuit on a board of 6-8-8-4. After the flop went check-check, Minni has led out on the turn for a half-pot bet. My hand currently has no made value and no real draw. While I have two overcards, they are not guaranteed to be 'clean' outs, as Minni could easily have me dominated with hands like A-10, K-10, or pocket pairs. Calling out of position with only Ten-high in hopes of hitting on the river or bluffing later is generally a losing strategy. Given the tournament context and the need to preserve my stack, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

In a heads-up situation, King-Ten offsuit is a strong hand that should be played aggressively from the button. Raising to 300 is a standard sizing to build the pot and exert positional pressure on the big blind, as this hand is well ahead of the average range.

Heads-up vs Flash’s SB open to 300 (pot 400, I must call 200). I hold J♦T♦ — a strong, highly playable heads-up hand with good equity and postflop potential. I 3-bet to 900 total (≈3x the open) to apply pressure and isolate: this sizing is big enough to fold out weaker A/Kx and small pairs while keeping calls from worse broadways and suited connectors that I dominate or have good equity against. If Flash folds I take the pot; if he calls I’m happy to play a deeper pot with a hand that hits many flops and can continue with a c-bet on favorable textures. If he 4-bets big I’ll reassess (likely fold to an extreme 4-bet jam given relative hand strength), but against his typical tendencies a 3-bet here maximizes value and fold equity.

King-Ten offsuit is a strong hand heads-up and is certainly worth calling a 3-bet in position. While 4-betting is an option to exert pressure, KTo doesn't play particularly well against a 5-bet shove. Calling allows me to use my positional advantage post-flop and see how Minni proceeds. Given the pot odds (calling 600 to win 1200) and the stack depth, folding would be overly cautious.

Flop: 6♠ 3♠ T♠. I hold T♦ J♦ — top pair with a decent kicker but the board is very wet (three spades). Flash called my 3‑bet preflop, so his calling range is wide and contains a lot of spade combos (both flushes and one‑spade flush draws), suited broadways and medium pairs. I want to get value from worse tens, medium pairs and broadways while charging and denying correct odds to spade draws. A c-bet of ~2/3 pot balances value and protection without overcommitting to a possible made flush. Plan: bet 1,200 into the 1,800 pot. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he raises big (polarized), I’ll give him credit for a made flush and fold unless the raise size is small enough to justify a call.

On the flop of Ts 6s 3s, I have top pair with the Ten of Hearts (Th), but the board is incredibly dangerous due to the three spades. My opponent, Minni, has 3-bet pre-flop and then followed up with a significant 1,200 lead (40% of the pot) on this monochromatic board. While I have top pair, I have no spade in my hand, meaning I have zero equity against a flush and no redraws. Minni's 3-betting range includes many big pairs with a spade (like AsAx, KsKx, QsQx), suited connectors in spades, or even a pure bluff with the Ace of spades. Given the high probability that I am either already crushed or facing a hand with massive equity against me, and considering I have no spade myself to improve, calling here just to see a turn is risky. However, in a heads-up match, folding top pair immediately to one bet might be too weak. I will call this one bet to see the turn, but I must be extremely cautious if another spade hits or if the aggression continues on non-spade turns.

Board: 6♠ 3♠ T♠ / 2♥. I have T♦ J♦ — top pair, decent kicker, but a very spade-heavy board. Pot is 4,200 and Flash called my 3‑bet preflop and my 1,200 flop c‑bet, so his continuing range is wide but contains: (a) made flushes (two spades), (b) single‑spade flush draws, (c) Tx and other one‑pair hands, and (d) some medium pairs/overcards. A small to medium turn bet achieves two goals: (1) extracts value from worse tens and medium pairs that will call, and (2) prices out single‑spade river draws. A single‑card flush draw that only has one spade has ~9/46 ≈ 19.6% to hit the river, so he needs to be getting roughly 4:1 pot odds to call — with the pot at 4,200 a bet > ~1,025 will make pure river‑only draws fold. I prefer to protect equity and deny those draws, so I will bet a clear half‑pot. Bet 2,100 chips. If Flash calls I re‑evaluate on the river; if he raises big (polarized/large raise or shove) I’ll give him credit for a made flush and fold (only considering calling a small, non‑committal raise where the math and blocker considerations justify it).

On the turn, the board is 6s 3s Ts 2h. While I have top pair (Tens) with a King kicker, the board texture is extremely coordinated with three spades from the flop. Minni's 3-bet pre-flop followed by a 40% pot bet on the flop and now a 50% pot bet on the turn represents a very strong range, likely including overpairs (JJ, QQ, AA), flushes (AsXs, K sXs), or top pair with a better kicker (AT). Crucially, I do not hold a spade in my hand, meaning I have no equity to improve to a flush on the river and am currently drawing dead to any made flush. Given Minni's consistent aggression across all three streets, my hand is likely a 'bluff catcher' at best, and even many of her bluffs (like the Ace of spades) have significant equity against me. In a tournament setting, preserving my stack is vital, and this is a disciplined fold against a range that likely has me crushed or in a high-variance situation.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening a very wide range. 4♦9♥ is weak but playable with position and plenty of chips, so I’ll apply pressure rather than limp. A small standard open to 200 (total) buys me fold equity against the big blind and keeps the pot manageable if called. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet (hand has poor playability vs 3-bets), otherwise play the flop in position — c-bet favorable textures, check and control on dry or hostile boards.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Eight offsuit. Minni, on the button, has raised to 200. Given that we are now heads-up and I am getting 3:1 odds on a call (100 more into a pot of 300), J8o is a mandatory defend. It has decent high-card value and connectivity in a heads-up range. I will call to see a flop and evaluate.

I am out of position on a very dry board that favors the pre-flop raiser's range significantly. With Jack-high and no real draw or pair potential, there is no reason to lead out with a bet. I will check and evaluate based on my opponent's action, though I am likely folding to any standard-sized bet.

Heads-up on the button I opened a very wide range pre-flop, so on A♠ Q♥ 2♣ I can credibly represent an Ace or broadway. My 9♥4♦ missed, but this is a good bluff spot — Flash checked and a c-bet will pick off a lot of his missed/capped calling range and charge weaker hands. I'll make a standard-sized c-bet of ~50% pot to apply pressure while keeping the pot controllable. If I face a substantial raise I will fold (hand has almost no equity); if called I'll re-evaluate on the turn and usually check to control the pot unless the turn gives a very favorable runout for a second barrel.

I have Jack-high with no pair or draw on a board that heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range (Ace-Queen-Deuce). Since I have no equity to continue and am out of position, I will fold to Minni's continuation bet.

In a heads-up situation, Ace-Ten offsuit (ATo) is a very strong hand. Being on the button gives me a positional advantage for the rest of the hand. I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 to build the pot and take initiative, following my established opening range.

Facing a 3x open to 300 from the button (SB) in heads-up. I have 2♠4♦ — a very weak hand, but the pot odds are attractive (200 more to call into a 400 pot, needing ~33% equity). I won't 3-bet a clear trash hand and folding the big blind here concedes too much initiative. I'll call to see a flop and play postflop: fold to heavy continuation-bets on dry boards, but continue/raise when I pick up a pair or meaningful draw or can exploit later streets with our larger stack.

I flopped a pair of deuces (2♠) on 2♦-7♥-6♥ after calling preflop. Flash opened wide from the button, so his range contains a lot of overcards and many heart draws. Betting ~1/2 pot (300 into 600) does three things: collects value from worse hands (A/K/Q-high, smaller pairs/misclicks), charges and folds some of the heart draw combos, and lets me define his range while keeping the pot manageable with my weak kicker. If he raises big I’ll assume a 7/6/set/strong two-pair line and fold; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and re-evaluate.

On a low, connected board of 2d-7h-6h, Minni has led with a 'donk bet' of 300 into a 600 pot. My hand, Ace-Ten offsuit, remains Ace-high with two overcards to the board. While I don't have any direct draws or a heart for the flush draw, folding to a single bet in a heads-up pot with my holding is too passive, especially with the positional advantage. Calling allows me to see a turn card, evaluate Minni's aggression on the next street, and potentially realize my equity if an Ace or Ten falls, or if she checks the turn.

Check. I hold a very weak pair (2♠4♦) on 2♦-7♥-6♥-9♦. I led the flop to charge draws and get information; Flash called, so his range includes lots of overcards, heart draws and some medium pairs. The 9 on the turn actually helps his calling range (9x and a few straight combos like 8+5 or 8+10) and doesn’t meaningfully hurt his hearts — I’m often behind. Betting now risks building a pot when I’m likely second-best or getting raised by a hand that crushed me. Checking controls the pot, lets me fold cheaply to aggression, and allows me to call smaller turn bets for pot odds. Plan: check here; call a modest turn bet (~<=½ pot) to see the river, but fold to any large turn raise. Re-evaluate on the river and only invest more if I improve or face a clear bluff.

On the turn 9d, the board has become quite connected (5-8, 8-T, and straights are possible). Minni checked after leading on the flop, which often indicates a marginal hand or a draw that has slowed down. My Ace-Ten has some showdown value and a backdoor straight possibility, but it's not strong enough to bet for value. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, take a free card to potentially improve to top pair or a straight, and can evaluate the river based on her next action. Checking in position with Ace-high after the opponent shows weakness is a standard pot-control play.

Board 2♦-7♥-6♥-9♦-3♥. I have 2♠4♦ — bottom pair with a weak kicker. I led the flop and got called, so Flash’s range contains overcards, medium pairs (7x/6x/9x), some two-pair/set lines and a lot of heart combos that were drawing. Both players checked the turn, which looks like pot control or marginal holdings. The 3♥ on the river is ugly for me: it completes any two-heart combo in his hand to a flush and doesn’t improve my hand. Betting here is thin — I’ll get called by everything that beats me and rarely fold out better hands. Per my earlier plan (“don’t invest more unless I improve or face a clear river bluff”), I’ll check and take the showdown. If Flash fires a small blocker bet I’ll re-evaluate (fold to large pressure, consider calling very small bets), but I won’t bluff the river with this holding.

The river card is the 3 of hearts, completing a possible flush and some straight combinations (like 4-5). Minni led on the flop but has checked both the turn and the river. This sequence typically suggests a hand with some showdown value that is afraid of the board getting wetter, or a missed draw. My Ace-Ten has a reasonable amount of showdown value in a heads-up pot against missed straight draws or lower unimproved cards. Betting would likely only get called by hands that beat me (like a pair of 6s or 7s) and wouldn't accomplish much as a bluff. Checking back allows me to see the showdown for free and potentially win with Ace-high.

Heads-up on the button with A♦3♣ — this is a clear open-raise. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50, but I want to take the initiative and get value/fold equity. Min-raise to 200 is available, but I'll raise to 300 (≈3x BB) — a normal heads-up sizing that pressures wide BB calling ranges while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks (my 26.6k vs Flash 13.2k). Plan: if called I use position to c-bet favorable flops (A-high textures for value, controlled lines on coordinated boards). If Flash 3-bets a small/medium size I will consider calling to play in position; if he 3-bets very large (heavy pressure / near-pot) I will let this one go — A3o doesn't want to get committed versus a large 3-bet without clear equity. Overall: raise to seize initiative and exploit BB’s wide heads-up range.

In a heads-up situation, while ranges are wider, Queen-Five offsuit is still a very weak hand to defend against a 3x open raise from a chip leader who has been showing significant aggression in previous hands. Given the stack depths and the fact that I am out of position for the rest of the hand, I will fold and wait for a more favorable spot.

I am on the button with King-Ten offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. Standard play is to raise to gain control of the pot and put pressure on the big blind. I will raise to 300 to build the pot early and potentially win the blinds immediately.

Heads-up vs the button — they opened to 300, a wide heads-up raise. J♥6♠ is marginal but defensible in the big blind; folding is too tight and a 3-bet/raise with this hand is unnecessary and will often run into better ranges. Calling 200 more (300 total committed) gives correct pot odds to see a flop, keeps weaker hands and bluffs in their range, and lets me play postflop with plans to cede the initiative on very unfavorable textures. If I hit a Jack or pick up strong equity I will apply pressure; otherwise control the pot and fold to heavy aggression. Against a large 3-bet preflop I will fold.

I have J♥6♠ on a 4♥‑7♦‑9♠ flop — no pair, only a marginal overcard and weak backdoor possibilities. Flash opened wide preflop but checked the flop; acting first out of position I don’t want to inflate the pot with a thin c‑bet that will get called by any pair or most overcards. With deep stacks and a small pot (600) pot‑control is correct: check and see the turn so I can fold to heavy aggression or continue only if I improve (pair the J) or pick up meaningful equity. Plan: check here, call small probes on the turn if reasonable, fold to strong pressure, and turn aggressive only when I improve or get a clear bluffing spot.

I hold King-Ten offsuit and was the pre-flop aggressor. The flop of 4h-7d-9s is relatively dry and doesn't strongly hit a typical big blind defending range. Since Minni checked to me, I will place a standard continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to take down the pot now or define her hand. I have two overcards and some backdoor straight possibilities if called.

Flash (button) opened wide preflop and now c‑bet small (300 into a 900 pot) on a fairly dry 4♥‑7♦‑9♠ board. I have J♥6♠ — no pair, only weak backdoor possibilities. A raise here is thin and will often run into better hands; folding is too tight given the price. Calling 300 into 900 gives ~25% pot odds. My chance to pair by river (J or 6) is ~24% and runner‑runner heart possibilities add a few percent, so my realized equity is in the mid‑20s — roughly sufficient to call against a wide c‑bet range. Plan: call to realize equity and keep bluffs/overcards in their range, then re‑evaluate on the turn — continue/raise only if I improve or get a clear bluffing opportunity; fold to strong turn/river aggression without improvement.

Check. On 4♥‑7♦‑9♠‑3♠ I have J♥6♠ — no pair and only a one‑card river to improve (three J’s + three 6’s ≈ 6 outs, ~12–13% to pair). Flash opened wide preflop and c‑bet the flop, so their range still contains lots of pairs and overcards that will call a turn bet. I have poor blockers and very little fold equity, so a bluff here is thin and will bloat a pot I’m unlikely to win at showdown. With deep stacks (26.2k vs 12.6k) pot control is the priority: check to realize my equity to the river and re‑evaluate. If I pair the J or 6 on the river I can extract value; if I miss and Flash bets a meaningful size I’ll fold. If Flash checks the river I can consider a small bluff depending on the card and their tendencies. Action: check now.

On this turn card, I still hold King-high with overcards. Minni's check-call on the flop suggests she likely has a piece of this board, such as a 7 or a 9, or perhaps a straight draw. Since the 3s doesn't change much, I will check behind to control the pot size and realize my equity. This allows me to see a free river card and potentially improve to the best hand or win at showdown if her range consists of weaker high cards or missed draws.

I paired my 6 on the river (Jh6s → a weak pair of 6s). Flash is a wide preflop raiser who c-bet the flop and then checked the turn, so his check-turn range contains a lot of missed overcards and marginal pairs (and occasionally slowplayed strong hands). With a real but vulnerable pair my goal is to extract value from worse hands (weaker one-pair combos, Jx, some turn-check bluffs) without bloating the pot against hands that already beat me (9x, 7x, trips). A small value bet ~1/3 pot is the best line: it gets called by many worse hands and folds pure air, while limiting losses vs stronger holdings. Plan: bet 400 into 1200; fold to a substantial raise (he’s likely ahead if he raises big), and if he calls I take it to showdown.

On the river, the board is 4h-7d-9s-3s-6d. I hold King-Ten offsuit (King-high). Minni check-called the flop, checked the turn, and has now led out for 400 into a pot of 1200 (a 1/3 pot bet). This small 'blocking' bet sizing often indicates a weak made hand, such as a small pair (4s, 6s, or 7s) or potentially a reached straight (8-5). While the pot odds are 4:1, meaning I only need to be right 20% of the time, King-high has very little showdown value here. Almost any hand Minni would check-call the flop with has either paired up or is an Ace-high that beats me. Given the board texture and the action, it is unlikely she is bluffing with a hand worse than King-high often enough to make this call profitable. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (I posted the SB). 5♠2♣ is a garbage hand heads-up, but in heads-up play the button should be opening very wide to apply pressure. Folding here surrenders 50 chips unnecessarily; calling to 100 unnecessarily gives good odds to the BB and a chance to play a marginal pot. I’ll raise to 300 (my usual 3x open) to leverage my positional advantage postflop, keep the initiative, and pressure Flash’s shorter stack. If Flash 3-bets big I can fold and preserve chips; if he calls I can c-bet or play positionally. Standard, aggressive heads-up open-sizing is optimal here.

I am in the Big Blind with Qc 4c. Minni raised to 300 from the button. In a heads-up situation, Q4 suited is a hand that is strong enough to defend against a standard 3x open. I am getting 2:1 odds on a call (200 to call into a pot of 400). This hand has decent playability and flush potential, so calling to see a flop is the standard play here.

I have Queen-high with a backdoor flush draw on a board of 6h-5h-Jc. Out of position as the big blind, the standard play is to check to the initial raiser (Minni) and see how she proceeds. My hand is currently weak, but a check allows me to evaluate the strength of her continuation bet if she makes one.

Heads-up on a wet-ish J-6-5 rainbow/paired-hearts flop, I have middle pair (5s) with a weak kicker. Flash checked, so I should protect my pair and extract value from worse overcards and Jx/6x that might check-call. A half-pot c‑bet (~300 into 600) balances value and denial of correct odds to pure heart draws; it also keeps the initiative and allows me to fold to a large check-raise but call smaller pressure to reevaluate on the turn. If called, I'll proceed cautiously on turns that bring hearts or Js; if he raises big I will likely fold, preserving stack. Betting 300 is the optimal mix of value and protection here.

I have completely missed this flop with Queen-high and no real draws. While the 1/3 pot continuation bet from Minni offers decent odds, Q-high out of position with almost no equity is not a hand worth floating. I have no straight draws and only a very weak backdoor flush draw. It is better to fold and conserve my stack for a more favorable board.

I am on the button with J8o. This is a standard opening hand in heads-up play, but I need to be cautious as Minni has a significant chip lead and has been playing aggressively. Folding is too tight, but a raise is better than a call to take control of the pot. I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 total.

Heads-up vs the button (Flash) who opened to 300. I have QTs — a strong, playable hand heads‑up with good equity and useful blockers. I'm out of position either way, so I want to take the initiative preflop: a 3‑bet will (1) pick up the pot when he folds to aggression (he has folded to reraise in prior hands), (2) isolate him and let me play a single opponent, and (3) build a pot with a hand that plays well postflop. I’ll size to ~3x the open (raise to 900 total) — big enough to apply pressure but not overcommit given his 12,000 stack and my 27,600. If he folds I win the 400 pot; if he calls I’m comfortable playing QTs OOP; if he 4‑bets I’ll reevaluate based on sizing. Raising to 900 balances fold equity and postflop playability here.

I opened with Jack-Eight offsuit from the button, a standard play in heads-up. Minni responded with a 3-bet to 900. While I have the advantage of position post-flop, J8o is a weak hand to continue with against a 3-bet, especially given Minni's significant chip lead and aggressive playstyle. Calling would put me in a difficult spot on many board textures. I will fold and wait for a stronger starting hand to confront her aggression.

I raise to 200. Heads-up on the button with 7c6c is a standard open: the hand has good playability and suited-connectivity for postflop play and implied odds against one opponent. Facing only the big blind I want to seize the initiative, apply pressure to a wide defending range, and avoid letting Flash see a cheap flop with unconstructed hands. A 2x BB (200 total) is a sensible, controlled size — big enough to generate fold equity but small enough not to bloat the pot with a marginal holding. Plan: fold or flat vs a large 3-bet depending on sizing; if called, continuation-bet favorable flops (clubs, low connected boards) and use position to realize equity and extract value; on dry high-card flops be prepared to check and control the pot.

I have Q7o in the big blind and Minni has min-raised to 200. I need to call 100 more, giving me 3:1 pot odds. In a heads-up situation, Q7o is strong enough to defend against a wide button opening range at these price points. I will call and see a flop.

I have Queen-high with no diamonds on a monotone board. This is a very weak hand as I have zero flush equity and haven't connected with the board at all. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check and evaluate based on my opponent's action. I cannot justify betting here without any equity or blockers to the flush.

Flop: 3♦ 9♦ 2♦ — a monotone diamond board. I hold 7♣6♣: no diamonds, no pair, and essentially zero real equity (only a remote runner‑runner straight).
