Flash (BB) checked; his preflop calling range will contain a large portion of diamond combinations and he’s shown a tendency to call down, so a continuation bet here would be a pure bluff with poor fold equity and likely to be called or check‑raised by many hands. With deep stacks I prefer pot control and to pick better spots — check to see what Flash does. Plan: fold to a strong raise, call a small probe if sizing is reasonable to realize equity, and only invest further if I improve or can credibly represent a made hand on later streets.

On the turn King of Hearts, I still have no pair and no flush draw on a monotone board. Since the flop checked through, it's possible Minni doesn't have a high diamond, but the King is a card that likely hits her pre-flop opening range better than mine. With zero equity and no showdown value, I will check again and likely fold to any significant bet.

Board now: 3♦ 9♦ 2♦ K♥. I hold 7♣6♣ — no pair, no diamond, effectively no showdown equity. Flash (BB) called preflop and checked both streets so far; his calling range contains many diamonds and he’s shown a tendency to call down. Betting the turn here would be a pure bluff with very poor fold equity and a high chance of being called by diamonds/top pairs. Pot-control and seeing the river is the highest-expected-value line: check and fold to a large bet, call small probes to realize equity, and only barrel the river if I improve or can credibly represent a made hand.

I have Queen-high on a board with three diamonds and a King. The flop and turn both checked through, indicating neither of us likely has a strong hand or a made flush. Queen-high has almost no showdown value, but there is little incentive to bluff into a small pot against a chip leader who has been playing relatively solid. I will check and hope for a free showdown, fully prepared to fold if Minni bets.

Check. I have 7♣6♣ on 3♦-9♦-2♦-K♥-5♣ — no pair, no diamond, essentially zero showdown equity. Flash checked flop and turn and has shown a tendency to call down; his continuing range will include many diamonds and one-pair hands that will rarely fold to a river bluff. The pot is small (400) and a river bluff here has very poor fold equity, so the highest EV line is pot control: check and concede the pot if he bets. If he checks back we lose a small amount but preserve our stack for better spots. Therefore I check and will fold to a meaningful bet.

In the Small Blind (Button) with 3d 9d in a heads-up situation against Minni, I have a semi-connected suited hand. While 9-3 suited is generally weak, it is playable in a heads-up context for a min-raise or a call. Given my current stack of 12,150 compared to Minni's 27,700, I want to maintain some pressure but also keep pots manageable with marginal hands. However, Minni has been quite aggressive lately. Raising 3x (300) is a standard opening move here to take control of the initiative and potentially win the blinds, but a simple call (completing the blind) is also acceptable to see a flop cheaply. Given the history of the match, I will raise to 300 to exert positional pressure.

Heads-up vs Flash (button/SB) who opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with KsTs — a very playable, high-equity hand heads-up (good top-pair potential, spade backdoor, and strong blocker to many A/K combos). Pot is 400 and it’s 200 to call; min re-raise is to 500. With deep stacks (I have ~277 BB, opponent ~119 BB) and a wide button opening range, this is a good spot to 3-bet for value and isolation. I’ll raise to 1000 total (a standard ~3–3.5x 3-bet sizing) to generate fold equity vs weaker aces/broadways and to build a pot when I’m likely ahead. If called I’ll play postflop OOP with c-bets on favorable textures (K/x, spade cards) and pot control on dry boards; if I face a large 4-bet I’ll re-evaluate (KTs is often a fold vs a polarized shove/very large 4-bet).

In this heads-up situation, I opened for 300 with 9d 3d on the button, and Minni has responded with a 3-bet to 1000. While I have position for the rest of the hand, 9-3 suited is a very weak hand to defend against a 3-bet. Minni has shown significant aggression recently, and calling here would likely lead to difficult post-flop spots with a marginal hand that is easily dominated or missed. Given the stack sizes and the need to preserve my chips for better opportunities, folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound choice.

I’m on the button (SB) heads-up with 6♠T♦. In heads-up play I should open very wide to seize initiative and deny the BB free flops. Pot is 150 and it’s 50 to call, so I’ll raise to the minimum allowed open (200 total) to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable with a marginal but playable hand. Versus a call I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops (when I pick up a ten or good draws) and check/control on scary, wet boards. Versus a large 3-bet I’ll fold this hand; I may call small 3-bets occasionally depending on sizing and implied odds. Overall this raise maximizes fold equity and keeps options postflop with deep stacks.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. Minni, on the button, has made a minimum raise to 200. In heads-up play, Ace-high is a strong starting hand against a wide button opening range. Given the pot odds (calling 100 to win a pot of 300) and the strength of my hand, a call is mandatory. While a 3-bet is an option, calling allows me to keep the pot manageable while playing out of position for the rest of the hand.

I have flopped bottom pair (sevens) on a very coordinated board with three hearts and three straight cards (7, 10, Jack). While I have a piece of the board, it is extremely vulnerable to many turns and any further aggression. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check and evaluate Minni's response. Checking allows me to see if she continues her aggression and at what sizing, helping me determine the strength of her range on this specific texture.

Flop is 7♥ T♥ J♥ and I hold T♦6♠ — I have second pair (tens) with a weak kicker and no heart. Heads-up my preflop raise gives me a wide value/bluff-catching range and Flash's cold-call range includes many heart combos (which already have the nut flush), Jx, some tens, and a lot of unpaired overcards. I don't want to bloat the pot and play a big pot when a single heart in his hand beats me, but I also should get value from worse tens, Jx and some loose calls. Plan: make a medium c-bet (~60% pot = 240) to extract value from worse hands and deny free cards to unpaired overcards, while keeping the pot manageable vs possible check-raises/flushes. If he check-raises large I will fold this vulnerable second-pair hand; if he calls I'll re-evaluate on the turn (value-bet blanks, pot-control or fold to danger cards like a heart).

On this highly coordinated board of 7h-Th-Jh, my bottom pair (7c) with Ace-high (Ad) is extremely vulnerable. The board is monotone (all hearts) and contains three cards to a straight. While I have a piece of the board, I have no hearts in my hand, meaning I have no redraw to a flush and am drawing nearly dead against any made flush. Furthermore, Minni's 60% pot-sized c-bet on such a wet texture indicates strength or a high-equity draw. Given my position out of play for the rest of the hand and the high likelihood that almost any turn card (any Heart, Queen, King, Eight, or Nine) will be a scare card or improve her range, I will make a disciplined fold to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with a suited Queen. While Deuce is a weak kicker, in heads-up play, suited Queens are generally strong enough to open for a raise. Minni has been playing aggressively, often 3-betting, but I must maintain a standard opening range from the button to avoid being exploited. I will raise to my standard 300 chips.

Heads-up vs the SB who opened to 300. I hold 5♣7♣ — a suited connector with good equity against a wide opening range. Pot is 400 and it’s only 200 more to call (total commit 300), so the pot odds are attractive to see a flop. I don’t want to 3-bet a marginal hand OOP vs the button — small 3-bets will be called a lot and lead to difficult postflop decisions deep-stacked. Plan: call to realize equity and play the flop based on texture (fold to heavy pressure on dry boards, proceed aggressively when I hit pairs/draws). Commit total 300 (100 already posted + 200 to call).

I flopped middle pair (5s) with 7 kicker on 4♥ 8♦ 5♥. Against the SB’s wide opening range in heads‑up, this is a fairly strong but vulnerable hand (two hearts on board and a 6 gives me a straight). I’m out of position, so I want pot control and to let the opener barrel first — checking preserves the option to call a standard c‑bet and to induce bluffs. Plan: check and call reasonable-sized bets (≈1/3–1/2 pot) to realize equity; if Flash checks back I’ll reevaluate on the turn; if he puts heavy pressure (large sizing or check‑raise line) I’ll tighten up and fold unless I improve. Therefore: check.

On this flop of 4h-8d-5h, I have a Queen-high flush draw and one overcard. As the pre-flop aggressor, I will follow up with a continuation bet. This allows me to win the pot immediately if Minni has missed, or to build a pot for when I potentially hit my flush. Given the wetness of the board, a bet of 300 (half pot) is appropriate to maintain pressure while keeping the pot manageable if I face a check-raise.

Flop: 4♥ 8♦ 5♥. I have 5♣7♣ — middle pair with a decent backdoor straight (6) but no flush. Villain opened wide from the button in heads‑up and has used a small (~1/3 pot) flop bet of 300, which represents a very wide range (overcards, floaters, small pairs, and draws). Out of position I want pot control and to realize equity rather than inflate the pot with a marginal raise. The sizing gives good pot odds to call and see a turn where I can re‑evaluate. Plan: call the 300 to keep worse hands and bluffs in, fold to heavy turn aggression if I don’t improve, and extract value or bluff‑catch on later streets if appropriate.

Heads‑up and out of position with a marginal made hand (5♣7♣ on 4♥‑8♦‑5♥‑J♠). Villain opened wide from the button and fired a small flop bet (wide, many bluffs/overcards), I called to realize equity. The turn J improves a lot of his value combos (Jx, two‑pair) but also gives him a free card to barrel — I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with just middle pair and a weak kicker. Betting here would risk building a pot against hands that already beat me and will fold only the weakest overcards; checking preserves pot control and keeps bluffs in his range. Plan: check and evaluate river — call small/medium bets to defend when appropriate, fold to heavy pressure if I don’t improve, and extract value or bluff‑catch on the river when I hit or have the proper odds.

The turn card is the Jack of Spades, which is largely a blank for my specific hand (Queen-high flush draw). Minni called the flop bet, suggesting a made hand like a pair of 8s or a straight draw. By checking back on the turn, I take a free card to realize my equity and try to hit my flush on the river. This also prevents me from being check-raised off my draw and keeps the pot manageable since I currently only have Queen-high.

I check. I have a marginal one-pair (5s) with a weak kicker and I’m out of position. Villain opened wide pre and fired a small flop c-bet then checked the turn — that line shows a lot of weakness or floating hands. The river A is dangerous because it gives many of his preflop raising Ax combos and some two‑pair/boat combos that now beat me, so a value/steal bet here has poor risk/reward (will be called by A's and better, and only folds the very weakest hands). Pot control and showdown is the right line: check and see showdown (or call a small river bet), fold to heavy pressure if he bets large.

The river is an Ace, which is a significant scare card. However, I missed my flush draw and currently only hold Queen-high. While the Ace is a good card to bluff on, my check on the turn weakened my perceived range, making a river bluff look less credible. Minni has been playing a large stack aggressively and has shown a willingness to call down. Given my stack size relative to hers, I will check back and concede the pot unless my Queen-high happens to beat a lower missed straight draw, rather than risking more chips on a low-equity bluff.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening very wide. 9♥2♠ is garbage face‑up, but vs one big blind it's still often +EV to take the initiative rather than just complete the blind. Pot is 150; calling 50 is cheap but gives Flash a free or cheap flop — raising to the minimum (200 total) applies pressure, uses my position postflop, and wins the blinds a fair amount. I’ll use a small, standard heads‑up open (200). If Flash 3‑bets big I’ll fold this hand; if he calls I’ll play the flop aggressively when it connects and otherwise control the pot with position.

In a heads-up situation, King-four suited is a strong starting hand that is well ahead of a typical button opening range. I am getting excellent pot odds (3:1) to see a flop, and since I have a significant stack of 110 big blinds, I can afford to defend. I will call the additional 100 chips to see the flop, even though I will be playing out of position for the rest of the hand.

I am out of position as the Big Blind in a heads-up pot. The flop is 6h-6c-5h, a paired board that doesn't strongly connect with my King-four of spades. While I have two overcards to the board, I have no made hand and no immediate draw. The standard play in this situation is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react and to keep the pot small with my marginal holding.

Heads‑up I opened a very wide range preflop so I’ve got a lot of bluffs in my range here. I hold 9♥2♠ which has essentially no showdown value on 6♥‑6♣‑5♥ and only a runner‑runner chance to improve. Flash checked the flop, so a continuation bet will fold a large portion of his unpaired overcards and weak holdings while keeping the initiative with position. I’ll c‑bet ~60% pot to maximize fold equity but not overcommit (bet 250 into the 400 pot). Plan: if he folds I take it down; if he calls I’ll play the turn cautiously (check/bluff only with improving or very favorable turns); if he raises I’ll assume value or a strong draw and fold rather than turning this into a big pot with a worthless hand.

On a paired board of 6h-6c-5h, my King-four of spades is currently just King-high with no direct draw and no hearts to cover the flush possibility. While I have two overcards, I am out of position against a deep-stacked opponent who has shown consistent aggression. Calling a C-bet here with the intention of folding to further pressure on the turn is a losing play in the long run, and I do not have enough equity or board coverage to justify a float or a check-raise bluff. I will fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable board.

I am on the button with 5c 3c. While it's a weak speculative hand, in a heads-up situation, I should be opening a wide range of hands to maintain pressure and utilize my positional advantage. Minni has a significant chip lead and has been playing aggressively, so I'll put in a standard 3x raise to 300. This allows me to take the pot pre-flop if she folds or play a pot with position if she calls. If she three-bets, I can easily fold without losing too many chips.

Heads-up vs the Button who min-raised to 300. I hold 8♣6♠ — a marginal, but playable hand heads-up. Pot is 400 and calling 200 (total commit 300) gives acceptable pot odds vs a wide button opening range, and my stack is deep so the call is cheap. 3-betting OOP with 8-6o is a poor risk/reward play (often dominated or out of position), and folding is overly tight given the price. I’ll call to see a flop and play cautiously out of position: look to continue with pairs or strong draws, and fold to heavy aggression on dry boards.

Flop heads‑up I have 8♣6♠ on K♠ 8♦ 9♥ — second pair with a very weak kicker. Flash was the preflop raiser and will have a lot of Kx, 9x and many broadway/overcard hands in his range, so I’m often either behind (to Ks/9x) or only a small favorite vs bluffs. Out of position with a marginal made hand, I want pot control and to avoid bloating the pot where I can get exploited. I’ll check to keep worse hands and bluffs in his range and plan to: check‑call small‑to‑medium continuation bets (≈≤1/2 pot) to realize equity, fold to large polarized bets/raises that represent Kx/sets/straights, and only raise if I have a clear read that he’s heavily bluffing. Checking also lets me see a turn and re‑evaluate if I improve or pick up more equity.

I was the pre-flop aggressor, and the flop of Ks-8d-9h is a favorable texture for my perceived range, as a pre-flop raiser is more likely to hold a King or a high pocket pair than the big blind defender. Since Minni checked to me, I will place a standard continuation bet of 300 (50% of the pot) to exert pressure and attempt to win the hand immediately. My actual hand, 5c 3c, has almost no showdown value and very little equity, so this is a pure bluff. If she calls or raises, I will likely check-fold on the turn unless the board improves my hand significantly.

I have second pair (8♣) with a very weak kicker on Ks‑8♦‑9♥. Flash is the preflop raiser with a wide BTN range that includes many Kx/9x/overcards and a fair number of bluffs. He bet 300 into ~600 (half‑pot); calling 300 to win a pot that would be ~1200 gives me ~25% break‑even equity, which is an excellent price to continue heads‑up with a marginal made hand. Raising OOP with 8‑6 offsuit is a losing line; folding is too nitty given the odds and his wide range. I’ll call to control the pot, realize equity and re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to large turn/river aggression unless I improve to two‑pair/trips or pick up a clear bluffing read.

I have 8♣6♠ on K♠‑8♦‑9♥‑6♥ — that’s two pair (8s and 6s) in a heads‑up spot. Flash was the preflop raiser (wide BTN range), bet the flop and then checked the turn, which often shows pot‑control/top‑pair or weakness. Pot is 1,200 and I’m facing no bet — this is a classic value + protection spot: I want to charge Kx/9x and weaker pairs, deny a free river (K/9/8/6 or a runner 7/10 that can make a straight in rare combos) and get value from hands that will call. I’ll bet ~2/3 pot (800) — big enough to extract value and protect, but small enough to get calls from single pair hands. If he check‑raises a small/medium amount I’ll call and re‑evaluate; a large polarized shove is likely sets or K8/K6 and I’ll fold/reassess given the sizing and pot odds. On the river I’ll adjust (value bet blanches, slow down if the board pairs or a K/9/8/6 lands).

Minni has led out on the turn for 800 into a 1200 pot after I continuation-bet the flop. The 6h turn connects with several drawing hands and potentially improved her range (such as 7-5 for a straight or two-pair combinations). My hand, 5c 3c, has no showdown value and very little equity to improve to the best hand. While I was the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, Minni's 'donk lead' on the turn after calling the flop indicates strength or a high-equity draw. Given my chip stack and her significant lead, there is no merit in calling or attempting a bluff raise here. Folding is the most prudent play.

Heads-up on the button with Tc2c — a weak hand but suited so it has some backdoor/flush potential. In heads-up play I should open a very wide range to seize the initiative and steal the BB. Flash has shown a mixed/loose style in previous hands, so a standard-sized raise will pressure many of his defending hands while keeping my risk reasonable. I’ll raise to a standard open of 300 total: if Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold this marginal hand; if he calls I’ll take the lead postflop and c-bet or realize my flush/straight equity on favorable boards.

In a heads-up situation, Ace-Five offsuit is a strong defending hand in the Big Blind. Minni has been extremely active and aggressive from the Small Blind, frequently raising to 300. I am receiving 3:1 pot odds to make the call. While a 3-bet is a viable aggressive option, calling allows me to see a flop and keep the pot size manageable given Minni's significant chip lead. I will look to hit an Ace or potentially a straight draw, or play cautiously if the board is uncoordinated.

In heads-up play, the Big Blind acts first post-flop. The flop of Kh-2s-3h provides me with Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4). While I have an overcard to the board, leading into the pre-flop raiser (Minni) is generally suboptimal. Minni has been very aggressive throughout the match. I will check to see how she reacts to the King on the board. This allows me to potentially realize my equity cheaply or evaluate her bet sizing if she continues.

Flop: Kh 2s 3h. I hold Tc2c — a pair of deuces with a ten kicker. Heads-up and having opened preflop, I want to keep the initiative. Flash called a wide range preflop and checks the flop often, so a small-to-medium continuation bet both extracts value from missed overcards/A-high hands and charges any backdoor equity, while keeping the pot controllable against stronger hands (Kx, bigger pocket pairs, or a 3). Pot is 600; I’ll make a ~1/2-pot c‑bet (300). Plan: if Flash check-raises big I’ll fold this vulnerable hand; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously — look to control the pot unless I improve or get clear value.

On a flop of Kh-2s-3h, I hold Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (any 4). Minni has bet 300 into a 600 pot, giving me 3:1 odds on a call. Given Minni's high frequency of aggression and wide range in heads-up play, folding Ace-high with a draw is too tight. My Ace provides some showdown value if she is bluffing with air or a lower draw, and the gutshot provides clean outs to a strong hand. I will call the continuation bet and re-evaluate on the turn, looking for an Ace, a 4, or a safe card that allows me to get to showdown cheaply.

In heads-up play, the Big Blind acts first on all post-flop streets. The turn card is a Trey of Diamonds, which pairs the board. This card is generally neutral, though it makes a full house or trips less likely for a standard pre-flop raising range. I currently hold Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (any 4). Given that Minni was the pre-flop aggressor and continued on the flop, the most standard and disciplined play is to check to the aggressor. This allows me to see how she reacts to the paired board and potentially realize my equity for free if she checks behind, or evaluate her bet sizing if she continues her aggression.

Turn (Kh 2s 3h 3d) pairs the board — my Tc2c is only a small pair of deuces and is now very vulnerable. Flash called my flop C-bet and then checked the turn, showing either pot-control with a marginal hand (Kx, a single 2) or weakness. Betting here will often get called by hands that beat me (Kx, any two that plays cautiously, or rare sets) and I have little fold equity to push them off. I want to avoid bloating the pot with a second-best hand, so I’ll check and control the size of the pot; I’ll call a small bet but fold to a large raise.

The river is a Six of Diamonds, completing a board of Kh-2s-3h-3d-6d. My Ace-high has decent showdown value in this heads-up pot, especially after Minni checked back the turn. The turn check often indicates a capped range or a desire to get to showdown cheaply with a marginal hand. By checking, I allow for the possibility of winning the pot if she checks behind with a missed draw or a weaker Ace. Leading out would likely only be called by hands that beat me (Kings, Sixes, or pocket pairs). I will check and evaluate if she decides to bet.

Board is K♣?K of hearts (Kh), 2♠, 3♥, 3♦, 6♦ and I hold Tc2c — just a single deuce (second pair) with a ten kicker. I led the hand (raised pre, c‑bet flop) and Flash called the flop but then checked the turn and river, which indicates either pot‑control with a K or weaker holding, or a missed float — not a strong value line. Betting the river has very little value: most hands that would call a river bet (Kx, 6x, any 3x/trips/full house) beat me, and many worse hands will simply fold, so I have almost no fold equity. I also block only a couple of the opponent’s full‑house/pocket‑deuce combos but not enough to warrant a bluff. With the pot only 1,200 and no bet to face, the prudent line is to check down to showdown and avoid bloating the pot with a likely second‑best hand (I’d call a small river bet but would fold to a large raise).

I am dealt a pocket pair of Fives on the button in a heads-up situation. Pocket fives is a strong hand pre-flop, especially heads-up. With 96.5 big blinds, I should definitely enter the pot. Standard play would be to raise to 3x the big blind (300) to build the pot and take control of the initiative. My opponent, Minni, has been playing aggressively and has a large stack, but a pair of fives is well worth a raise here to see how they react.

Heads-up vs a small‑blind open to 3bb, Flash is opening a very wide range. 4♠6♠ is a suited connector with good post‑flop playability and strong implied odds against a wide SB opening. Calling is cheap here: I only need to put in 200 more (300 total committed) to see a flop and can realize equity OOP or pot‑control. A 3‑bet bluff is possible, but with this low suited connector I prefer to keep the pot manageable and play the flop; folding is too tight. I will call to see the flop.

Flop analysis: I have 4♠6♠ on A♦ T♥ 9♣ — no pair, no immediate straight, and no single‑card flush draw (only a two‑card backdoor spade flush and a tiny double‑runner straight draw). Flash opened very wide to 3bb preflop, so his continuation range contains a lot of A‑x, broadways and T/9 combos that connect well with this board. My showdown equity is essentially single‑digit versus a typical c‑betting range, and I’m out of position.

Plan: Don’t build the pot with essentially air. Checking controls the pot size, keeps weaker holdings and bluffs in his range, and lets me fold to aggression without losing many chips. If Flash checks back I can consider a small delayed bluff or a stab on a safe turn card, but if he fires I’ll fold the vast majority of the time (only calling extremely small probes or hands where a cheap float makes sense).

Action: check to keep the pot small and avoid committing equity I don’t have.

The flop of Ad-Th-9c is quite unfavorable for my pocket fives, as it contains an Ace and two other cards (Ten and Nine) that are well within the calling range of my opponent, Minni. With Minni checking, I have the option to continuation bet, but 55 has very little equity on this board and no real draws. Checking back allows me to control the size of the pot and see a turn card for free, preserving my stack in a situation where I am likely behind or facing high-equity overcards.

I hold 4s6s on A♦ T♥ 9♣ 9♦ — no pair and no realistic draw. I checked the flop to control the pot and Flash checked back, showing passivity; betting OOP here with pure air is a low‑EV play because he'll call many worse hands and only fold the absolute air. The turn pair makes my hand even worse (it helps many hands in his range), so I should keep the pot small and preserve stack depth for later opportunities. Plan: check back, see the river, and fold to meaningful aggression — only consider calling very small probes or bluffing the river on a very safe card.

The board has paired the Nine, and the Ace and Ten are still present. My pocket fives are currently an underpair to most of the board and Minni's likely calling range. Minni has checked both the flop and the turn, which could indicate weakness, but given the texture of the board (Ad-Th-9c-9d), she could easily be holding an Ace or a Ten and looking to check-call or trap. There is little value in betting here as I am unlikely to get better hands to fold or worse hands to call. Checking back allows me to see the river for free and attempt to reach showdown with my small pair, which still has some equity against complete bluffs.

Board A♦ T♥ 9♣ 9♦ 5♥ and I hold 4♠6♠ — no pair, no draw, basically air. Flash opened very wide preflop but then checked flop and turn, a line that can be either a weak showdown hand or a slow‑played value (A‑x, T‑x, 9x, or a pocket pair) — almost all of which beat me. To profitably bluff the river I need meaningful fold equity (≈33%+ against a half‑pot bet), and from his passive line plus his history of calling down in the session I expect his calling frequency to be high. I also have no blockers to A or 9, so my bluff has low chance of success and a call would lose a small amount anyway but a failed bluff creates needless variance. With large effective stacks relative to this small pot, preserving chips for better spots is higher EV. Action: check and fold to any significant river bet (only consider calling a tiny probe if it’s absurdly small).

I have improved to a full house (Fives full of Nines) on the river. The board is Ad-Th-9c-9d-5h. Minni has checked on every street, suggesting she may be holding a weak Ace, a Ten, or perhaps missed a draw. While there are some hands that beat me (like a Nine for a higher full house), her passive play makes those unlikely. I will place a value bet of 450 into the 600 pot, aiming to get called by an Ace or a Ten.

I have 4♠6♠ on A♦ T♥ 9♣ 9♦ 5♥ — absolutely no pair and zero meaningful blockers. Pot was 1050 and Flash bets 450; calling 450 to contest a pot that would be 1950 after my call requires ~23% equity. My hand has virtually no showdown equity versus a reasonable betting range here (A‑x, T‑x, 9x, pocket pairs and many hands he’ll call down with given his history). Flash checked flop and turn (a passive line) then puts out a smallish river probe — that line more often indicates a weak value or a thin value attempt rather than a polarizing bluff, and he’s shown a high calling frequency in this match. Raising as a bluff OOP is high variance and unlikely to fold enough of his range to be +EV. With deep effective stacks and many better spots to invest in, preserving chips is the correct play. I will fold.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with Q♣3♠ — marginal but playable because of position. Limping gives up initiative; folding is too tight in heads-up. I want to take the initiative and try to steal the blind while keeping the pot manageable, so I’ll make a standard open to the minimum raise. Raise to 200 total (2BB) — if Flash folds I scoop the blinds; if he calls I can use position to c-bet favorable flops and control the pot; if he 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate and likely fold this marginal holding.

I have Ace-Seven of Hearts in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 200 (a min-raise). My hand is very strong for heads-up play, especially suited. I want to build a pot and take the initiative, but given the shallow stacks relative to the total chips in play (though I still have 99 BBs), a 3-bet is the standard play here to extract value and potentially end the hand pre-flop. I will raise to 700 to put pressure on Minni.

I opened to 200 on the button with Qc3s and Flash 3‑bet to 700. Calling would cost 500 to win a pot of 1400, so I need ~36% equity to justify a call. Q3o is a weak, often‑dominated hand vs a typical 3‑bet/4‑bet range and has poor playability postflop. 4‑betting as a bluff is possible with deep stacks but is too spewy here given Flash’s sizing and earlier aggression. I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack for better spots.
In heads-up play, Ace-Seven offsuit is a very strong hand from the button. I will raise to 300 to maintain aggression and take control of the pot early.

I have Jc2c — a weak but suited hand. Flash opened from the button to 300; I’m in the big blind and only need to put in 200 more (300 total) to see a flop. With deep stacks and Flash opening very frequently this table, I’d prefer to defend cheaply rather than build a bigger pot with a marginal hand out of position. Calling gives good pot odds (200 into 400, ~3:1) and preserves maneuverability post‑flop: continue on a jack, club draw, or a favorable board and fold to strong aggression otherwise. A bluff 3‑bet here is unnecessary against a wide opener who often calls, so I’ll just call.

I have Jc2c on Ad 9c 4c — a pure drawing hand: 9 club outs to a flush and 3 additional jacks for ~12 total outs. That’s ~34.8% to make a flush by the river and ~45% to pair or better by river. I’m out of position vs Flash (the frequent opener) on an Ace‑high board, so I don’t want to build a big pot with only a draw. Checking controls the pot and lets me call a reasonable c‑bet (I’ll call small/medium bets given my equity and pot odds) or fold to extreme aggression that prices me out. If Flash checks back I get a free card to realize equity. Plan: check and call standard c‑bets; only raise as a polarized play or if I pick up additional equity on the turn.

On this flop of Ad-9c-4c, I have top pair with a 7 kicker. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. Minni has checked to me. I will bet 300 (half pot) to get value from weaker aces, flush draws, and middle pairs like 9x. It also protects my hand against various draws.

Heads‑up on Ad 9c 4c I have Jc2c — the nut flush draw (9 club outs) plus a few additional pair outs (roughly 12 meaningful outs by my earlier read). Flash is a frequent opener and this is a standard c‑bet of 300 into a 900 pot; calling 300 gives me ~25% pot odds while my equity to improve by the river is well above that (~35% to make the flush, ~45% to pair or better by river by my count). I’m out of position, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand by raising; folding would be too tight given the price. Call to realize equity and keep options: if I hit a club or pair on the turn I can start extracting value or raise/shove as appropriate; if I miss I’ll control the pot and fold to heavy turn aggression.

Check. Flash checked to me on the turn and I only have a one‑card draw: Jc2c gives me 9 club outs to the nut flush and 3 jacks for a total of 12 outs (12/46 ≈ 26.1% to improve on the river; flush ≈ 9/46 ≈ 19.6%). The pot is 1,200 and I'm out of position with deep stacks (Minni 29,200 vs Flash 9,600). Betting here is a semi‑bluff but will often be called by Ax, pairs or broadways in Flash's wide opening range, which would bloat a pot OOP with only one card to come. I prefer pot control and to realize my equity for (essentially) a free river — if I hit I can extract value, if I miss I can fold to heavy river aggression. I’ll check and re‑evaluate on the river (call reasonable bets when I have the odds or fold to large pressure; value‑bet when I complete).

On the turn, the board is Ad-9c-4c-6h. I have top pair (Aces) with a 7 kicker. The 6h is a blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture, though it does pick up some gutshot possibilities for hands like 5-7. Minni has checked again. I will continue betting to gain value from a 9, smaller pocket pairs, or flush draws. A bet of 800 into a 1200 pot (2/3 pot) keeps the pressure on and builds the pot for when my hand is likely the best.

I checked the turn with Jc2c to control the pot and realize equity. Facing Flash’s 800 into a 2000 pot I’m getting 800 to win 2800 (≈28.6% break‑even). I have 9 club outs to the nut flush + 3 jacks (12 total) → 12/46 ≈ 26.1% to improve by the river. If you count only the guaranteed clean outs vs most Ax value hands it’s 9/46 ≈ 19.6%. On pure pot‑odds the call is slightly behind, but: 1) I’ve already invested chips and the decision is close; 2) there are reasonable implied odds if I hit the flush (Flash often calls down with top pair/Ax); 3) I’m out of position so a raise would be a risky semi‑bluff that bloats the pot with only one card to come and would often be called by Ax. Plan: call the 800 to realize equity, fold to very large river shove if I miss, and extract value / raise appropriately if I complete the flush or pair. Therefore I will call 800.

I hold Jc2c on Ad 9c 4c 6h 6s. I missed — there isn’t a five‑card club flush (there are only two clubs on board), and I have no pair. Flash has been opening and betting aggressively on flop and turn, so his range contains a lot of Ax, single pair hands (9x/4x), and some stronger made hands; many of those all beat my J‑high. Pot is 2,800 and I am first to act. Betting here would be a pure bluff with very limited fold equity against a player who has shown willingness to barrel; a bet also exposes me to a raise that I cannot profitably continue with. The low‑risk, high‑EV line is to check and fold to a value/large bet — if Flash checks back I lose only a small pot, if he bluffs I can call or raise selectively, and if he bets value I conserve chips. Action: check to keep the pot under control and avoid spewing chips with no showdown value.

On the river, the board is Ad-9c-4c-6h-6s. I have top pair (Aces) with a pair of sixes on the board, using a 7 as my kicker. Minni has called my bets on both the flop and the turn and has now checked to me on the river. While my hand is strong in a heads-up context, the seven kicker is only mediocre. Betting now risks a check-raise from a hand that contains a six or a better Ace. Since the flush draw missed, there are several hands I beat that will simply fold to a bet, while hands that beat me (like A8-AK or a slow-played set) will certainly call or raise. Checking back allows me to reach a showdown and realize my hand's value without further risk.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) — this is an ideal spot to open a wide range. I have T♠3♠: not a monster but suited and playable in position with good postflop playability. With 50 to call into a 150 pot I’d rather take the initiative than give Flash a cheap look; a standard open to 200 (total) applies pressure, denies free flops, and keeps the pot a reasonable size. If Flash calls I’ll use positional advantage to c-bet favorable textures and play cautiously if he shows strength; if he 3-bets big I’ll usually fold this hand. Raising to 200 maximizes fold equity and preserves future options.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit. Minni, who has been aggressive but sometimes folds to 3-bets (as seen in Hand #92), raised to 2bb (200). A8o is a strong hand in heads-up play, well ahead of an average Button opening range. While I could 3-bet to take control of the pot, calling is a solid defensive play that keeps the pot manageable while I have a hand with decent showdown value. I will call the 100 to see the flop.

I have Ace-high on a Queen-high board. As the player out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to see how the opponent reacts to this board. Ace-high still holds significant showdown value in heads-up play, but leading out here would likely only get called by better hands or fold out air that I currently beat.

Heads-up I opened preflop and Flash checked the flop — Q♠ 4♠ 3♦. I hold T♠3♠: a pair of treys with a very strong spade draw (four to a spade flush). This hand has both showdown value and equity, and as the preflop aggressor I want to take the initiative. A half-pot c-bet (~50%) charges unpaired overcards and smaller pairs, denies free spade cards, and builds value when I'm ahead while keeping the pot controllable given my vulnerable pair. If Flash raises big I’ll re-evaluate (often folding to a large raise), but a call allows me to use position on the turn. Bet 200 to apply pressure and collect equity/value.

I have Ace-high (Ad 8c) on a board of Qs-4s-3d. After I checked, Minni bet 200 into a pot of 400. Ace-high has significant showdown value in heads-up play, and I am still ahead of all of Minni's air and draw-heavy bluffs (like spade draws or 56 straight draws). Folding would be too tight given the pot odds (3:1) and my relative hand strength. Calling allows me to keep the pot controlled while giving me the opportunity to either improve on the turn or potentially reach showdown cheaply if the board runs out favorably.

The board has become more coordinated with the addition of the Jack of Diamonds. I still hold Ace-high (Ad 8c), which maintains some showdown value but has not improved. Minni checked back on the turn, which often indicates either a medium-strength hand trying to reach showdown cheaply or a total air hand that has given up. By checking here, I allow Minni to continue with her bluffs if she has air, or I can potentially realize my equity and see a free river card to try and hit an Ace or improve to a winning hand. Leading out into a board that now contains a Queen and a Jack (likely in a Button opening range) is not ideal with just Ace-high.

I hold T♠3♠ on a 3♦ 4♠ Q♠ J♦ board — bottom pair plus a very strong spade draw (four spades visible, ~9 outs to a flush ≈19–20% on the river). Heads‑up I was the preflop aggressor and c‑bet the flop; Flash called and then checked the turn. That line is consistent with a wide calling/checking range (single-pair Q/J, medium pair, turned checks with missed overcards, or spade draws). Betting here is a clear semi‑bluff: I have both showdown value and meaningful equity to improve, plus fold equity to take down the pot right now against many hands (overcards, weak Kx, missed Jx, pure bluffs). A half‑pot bet charges draws and weaker hands, extracts value from worse one‑pair hands that will call, and keeps the pot manageable if I’m raised. If Flash check‑raises large I’ll typically fold this vulnerable pair and draw; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate the river (value bet big if I hit the flush, pot‑control or check back with a blank depending on his tendencies). Bet half the pot (400) to apply pressure and build value while preserving options.

I am currently holding Ace-high (Ad 8c) on a board of Qs-4s-3d-Jd. Minni has followed up her flop continuation bet with a second barrel on the turn after I checked. The Jack of Diamonds is a highly coordinated card that frequently improves a Button's pre-flop raising range, connecting with hands like AJ, KJ, QJ, and JT. While Ace-high has some showdown value in heads-up play, calling two streets without a pair or a strong draw on this board texture is overplaying the hand. Given the potential for another large bet on the river and the likelihood that I am behind a Queen, a Jack, or a pocket pair, I will fold.

In a heads-up situation, 97s is a very strong hand to play from the button/small blind. It has great playability, straight and flush potential, and is well above the average hand. I will raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) to build the pot and take the initiative in the hand.

I’m in the BB with K♦9♥ facing a raise to 300 from the button. I’ve already posted 100 and need to put 200 more (total committed 300) to call. Heads‑up K9o has decent equity versus a wide opening range and plays well postflop; stacks are deep so there’s room to maneuver. I don’t want to 3‑bet out of position and bloat the pot with a marginal hand, so I’ll just call to see a flop, keep the pot manageable, and evaluate on texture/reader action — continue on king/9/backdoor draws, fold to heavy further aggression on dry, unfavorable boards.

I have K♦9♥ on K♠ A♣ 5♦ — top pair but dominated by any Ax. Flash opened wide from the button and c‑bets often, so I want to control the pot and keep his range wide. I’ll check to induce a continuation bet rather than bloating the pot OOP with a lead or polarizing 3‑bet. Plan: check‑call small/medium c‑bets (roughly up to ~pot) to realize equity and re‑evaluate on the turn; fold to very large raises/all‑ins unless I pick up a clearer read or improvement. If he checks back I’ll see showdown and proceed from there.

On a board of Ac-Ks-5d, I have a significant range advantage as the pre-flop raiser. My 9s7s has very little showdown value, but this is a prime opportunity for a continuation bet. The Ace and King are squarely in my perceived range, and Minni's check suggests she likely hasn't connected strongly. A bet of 300 (half the pot) should be sufficient to fold out her non-pairing hands and marginal holdings.

I have K♦9♥ on K♠ A♣ 5♦ — top pair but a weak kicker and vulnerable to any Ax in Button’s wide opening range. Flash c‑bet 300 into a 900 pot (small probe) — exactly the spot I checked to induce. I’ll call 300 to keep the pot controllable, extract value from worse kings/pocket pairs and let bluffs continue. Raising OOP would polarize and bloat the pot against many Ax hands; folding is too weak given reasonable equity and the cheap price. Plan: call 300, re‑evaluate on the turn — call small/medium barrels to reach showdown, fold to large raises/shoves unless I improve or get a clear read.

The turn (Ad) just paired the board and I’ve turned two pair — Aces and Kings (Kd9h). That’s a big improvement vs my flop top‑pair but I’m still vulnerable to several of Flash’s likely hands: any Ax (now trips or better), AK/KK (full houses), and Kx hands with better kickers. Flash opened wide and c‑bet small on the flop, so his range contains both bluffs/weaker pairs and the strong hands that crush me. I want to control the pot size and avoid bloating it OOP into a potential trap. Checking keeps worse hands and bluffs in and lets him reveal his strength. Plan: check here; call small‑to‑medium barrels (~up to about pot size) to get to showdown and fold to large polarized shoves that very likely represent trips/full houses. If he checks back I get a showdown with a very good hand vs his continuing range.

The turn pairs the Ace, which is a card that hits my pre-flop raising range very well. However, Minni called my flop bet on an A-K high board, suggesting she has at least a King or a weak Ace. If she has an Ace, she now has trips and won't fold. If she has a King, the second Ace is scary for her, but she might still call one more bet. With no equity and no real draws, I will check back to control the pot size and potentially see a cheap showdown or find a better bluffing opportunity on the river.

I have K♦9♥ on K♠ A♣ 5♦ A♦ T♦ — that’s two pair (Aces & Kings). It’s a strong hand heads‑up but very vulnerable here: the river T♦ completed diamond draws (any two diamonds in Flash’s hand is now a flush), and several villain combos beat me outright — AK (now a full house), KK (full house), AA (trips/quads), and Kx hands with a better kicker can also beat or chop. Flash opened wide preflop and c‑bet small on the flop, then checked the turn; that line is consistent with a lot of medium strength hands or missed draws, but it’s also exactly the line some players take with traps (and with AK he would have a disguised full house on the river). With the pot only 1,200 and opponent still having ~10,600 behind, betting risks being raised/jammed by the small subset of hands that beat me while only extracting thin value from Kx hands that call. I prefer to keep the pot small and go to showdown — check and see what he shows. If he now bets a small amount I’ll call for value against worse kings and bluffs; I’ll fold to a large polarized shove that very likely represents trips/full houses/flushes.

The river Ten doesn't improve my 9-high, so I have zero showdown value. However, Minni's line of checking the turn and river after calling a bet on an Ace-King-high flop suggests she has a marginal made hand, such as a King or a pocket pair, and is looking for a cheap showdown. Since the board contains two Aces, it is very difficult for her to call a significant bet without an Ace herself. By betting 800 into the 1200 pot, I am representing a strong hand that checked the turn for pot control or to trap, or a straight that just completed. Given her history of folding to pressure in recent hands, this is a prime opportunity to take the pot.

I have two pair (Aces & Kings) with K♦9♥ on K♠ A♣ 5♦ A♦ T♦ — a strong showdown hand but vulnerable to full houses/quads and the completed diamond flush. Flash opened wide pre, c‑bet small on the flop, checked the turn (when the board paired) and now bets 800 into ~1,200: that line fits both value (AK/KK/AA, pocket 5s/10s, or now‑made two‑diamond flushes) and bluffs (missed diamonds/backdoor floats). Calling 800 to win ~2,800 requires ~28.6% equity. Given his wide range and the turn check, I expect enough worse Kx and bluffs in his river‑betting combinations to make a call +EV. Raising would polarize and risk a shove from the small subset of hands that absolutely crush me; folding is too nitty given pot odds and the frequency of bluffs. I’ll call to reach showdown, extract value from worse kings/pairs and catch bluffs, and fold to a large shove if he jams representing the very small range that beats me.

Heads-up on the button (I already have the SB posted), I should be opening very wide. Tc2d is a weak hand but in heads-up play it’s still worth applying pressure from the button to pick up the blinds and use positional advantage. A min-raise to 200 is a standard, low-cost aggression: it buys fold equity against marginal BB holdings, keeps the pot manageable if Flash 3-bets (I can fold), and lets me play postflop with position if he calls. I’ll raise to 200.

I am holding King-Three of Spades in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind, making it 100 more for me to call. K3s is a strong enough hand to defend my blind against a standard button/small blind raise, especially getting 3-to-1 pot odds. My goal is to see a flop and realize my equity or outplay my opponent on later streets. I will call the raise.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Three of Spades on a board of Jack-Nine-Four. I have one overcard and a backdoor spade draw. As the Big Blind in a heads-up pot where I called a pre-flop raise, it is standard to check to the aggressor to see how they react. I do not have enough strength to lead out here, but I have enough equity to potentially continue if the bet is small.

I opened very wide from the button preflop, so my raising range contains a lot of unpaired broadway cards and bluffs. The flop (4h 9c Js) is fairly dry and misses most of Flash’s calling range; he checked, so I should try to take the pot now. With Tc2d I have no pair but reasonable backdoor and showdown equity — this is primarily a fold-equity c-bet. I’ll make a half-pot continuation bet (200) to pressure unpaired hands and charge any backdoor draws while keeping the pot manageable. If he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and fold to meaningful aggression unless I improve.

I have King-high with a backdoor spade flush draw on a Jack-high board. Minni has placed a half-pot continuation bet of 200. Given the pot odds of 3-to-1 and the fact that we are heads-up, folding King-high with an overcard and some backdoor potential would be too exploitable. I will call to see a turn card, looking to improve to a King or pick up a spade draw.

On the turn, the 5h does not improve my King-high hand or my backdoor flush potential. As the out-of-position player who called a flop bet, my standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out here with only King-high would likely be ineffective as a bluff and has no value. I will check and see how Minni acts.

I opened extremely wide preflop and c‑bet the dry flop as a continuation with a very wide range. Flash called my small flop bet and then checked the turn (4h 9c Js 5h) — a very benign card for my hand. With Tc2d I have virtually no equity (only a single‑card pair on the river if a T or 2 comes) and I don’t block meaningful parts of his value range. Betting the turn would be a pure bluff that risks a check‑raise or a call from better Jx/9x/paired hands, bloating a pot I’m unlikely to win at showdown.

I’m going to pot‑control and realize my (minimal) showdown equity — check behind. If he checks the river I can try for a cheap showdown or bluff then; if he bets the river I’ll fold to meaningful sizing unless I actually pair up. Checking preserves chips and keeps us flexible for the river.

I have improved to top pair with my King on the river. On the turn, Minni checked back, which often indicates she does not have a Jack and is either playing a marginal hand like a 9 or a pocket pair for pot control, or she is giving up on a bluff. By checking the river, I am playing my top pair (with a weak kicker) cautiously while also giving Minni the opportunity to bluff with any missed straight draws (like 67 or 78) or to bet a marginal hand that she might have checked on the turn. Since the King is an overcard to the board, betting might simply fold out the hands I want to get value from, whereas checking allows me to capture more chips if she decides to turn her hand into a bluff or value-bet a weaker pair.

Board: 4h 9c Js 5h Kh. I’m on the button with Tc2d — no pair, no relevant blocker, essentially zero showdown value. I opened extremely wide preflop and c‑bet the dry flop; Flash called the small bet and then checked the turn. The river king is a blank for my hand and doesn’t credibly block strong parts of his calling range (Jx, 9x, small pairs, some floats). Betting now would be a pure bluff; with the pot at 800 a half‑pot bluff (~400) needs >33% folds to be +EV. Given Flash’s line (calling the flop) he’s likely to defend with many of those hands and fold far less than that. There’s no reason to risk chips for a marginal bluff — I’ll preserve my stack and realize the (minimal) showdown equity by checking behind.

7-4 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. Although I am on the button in a heads-up situation, the hand's post-flop playability is extremely poor. Given that Minni has a significant chip lead and has been active in defending the Big Blind, there is no reason to risk chips or attempt a bluff with this hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

Heads-up on the button with K♦3♥ — a marginal but playable Kx in position. In heads-up play the button should open very wide and take the initiative; I want to build the pot and apply pressure to the big blind (Flash), who has shown frequent preflop aggression but also many flat calls. I’ll raise to 300 total (3x the BB) — big enough to get folds and protect my hand, but not oversized given my deep stack (29,800) and his 10,050. Plan: if he flats I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops; if he 3-bets large I’ll largely fold K3o and wait for better spots.

Holding Q4o in the Big Blind, I am facing a 3x open from Minni. While defending a wide range is standard in heads-up play, Q4o is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position and is easily dominated. Given that Minni has a significantly larger stack and has been playing aggressively, I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

In a heads-up situation, King-high (K4o) is well within the standard opening range from the button. I have a 100BB stack, and while Minni has a significant lead, maintaining aggression and playing standard ranges is the correct approach to accumulate chips. I will raise to 3x the big blind to 300.

Flash opened to 300 from the button (SB). I’m in the BB with pocket fives (55). Effective stack is about 97–98bb, so set-mining has good implied odds heads-up. 3-betting is an option, but a min 3-bet to 500 is unlikely to generate meaningful fold equity and a larger 3-bet would commit too much out of position with a small pair. I’ll call the 200 more (total 300) to keep the pot manageable, realize equity, and see a flop — raise big for value if I hit a set, otherwise control the pot OOP and fold to heavy aggression when appropriate.

I have pocket 5s on 7♠-6♠-8♣ — I missed my set and currently hold an underpair. Villain opened wide heads-up preflop and then checked the flop, so their range is wide but also contains many hands that currently beat me (pairs/overcards) and a number of two-spade combos that have flush draws. I have two clean outs to a set and eight cards (4s and 9s) that complete a straight on the turn (roughly 10 meaningful outs total), plus remote backdoor spade considerations. Betting here to bluff or protect would bloat the pot OOP and frequently get called by overcards/draws, putting me in uncomfortable spots with a marginal holding. I’ll check to control the pot, realize equity, and only pursue the hand if I improve (set/straight) or if the opponent barrels very small on the turn. I’ll fold to heavy turn aggression unless I hit.

On a board like 7s-6s-8c, the texture heavily favors the Big Blind's calling range, which contains many more combinations of straights (like 59 or 45), two pairs, and sets. My hand, King-high (Kd4h), has no connectivity or draw on this board. Betting here is risky because Minni is unlikely to fold many hands given how well this flop hits her range. Checking back allows me to see a turn for free and keeps the pot small, which is prudent given the board connectivity and my hand's lack of equity.

Turn is J♠, giving a three-spade board (7♠-6♠-8♣-J♠). I have 5♠5♦ — an underpair with ~10 clean outs to improve to a straight or set, but the J♠ completes many flushes (any two spades in villain’s hand) and leaves lots of hands that already beat me. Villain opened wide from the button and checked the flop, so their range is broad and contains many spade draws and pairs; I’m out of position. I’ll check to control the pot, realize equity, and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal holding. Plan: if Flash bets small I can call for my outs; if they fire a large/commitment-sized bet or shove I’ll fold.

On a board of 7s-6s-8c-Js, the texture is incredibly dangerous. It completed several straights (T9, 95, 54) and the spade flush draw. My hand, Kd4h, has no real equity or showdown value. While Minni has checked twice, this board texture hits a Big Blind defending range much harder than my button opening range. Betting here as a bluff is risky because she may be trapping with a made hand or a strong draw given her massive stack advantage. Checking behind allows me to see the river for free and potentially realize equity if a King falls, while keeping the pot small.

I called preflop to set-mine and followed the plan to pot-control — I never improved and both of us checked flop and turn. On the river (Q♣) my 5♠5♦ is still just an underpair: it beats missed A/K highs but loses to any pair (6/7/8/ J/Q), and a spade flush line would also beat me. Betting here would be a thin bluff that mostly gets weaker hands to fold but gets called by the hands that actually beat me, so it’s low or negative EV. With a small pot (600) and deep stacks there’s no reason to gamble out of position — take the free showdown. Check and see a showdown rather than bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

The board ended up 7s-6s-8c-Js-Qc, which is extremely connected and features three spades. Minni has checked on all three post-flop streets. While it's possible she is weak, my King-high (Kd4h) has very little showdown value, but it's also not a great hand to bluff with on such a coordinated board where she could easily have a piece of it (any 6, 7, 8, J, or Q) that she's willing to call a small river bet with. Since the pot is small and I have no real equity, checking back and taking the showdown is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) I should open-raise rather than just call 50. T7o is well within a standard HU raising range — I want to seize the initiative, use positional advantage postflop and fold out many of the BB’s marginal hands. A 3x BB open to 300 gives good fold equity without bloating the pot with a marginal holding; if Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold, if he just calls I’ll play for position and c-bet favorable flops or control pot size depending on texture. Raising to 300 is the most +EV line here.

I have a premium hand (Pocket Jacks) in a heads-up situation. Minni has been quite aggressive throughout the match. I need to 3-bet for value and to take control of the hand. A raise to 900 is a standard size that builds a pot and puts pressure on her opening range.

I opened to 300 on the button and Flash shoved back a standard 3-bet to 900. T‑7 offsuit is far from premium, but in HU with position it’s well within a defend/call range vs a 3‑bet sizing this size. Calling keeps the pot controllable, lets me use position postflop, and gives me reasonable pot odds: I must call 600 into a 1,200 pot (≈33% break‑even equity). I don’t want to 4‑bet bluff here — T7o lacks the blockers and equity to make a profitable 4‑bet vs a shortish 3‑bet range, and a larger 4‑bet would commit too much with a marginal hand. Plan: call 900 total, see the flop in position, c-bet or check based on texture, fold to heavy action on unfavorable boards, and extract value when I hit. If Flash 4‑bets shove I’ll fold this hand.

The flop is quite dangerous for my pocket Jacks as it contains both an Ace and a Queen, which are two overcards that significantly hit the range of hands Minni would use to call a pre-flop 3-bet. At this point, I am likely behind any Ace or Queen in her hand. By checking, I am looking to control the size of the pot and evaluate her reaction. I am essentially in 'pot control' mode, hoping to reach a showdown cheaply or fold if the pressure becomes too high on such a coordinated board.

Flop: Ad Qh 6h. I hold Ts7c — no pair, no heart, no meaningful draw. Flash was the 3-bettor preflop, so his calling/3‑betting range is weighted heavily toward Ax, broadway hands and pocket pairs that connect with this Ace‑high board far more often than my air. With position and a big effective stack advantage I should not bloat the pot bluffing into a checked flop that likely hits his range. Checking controls the pot, preserves fold equity on later streets, and lets me see a free turn where I can realize equity if I pick up a pair or backdoor. Plan: check, fold to a sizable turn bet unless the sizing is tiny (in which case I can call and re-evaluate), and only bet/raise if I actually improve or he gives a clear opportunity to steal on a safer river.

The turn brings a third heart to a board that already contains two overcards (Ace and Queen) to my pocket Jacks. Since I do not hold a heart, the board is increasingly dangerous. My check on the flop was for pot control, and Minni checking back could indicate she is also cautious or has a marginal hand. However, the nine of hearts completes many draws. I will check again to see Minni's reaction. I am not interested in betting into this board, as I am likely only getting called by hands that beat me (Ax, Qx, or flushes).

I’m out of position preflop caller to Flash’s 3‑bet and I’ve missed both flop and turn with T7o. The turn 9h completes a third heart (Qh‑6h‑9h) which heavily favors the 3‑bettor’s range (many A‑x and broadway heart combos now have a made flush or heavy equity). Flash checked the turn, which could be weakness or a slowplay, but I have no pair, no heart and very little fold equity. Don’t bloat the pot or attempt a thin bluff into a range that connects so well — control the pot and preserve chips. Action: check behind on the turn; fold to any meaningful bet on the river and only look for a bluff or a value attempt if the runout and opponent’s actions clearly justify it.

The river has brought a fourth heart to the board, and with an Ace and a Queen also present, my pocket Jacks are in a very precarious position. I do not hold a heart, meaning any single heart in Minni's hand beats me. Given that the action went check-check on both the flop and the turn, it's possible Minni is also weak, but betting here serves no purpose; I won't be called by worse hands, and I'll likely be raised by any flush. Checking is the only viable option to see if I can reach a cheap showdown or to evaluate a potential bet from her.

I opened preflop, Flash 3‑bet and I called; my hand (T♠7♣) missed the entire board. The runout Ad Qh 6h 9h 5h contains four hearts, which heavily favors the 3‑better’s range (many Ax and broadway/heart combos). Flash checked flop and turn — could be pot control or a slowplay — but I have no pair, no heart and very little fold equity. A river bluff has low EV here: he will call with many single pair hands and any heart, and I don’t want to bloat the pot with a losing hand. Preserve the stack and move to better spots: check behind and avoid risking chips on a thin bluff.

In a heads-up situation, Qs 9s is a very strong hand, well within the top tier of my opening range from the button. I want to take the initiative and build a pot while I have position. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to either win the pot immediately or set up a post-flop situation where I can use my positional advantage against Minni.

Heads‑up vs a button open to 300. I’m in the big blind with K♥4♣ — a marginal hand but playable heads‑up. I only need to put in 200 more into the 400 pot (total commitment 300), giving ~3:1 pot odds (≈25% equity required). Flash opens a wide range from the button often, so Kx has reasonable equity and can flop top pair. I won’t 3‑bet a weak kicker; I’ll call, see the flop, and then proceed cautiously — check/control the pot out of position and fold to heavy aggression on bad textures, or extract value when I connect.

Flop T♥ 4♣ 3♦ and I hold K♥4♣ — I’ve got second pair (a 4) with a decent kicker but I’m out of position. Flash opened wide from the button preflop, so his range has many missed overcards and some bluffs. My priority is pot control and to realize equity rather than inflate an OOP pot with a marginal hand. I’ll check and see how he reacts: call small-to-medium continuation bets to extract value from worse hands and to keep bluffs in, but fold to heavy pressure or large overbets unless I have a clear read to proceed. If he checks back I’m happy to take showdown cheaply.

On a dry board of Th-4s-3d, as the pre-flop aggressor, I maintain a range advantage. I hold Qs 9s, which gives me one overcard to the board and a backdoor flush draw. Minni checked to me, which is a standard defensive play. I will place a continuation bet of 300, which is half the pot, to pressure her air and marginal hands. This sizing is effective on dry boards for winning the pot immediately or setting up further action on later streets.

I’ll call 300. I have K♥4♣ — second pair with a decent kicker heads‑up. Flash opened wide from the button, so his range contains many missed overcards and bluffs; a modest 300 into the current pot gives me ~25% equity required to call (300/(900+300)). Calling controls the pot out of position, keeps weaker hands and bluffs in, and lets me re‑evaluate on the turn. Plan: call small turn bets for value, fold to large overbets or shove lines that represent strong two‑pair/sets/top pair with a better kicker, and extract value later on safe runouts or when he shows weakness.

I have K♥4♣ on T♥-4♠-3♦-5♦ — second pair with a good kicker heads‑up, out of position. Flash led the flop and I called to control the pot; he then checked the turn, which signals either pot control/weakness or a line that wants to see a safe river. This is a marginal made hand that wants showdown, not a bloated pot. Betting here could fold out some worse overcards but also invites a check‑raise or commits me to a larger pot against stronger hands (Tx, two‑pair, 5x, or a disguised straight). With deep stacks I prefer pot control: check behind, take it to the river, and re‑evaluate — call small river bets for value, fold to large river aggression that represents a better made hand.

The turn 5d is a relatively blank card that doesn't significantly improve my hand. Since Minni called my continuation bet on the flop, she likely has a marginal hand such as a pair of Tens or a smaller pocket pair. With only Queen-high and no real draw, I have very little equity if she calls a second barrel. Checking behind allows me to control the size of the pot and see a free river card, where I might hit a Queen or potentially bluff depending on the final board and her action.

The river K♦ pairs my king and gives me two pair (Kings and Fours) on T♥-4♠-3♦-5♦-K♦. Flash opened wide preflop and c-bet the flop but checked the turn — a line that often contains lots of single-pair hands, missed overcards and bluffs. Heads‑up my two pair is very strong versus that range and should get called by many worse hands (Tx, single 4x, weaker Kx, some turned/rivered pairs and bluffs). I want to extract value while keeping the bet small enough to induce calls, so I’ll bet about half the pot. Bet 600 to target calls from worse hands and deny free showdowns; if he raises big I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing (large raises often represent Kings/trips), but against standard calling ranges this is an EV+ value bet.

On the river, a King appears, which is a scare card. I have Queen-high and no showdown value. Minni's lead of 600 into 1200 (a 1/3 pot bet) after I checked the turn suggests she likely has a marginal hand like a Ten or a small pair that she's betting for thin value or protection. While I could potentially represent the King with a raise, Minni's large stack and past behavior suggest she can be sticky. Without a strong story for the King (given my turn check), folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with 2♥T♠ — marginal but playable in position. In heads-up play you should open a very wide range; raising takes the initiative, lets me steal the blind, and gives me position postflop. I’ll raise to the minimum open (200 total) to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable. If Flash folds I win the pot; if he calls I can play postflop in position; if he 3-bets large or shoves I’ll fold this weak, poorly-connected hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Trey of diamonds. Minni, on the button, has raised to 200. I need to call only 100 more to see the flop in a pot of 300. With 4:1 pot odds and a suited hand that has some high-card value, defending my big blind is the standard and correct play here. I'm not looking to build a massive pot pre-flop with this hand, but it plays well enough post-flop to justify a call.

I have flopped a flush draw with Jd3d on a 2d9d8s board. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check and see how they act. This keeps the pot manageable while I look to complete my draw.

I opened preflop and got a call; I’m in position and I’ve flopped a small pair (pair of deuces with T kicker) on 2♦ 9♦ 8♠. Flash checked the flop, and in heads‑up play a large portion of his calling range are unpaired broadways and weak holdings that missed this board. Betting takes the initiative, extracts value from worse hands (Kx/Qx/Jx/Tx, some 9/8 with weak kickers that will call), and denies equity to turn cards and backdoor draws. The board is somewhat coordinated (connected 9‑8 and two diamonds), so I don’t want to give a free card; however my hand is not strong enough to build a big pot, so I’ll use a controlled half‑pot c‑bet to get folds and keep the pot manageable. Bet half the pot (200). If called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (check/control vs large actions, fold to a sizeable check‑raise), re-evaluating based on the card and Flash’s reaction.

I have a flush draw with Jd 3d on a board of 2d 9d 8s. Minni has made a standard continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 400. With 3:1 pot odds and approximately 35% equity to make a flush by the river, a call is the most straightforward and mathematically sound play. I am out of position, so calling allows me to see the turn without bloating the pot unnecessarily if I miss.

On the turn, the board pairs with the 8c, which doesn't improve my hand but also doesn't change the board texture significantly for most of Minni's likely opening range. I still have a jack-high flush draw. Being out of position, I will continue to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows me to see if she wants to continue betting or if I can get a free card to try and hit my flush on the river. Leading out here would likely only be called by better hands or fold out hands I currently beat, so checking is the most prudent play to manage the pot while chasing my draw.

Turn pairs the board (2♦ 9♦ 8♠ 8♣). I hold 2♥T♠ — bottom pair with a ten kicker. Flash called my flop c-bet and now checks the paired turn; that line is consistent with a one-pair, a missed diamond draw, or a slow-played 8/9. Betting here would fold out some worse hands but also build a pot where I’m often behind to 8x/9x/two-pair — I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. With position and a big effective stack advantage, pot control is the prudent line: check and evaluate his action. If he bets a small-to-medium size I can call to see showdown; I’ll fold to a large raise. If he checks back I can pursue a small river steal on safe runouts or show down my pair cheaply.

I missed my flush draw and am left with Jack-high. The board is 2d 9d 8s 8c Ks. On the river, the King is a scare card, but since both Minni and I checked the turn, it's possible she has a marginal hand like a 9 or a small pair. I have no showdown value, but I also don't have a very convincing story to tell if I bet, as I've checked every street so far. Blown draws in the Big Blind are common, and Minni is unlikely to fold a better hand (like any pair) for a small or medium-sized bet given our stack sizes. I will check and concede the pot unless Minni also has a lower missed draw, which is unlikely.

I actually have two pair on the river (8s/8c on the board plus my 2h with the 2d on board = 8s/2s two pair). Pot is 800 and Flash (BB) called my flop c‑bet then checked turn and river. His line is consistent with a wide calling range: missed diamond draws, single pairs (9x, Kx, Tx/Qx), and occasional 2x combos. My two‑pair is good vs most of that range but is vulnerable specifically to Kx and 9x (both beat me) and ties with remaining deuce combos. Betting here mostly gets called by better hands and by worse hands I can beat — it risks bloating the pot when I’m often behind. Pot control / showdown is the highest EV line: check and see his action. If he bets a small–medium size I’ll call for showdown; I’ll respect a very large river shove as likely Kx/9x and fold if the sizing makes the call incorrect.

In heads-up play, holding the button provides a significant advantage as I will be in position for all subsequent betting rounds. 5c3c is a speculative suited hand that benefits from the initiative. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to pressure the big blind and attempt to take down the blinds or play a controlled pot in position.

Heads-up vs the button who opened to 300; A♠7♣ is a very workable hand in the big blind — an ace blocker plus decent top-pair potential against a wide SB opening range. Folding is too nitty; calling 200 to 300 total keeps me OOP and gives Flash good c-bet leverage. I want to seize initiative and force folds from weaker holdings (smaller aces, broadways, suited connectors) while still getting called by worse, so I 3‑bet to a size that gives meaningful fold equity but doesn’t commit me excessively. Raising to 1000 (~3.3x the open) accomplishes that: it pressures his wide raising frequency, isolates him, and keeps postflop play straightforward. Plan if called: c-bet many dry A-high flops for value, check/controls on coordinated boards and re-evaluate to aggression or pot control based on opponent’s reaction; fold to extreme 4-bet shove only if the shove range is heavily dominated and odds are incorrect.

I am facing a 3-bet to 1000 from Minni. My hand, 5c3c, is a very weak suited connector that struggles to realize equity against a 3-bet range. Although I have the advantage of being in position, calling 700 more (7% of my stack) with such a marginal hand is not profitable in the long run, especially against an opponent who has shown significant aggression. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with 6♣8♣ — a suited connector that plays very well heads-up. Against one opponent I should be opening a wide range to take the initiative and use position postflop. Raising to the min of 200 (2x BB) is a standard, efficient size: it builds the pot when I have good playability, denies the BB a free flop, and keeps SPR manageable with these stacks. Plan: if called, c-bet on dry, favorable flops and play draws aggressively; if met with a large 3‑bet I’ll reassess and likely fold unless the sizing/reads justify a call.

I am in the Big Blind with Seven of Spades and Deuce of Hearts, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Minni, who currently has a massive chip lead, has made a minimum raise to 200. Although it only costs 100 to call, the hand has zero playability and I would be out of position throughout the rest of the hand. With a stack of 9,250 against an opponent with over 30,000, there is no reason to risk chips with such a poor hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

In heads-up play, K7o is a strong enough hand to open from the button. My standard opening raise has been 300, and I will maintain that consistency here to apply pressure and build a pot with a hand that has decent high-card value.

Heads-up in the big blind with Q♥3♥ vs a button(SB) open to 300. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 more to call — ~2:1 pot odds. Q3s is a playable, suited hand heads‑up: it can make top pair, two pair, and has flush/backdoor equity. Raising here (min to 500) with a marginal hand would commit me out of position and put me in difficult post‑flop spots against a player who raises frequently. Folding is too tight given the price and my stack depth. Plan: call to see a flop, play cautiously OOP — continue with top pair/strong draws, otherwise control pot size and fold to heavy pressure.

Heads‑up OOP with Qh3h on T♠4♥9♦: I have no pair, only a backdoor heart draw and two overcards. Opponent (Flash) has shown a tendency to raise preflop, but on this dry, moderately coordinated flop I don't want to manufacture a pot out of position with a marginal hand. Checking controls the pot size, lets me realize my equity (free turn card to pair a Q/3 or pick up hearts), and keeps the initiative for later streets if I improve. Plan: check — call a small/medium bet to see a turn given decent fold equity vs bluffs and backdoor equity, but fold to heavy pressure or large raises since my hand is easily dominated and not worth bloating the pot OOP. I won't lead or raise here with Q3h.

On a board of Ts-4h-9d, I have King-high. As the pre-flop aggressor, I'll make a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to try and take it down. This board is somewhat connected, but Minni's check gives me the opportunity to maintain initiative. If called, I can evaluate the turn, and if raised, I will likely fold given I have no made hand or significant draw.

I’ll call 300. I’m out of position with Q♥3♥ on T♠4♥9♦ — no pair, one overcard (Q) and only a backdoor heart possibility. Flash has been opening/raising frequently, and his flop bet is a modest-sized probe rather than a big polarizing shove. The pot is 900 so calling 300 gives roughly 4:1 on the money (need ~25% equity). My chance to improve to at least a pair or better by the river (Q or 3, plus backdoor heart possibilities) is in that neighborhood, so this is a +EV price to see the turn. Raising OOP with this hand would overcommit in a tough spot and is unnecessary against an aggressive opponent. Plan after calling: fold to large turn/river pressure unless I pair the Q (or pick up a real 4‑card heart draw on the turn), and be prepared to value/raise if I improve.

Check. Heads‑up OOP with Q♥3♥ on T♠–4♥–9♦–5♥: I have no pair but a four‑card heart draw plus a Q overcard.
