I roughly have ~15 outs (~33% to improve by the river), so this is a hand I want to realize equity with rather than bloat the pot OOP. Flash has shown frequent aggression; betting here risks facing a shove/raise that forces a difficult fold of significant equity. Plan: keep the pot under control — check and see the river. If Flash fires a small/medium bet on the river (≈≤½ pot) I’ll call to realize equity; fold to large, polarized bets unless I pair or hit the flush, and extract value if I complete.

On the turn, the board is Ts-9d-4h-5h. I currently have King-high with no significant draws. After Minni called my flop continuation bet, it is likely she has a piece of the board, such as a Ten, a Nine, or a straight draw like QJ or 87. Since the 5h doesn't significantly change the board texture or improve my hand, betting again is risky against a sticky opponent like Minni. By checking back, I can see a free river card and potentially realize my equity if a King or perhaps a Seven falls, or simply reach a cheap showdown if she is also holding a weak hand.

I completed the flush on the river: Qh3h with board Ts–4h–9d–5h–Jh gives me a Q‑high heart flush. Villain bet a small c‑bet on the flop then checked the turn and river — that line looks like medium‑strength hands (T, 9, Jx) or missed draws/pot‑control, not a consistent look for a large slow‑played nut. Checking back loses value from hands that will call (one‑pair hands, some J‑hands and weaker hearts); the main hands that beat me are Ah (and Kh to a lesser extent), but those combos are relatively few and often would have bet or polarized differently. I want a value bet that gets called by worse pairs and thin calls but doesn't overcommit me against the very small range of nut‑flush combos, so I size to ~2/3 pot. Bet 800 into the 1200 pot to extract value; if he raises big I’ll assume a majority of the raise range is a nut or near‑nut hand and will fold or re‑evaluate based on sizing.

On a river board of Ts-9d-4h-5h-Jh, I have only King-high. Minni called my flop continuation bet, which suggests she has a piece of the board (a Ten or a Nine) or a draw. The river Jack is a card that likely connects with her calling range (like QJ or J-Ten). After the turn went check-check, her lead for 800 into a 1200 pot on the river looks like a value bet or an attempt to take the pot away from a hand that showed weakness. Since I have no showdown value and don't beat even her 'bluffs' if they include small pairs or Ace-high, and given her significant chip lead, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with A♠K♠ — a clear open-raise spot. I want initiative, to isolate the BB and retain post-flop leverage in position. I’ll size a standard 3x BB open (to 300 total) — big enough to fold out weak hands and get value from worse aces and broadways, but not so large that I unnecessarily commit a big portion of my deep stack. If Flash 3-bets, I’ll reassess (call or 4-bet/shove depending on sizing and reads); for now a 300 total raise maximizes my equity and postflop options.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Hearts and Nine of Spades. Minni, who has a significant chip lead, has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. T9o is a solid defensive hand in this position, and I am getting 2:1 odds to see a flop. Given the current tournament stage and the stack depths, calling is the most standard and effective play to realize my hand's equity without overcommitting pre-flop.

I am in the Big Blind and will be first to act on this flop. The board is 4d-5d-4s, which is a paired board. My hand, T9o, has no direct connection to this board. Given that Minni is the pre-flop aggressor and has a massive chip lead, I should play cautiously. Checking is the standard play here as the caller in the Big Blind to see how the opponent reacts to this texture.

I was the preflop raiser and have A♠K♠ on a 4♦-5♦-4♠ flop. In position with two big overcards and a little backdoor spade potential, this is a standard c-bet spot — Flash checked, so I can use a half-pot probe to fold out pure floats/weak diamonds and get value from worse pairs/overcards. A 300 (≈1/2 pot) bet keeps the pot manageable, preserves fold equity, and lets me re-evaluate on the turn: if he raises big I’ll give credit to sets/strong hands and fold; if he calls I can continue vs many of his calling combos or barrel favorable turn cards (A/K). Bet 300.

The flop of 4d-5d-4s is a paired board that doesn't connect with my Ten-high (T9o). Minni, the pre-flop aggressor and significant chip leader, has made a continuation bet of 300 into a 600 pot. While she could be betting with air, T9o has very little equity and no draws. I am out of position and would likely need to hit a Nine or Ten to improve, which could still be behind overpairs or better. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my 83BB stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with Qd2d. In heads-up play, Q2 suited is a strong enough hand to open for a raise from the small blind to take control of the pot and apply pressure on the big blind. A standard raise to 300 is consistent with my previous play.

Heads-up vs the SB who opened to 300. I hold A♦J♦ — a very strong heads-up hand and ahead of a wide SB opening range (broadways, suited connectors, small pairs and occasional bluffs). Pot is 400 and I need 200 to call; instead I want to take the initiative and build/isolate with a value 3‑bet. A raise to 1,200 (total) is large enough to generate fold equity against many speculative hands and get value from worse A‑x, Kx and medium pockets, but small enough to avoid committing too much if Flash jams. If called I’ll continue as the aggressor (c‑bet favorable Ace/broadway boards, pot‑control on dangerous textures). If Flash 4‑bets very large I’ll re‑evaluate against their range, but overall 3‑betting here maximizes fold equity and extracts value from a wide opening range.

In Hand #107, I am on the button with Qd2d and faced with a large 3-bet to 1200 from Minni. While Q2 suited has some playability in position, a 4x 3-bet from the chip leader is a very strong signal. Looking at recent history, Minni has been increasing the size of her 3-bets (previously 700, 900, 1000, and now 1200), suggesting either premium strength or a concerted effort to use her massive stack to bully me. Given that I previously folded A7s to a smaller 3-bet, calling here with Q2s would be inconsistent and likely lead to a difficult post-flop situation where I am forced to commit a significant portion of my stack on a weak holding. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous spot to challenge her lead.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with T9o — a playable, well-connected hand in position. The pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call, but I want initiative and fold equity against a wide BB range. Raising to the min (200 total) is standard: it applies pressure, isolates Flash, and keeps the pot a reasonable size vs his 7,950 stack. If he calls I’ll play the flop in position and C-bet appropriate textures; if he 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold or defend depending on sizing). Calling is too passive and gives him the chance to take control; folding is unnecessary with this hand. Raise to 200 to open the hand aggressively from position.

Holding 9-5 offsuit in the big blind against a min-raise from a massive chip leader (Minni), I am getting 3:1 odds to call. However, I will be out of position for the rest of the hand with a very weak holding that doesn't flop well. Given the stack disadvantage and the low quality of the hand, it is better to fold and wait for a more profitable spot to commit chips.

I have King-Queen suited on the button, which is an extremely strong starting hand in a heads-up situation. With a stack of 79 big blinds, I want to take the initiative and build the pot early. A standard raise to 3x the big blind is appropriate here to extract value and potentially take down the blinds or see a flop with the betting lead.

Heads-up in the big blind with Ad5c — this is a hand I want to see a flop with rather than take an out-of-position, small 3-bet. Flash’s open to 300 is standard and likely wide from the SB/button, so calling 200 (300 total) gives good pot odds (calling 200 into a 400 pot) and preserves stack depth for postflop play. A three-bet to the minimum 500 would be a weak sizing that still leaves me OOP and can be called by a wide range; a larger isolation 3-bet could be reasonable but I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal kicker hand. Plan: call, see the flop — continue aggressively on A-high boards or with strong draws, exercise pot control or fold to heavy action on dry boards where my ace is likely dominated.

Heads-up out of position with Ad5c on 3h-3c-2h. I have only Ace-high with very little improvement potential, but vs a wide button open my Ace is ahead of a lot of unpaired overcards and broadways. The board is low and paired — sets/trips are possible but relatively rare — so applying measured pressure makes sense. A c-bet ~50% pot (300 into 600) buys the pot a good percentage of the time, keeps the pot manageable if I’m called, and charges speculative hands; if Flash raises I can fold since my hand has poor showdown equity against pairs/trips. Plan: bet 300 to take it down or realize equity cheaply; proceed cautiously on the turn only with improvement or a clear read.

In this heads-up situation, I raised pre-flop with KQ suited and Minni called. The flop is 3-3-2, a very dry board that likely missed both of us. Minni has led out with a donk bet of 300 into the 600 pot. While I haven't hit a pair, my KQ represents significant overcard equity and I have the potential to take the pot on later streets if I hit a King or Queen, or if Minni checks the turn. Folding to a single bet on such a low board with KQ is too weak, and raising doesn't accomplish much against a range that might include a small pair or a 2. I will call to see the turn and re-evaluate.

Turn changes the hand: with Ad5c on 3h-3c-2h-4d I’ve completed the A-2-3-4-5 wheel. That is a very strong made hand heads-up — only full houses/quads beat me (combinations like both remaining 3s, 3+2, pocket 2s, pocket 4s, or 3+4). Flash called my flop c-bet, so his continuation range contains a lot of hands I now beat (overcards, single pairs, some two-pair/medium pairs that will still call a reasonable bet). I want to extract value while keeping worse hands in the pot and price out unlikely draws. Betting a pot-sized amount gives me good value vs calls and still lets me fold to a very large raise that should represent the small set of full houses/quads. Plan: value-bet 1,200 into the 1,200 pot; if he raises big I’ll re-assess (raise likely indicates a full house/quads).

In this hand, I raised pre-flop with KQ suited and was called by Minni in the big blind. The flop came 3-3-2, and Minni led out with a small donk bet, which I called with my overcards. The turn is a 4, which completes a potential straight for hands like A5. Minni has now fired a pot-sized bet of 1200. This betting sequence (donk flop, pot turn) is typically indicative of a very strong hand, such as trips or a straight, or a high-equity draw. While I have two overcards, I am currently holding only King-high on a board that is likely to have hit Minni's defending range. Given the large bet size and my relatively short stack compared to Minni, calling here would commit a significant portion of my chips without a made hand. I will fold and look for a better spot to utilize my stack.

Heads-up on the button with pocket deuces and deep effective stacks (~72 BB on Flash). This is a classic set-mining/value hand where I want initiative and to isolate the big blind. A standard 2x BB raise (to 200 total) accomplishes that: builds the pot for a set, folds out some worse hands, and keeps the action heads-up with me having postflop position. If Flash shoves I’ll be happy to call given my pair’s equity against a wide shove; if he calls I’ll proceed looking for a set and play cautiously on ace/king-heavy boards.

I am holding Jack of Hearts and Nine of Hearts in the Big Blind. Minni has min-raised to 200 from the Button/Small Blind. With a stack of 72.5 BBs and a solid suited connector, this is an easy call to see the flop and realize my equity. The price is excellent (100 to call into a pot of 300), and my hand plays well post-flop.

I have middle pair (nines) with a Jack kicker and a gutshot straight draw on a very coordinated board (Tc-7c-9s). Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked to me. While my hand has value, betting here might force out hands I beat or get me check-raised by better hands or strong draws. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and keep Minni's range wide. It is a standard play to check back middle pair in this spot.

I raised preflop with 22 and the flop (Tc 7c 9s) is fairly favorable to c-betting: many of Flash’s calling hands are overcards or draws and can fold to pressure, while club/straight draws pay to be charged. I’ll make a small continuation bet (~1/3 pot = 150) to extract folds from A/K/Q highs, deny free equity to draws, and keep the pot manageable if called. If Flash calls I’ll reassess on the turn and generally check-call to realize equity unless I improve; if he check-raises big I’ll fold because my hand has little showdown value without a set. Bet 150.

I have middle pair (nines) with a Jack kicker and a gutshot straight draw on a coordinated board (Tc-7c-9s). Minni has made a small continuation bet of 150 into a pot of 400. This price is very attractive, and my hand has significant equity against her range. I should not fold middle pair here, but raising is also not ideal as it might overplay my hand or force out bluffs. Calling is the most solid play to control the pot size and see how the turn develops.

On the turn, the board pairs with the Seven of Spades. My hand, a pair of nines with a Jack kicker and a gutshot straight draw, retains significant value but is vulnerable to a Ten or any Seven. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the most appropriate action to control the pot size and see how Minni reacts to the board pairing. I am looking to reach showdown cheaply or potentially improve on the river.

Turn paired the board (Tc 7c 9s 7s) and my pocket deuces remain an underpair with very little showdown value. Flash check-called my small flop c-bet, so his range contains a lot of hands that now beat me (top pairs, T9, 99, possibly slow-played hands) plus some draws — betting here has limited fold equity and exposes me to a check-raise I should fold. With deep stacks and a 700-pot I prefer pot control and to realize equity cheaply: check behind to see the river and re-evaluate. If he bets the river I’ll fold large bets without improvement and selectively call small ones depending on the card and sizing.

On the river, the board has completed as Tc-7c-9s-7s-Ts, creating a board of Tens full of Sevens with a Nine. My best hand is Tens full of Sevens with my Jack kicker (Tc-Ts-7c-7s-Jh). Since the board is so coordinated and heavy with high pairs, any Ten or Seven in Minni's range beats me. Furthermore, any card higher than a Jack (Ace, King, Queen) also beats me by improving the fifth card kicker. Betting offers little value as I will likely only be called by better hands or hands that chop. Checking is the most prudent action to reach showdown and minimize losses.

Check. The river (Tc 7c 9s 7s Ts) is a heavily paired, high-card board with three spades — my pocket deuces have essentially zero showdown value (the best five is the board with a 2 kicker). Flash cold‑called my flop c‑bet and then checked both turn and river; that line contains a lot of hands that now beat me (any T or 7, T9/99, many paired or slow‑played hands, plus some completed spade draws). Betting here has very limited fold equity and only risks getting called or check‑raised by better hands. With a 700 pot and deep stacks I prefer pot control and to avoid throwing good chips away with a near‑dead hand, so I’ll check and fold to any meaningful river bet.

I am in the Small Blind/Button position with 84o. While heads-up play typically involves playing a very wide range from the button, 84o is exceptionally weak. Given that Minni has a significant chip lead and has been playing aggressively (including several 3-bets recently), I will choose to fold this hand and wait for a better opportunity to commit my chips, rather than playing a marginal hand out of position post-flop against a dominant stack.

Raise to 300 total. Heads-up on the button I should open a wide range — Q5o is marginal but has Q-high playability and benefits from position. Calling the extra 50 is too passive and gives the BB good odds to see a flop; a 3x-type open to 300 applies pressure, wins the blinds often, and keeps initiative. Flash has ~75bb so he can defend, but I will fold Q5o to a sizable 3-bet and play cautiously postflop if called (continue with top pair, strong draws or favorable textures, otherwise check/fold or pot-control). 300 is a standard, comfortable sizing to accomplish those goals.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Six of clubs (T6s). Minni has raised to 300 from the Button/Small Blind. I have already posted 100, so I need to call 200 more to see the flop. The pot is currently 400, giving me 2:1 odds on a call. T6s is a reasonable hand to defend in a heads-up situation, as it has flush and straight potential and can occasionally win with a pair. Given the pot odds and the playability of suited connectors/one-gappers, calling is a standard defensive play here.

I have Ten-High on a Jack-Eight-Trey rainbow board. I have no direct pair or draw, only weak backdoor potential. As the out-of-position player against the pre-flop raiser, checking is the standard play to see how the opponent reacts. Leading here into the raiser with air would be a high-risk, low-reward play.

Heads-up on the button after my 300 open and a check from Flash. My Qh5c has no pair and basically no showdown value on this dry J♠-8♦-3♥ board, so I should be trying to pick up the pot rather than slow-play. A standard continuation bet (~50% pot) applies pressure to unpaired hands and small pocket pairs while keeping the size reasonable against a 7.35k stack. Bet 300 (half pot): if he folds I take it down; if he calls I will proceed cautiously — check the turn and only continue with improvement or clear equity, and fold to a strong check-raise or large turn/river aggression.

I have Ten-high on a board of Jack-Eight-Trey rainbow. I have no direct straight or flush draws and no pairs. Minni's continuation bet is standard, and while I'm getting 3:1 odds, my hand has very little equity to improve to the best hand. Conserving my stack for a better opportunity is the most prudent move here.

I am on the button with 5d2d, a speculative suited hand. While the hand is weak, it has potential for straights and flushes. Given the heads-up dynamic and the stack sizes, I'll complete the small blind to see a flop for a small investment, rather than raising into the much larger stack of Minni.

I'm in the big blind with Q♠2♣ and Flash (SB) just limped. Q2o is too weak to build a pot out of position — raising here would commit extra chips with poor postflop playability and often get called by better high-card hands. I'll check to keep the pot small, see a flop cheaply, and only continue if I connect (top pair, two pair, or a playable draw); otherwise fold to meaningful aggression. This preserves my large stack and avoids playing a marginal hand OOP against an opponent who has shown both limps and aggression.

I have Q♠2♣ on K♠‑3♥‑K♣ — I missed the flop (no pair, no draw) and I'm out of position vs a limping SB. This board is dominated by any K or pocket pair and offers no realistic bluffs that profitably fold out better hands. Pot‑control: check and concede the initiative — fold to a bet. If Flash checks back I can see the turn cheap; if he bets I will fold and preserve my 32.5k stack for better spots.

On a paired board of Ks-3h-Kc, I have 5-high with no direct connection to the board. Minni has checked to me. Since I am in position and have very little equity with this hand, checking back is the most prudent play to see a free turn card and avoid building a pot with a weak hand against a much larger stack.

Turn decision: I have Q♠2♣ on Ks‑3h‑Kc‑Js — no pair, no relevant draw, very poor showdown value and dominated by almost any king, pocket pair, or decent broadway. Flash limped then checked the flop and checked the turn, which can be weakness but can also be a trap; with me out of position and a tiny pot relative to stacks, I shouldn’t build the pot with a junk hand. Best line is pot control: check and see the river for free. If Flash leads into me I’ll fold (no profitable call vs a meaningful bet); if he checks back I can re-evaluate the river and look for a better bluff/fold spot.

The board is paired (Ks-Kc) and the turn has brought a Jack of Spades. Minni has checked for the second time, indicating weakness or a very passive approach with a marginal hand. While I have no showdown value with 5-high, checking back allows me to see a free river card. There is little reason to bluff into a small pot against a player with a massive chip lead who has been calling down frequently in previous hands. I will check to maintain the pot size and hope for a favorable river or a cheap showdown if the action remains checked through.

Board: Ks 3h Kc Js 2s. I hold Qs2c — I picked up a lone deuce on the river for a small pair. Flash limped pre and checked flop/turn, which is a very wide, passive line and makes kings/trips less likely (those hands would usually extract value earlier). My pair of twos has modest showdown value versus that range (beats a lot of A‑high and other missed hands) but still loses to any K, any J, or any two with a better kicker. With stacks deep-to-me and the pot tiny (200), the right play is a small value bet to extract from worse hands that will call a cheap river bet while keeping the price of a raise meaningful. I’ll bet small (300) to get calls from worse pairs/Ax/weak broadways and fold if Flash shows aggression — a raise is likely to represent a king/trips or a strong jack and I’m happy to fold this marginal hand to avoid a costly mistake.

On a board of Ks-3h-Kc-Js-2s, I have bottom pair (deuces) with a 5 kicker. The board is quite heavy with overcards (King, King, Jack). Minni checked through the flop and turn but has now lead out on the river with a 300-chip bet into a 200-chip pot. My hand is effectively a bluff-catcher, but it is a very poor one. Any King, Jack, or Three in Minni's hand beats me, and even if she is also playing the board's two pair (Kings and Deuces), any card in her hand higher than a 5 (like an Ace, Queen, or Ten) would give her a better kicker. Given the polarizing over-bet and my lack of showdown value, folding is the safest and most logical play.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening very wide. 2♦9♥ is a weak hand, but from the SB/button I have positional advantage postflop and a very large stack (32,700) vs Flash's 7,150, so applying light pressure is profitable. A min open to 200 (2×BB) is a standard steal size here: it gives fold equity, keeps the pot small if called, and lets me play postflop in position where marginal hands gain value. If Flash 3-bets heavy or jams, 2-9o is easily folded; if he calls I can proceed cautiously with position. Overall raising to 200 maximizes long-term EV in this heads-up spot.

In Hand #114, I'm in the Big Blind with Qc7c and Minni has raised to 200. The pot is 300, and it costs me 100 to call. Although Minni has a significant stack advantage, Queen-Seven suited is a strong enough hand to defend my big blind against a small 2x raise in a heads-up situation. I will call to see the flop and re-evaluate.

I have flopped top pair with a Queen. In a heads-up match, top pair is a very strong hand, even with a mediocre kicker like a seven. The board has some straight and flush possibilities (hearts, and cards like KT or T9). I will follow the standard practice of checking to the pre-flop aggressor to see how she responds, intending to call most reasonable bet sizes.

Heads-up I opened very wide preflop so my range is broad here and the flop Qh–Jd–3h is fairly dry except for two hearts. Flash checked, showing either weakness or pot control. My 9♥2♦ has almost no showdown value (only backdoor heart and single-card outs), so I should be trying to win the pot now with position and a large stack advantage. A half‑pot c-bet (200 into the 400 pot) applies pressure to many of his missed hands while keeping the pot manageable if he calls. If he folds I pick up the pot; if he calls I can reassess on the turn; if he raises strongly I’ll respect that and fold this marginal holding. This sizing balances fold equity and pot control and is the +EV line here.

I have flopped top pair with my Queen. While my seven kicker is not exceptionally strong, it is quite solid in a heads-up encounter. Minni's bet of 200 into a 400-chip pot is a standard continuation bet. The board contains various draws, including a heart flush draw and several straight possibilities, so I must remain cautious. However, folding at this stage is not an option given the strength of my hand. I will choose to call rather than raise to keep the pot manageable and see how the turn card affects the board texture.

On the turn, the Ten of Hearts is a highly coordinated card that completes several potential straights (like K9 or 98) and completes a flush draw if Minni was holding two hearts. While I still hold top pair with my Queen, the board has become significantly more dangerous for my hand. Since I am out of position, I will check to see how Minni reacts. This allows me to gauge the strength of her hand and keeps the pot manageable, given the high probability that the board texture has improved her range or at least given her more semi-bluffing opportunities.

Board: Qh Jd 3h Th. I hold 9♥2♦ — no pair but I picked up a big draw on the turn: an open‑ended straight draw (Ks or 8s) plus a one‑card heart flush draw. That’s ~15 unique outs (~32% to improve on the river). Heads‑up and in position with a huge stack advantage, I should apply pressure to a range that called the flop but checked the turn (many marginal Qx/Jx/Kx or busted draws). A medium‑large turn bet both (1) picks up the 800 chip pot when he folds and (2) builds a pot I can hit with decent equity if called. I don’t want to overcommit to an all‑in — if he raises big or jams I’ll fold this drawing hand — but a ~3/4 pot bet maximizes fold equity and keeps the decision clear. Bet 600: strong enough to fold many marginal hands, small enough to avoid committing to a bad all‑in if he jams. If called I’ll reevaluate on the river (bet for value/bluff or fold to heavy action depending on the card).

On the turn, the Ten of Hearts (Th) is an extremely coordinated 'scare card.' It completes potential straights (K9, 98) and any heart flush that was drawing on the flop. While I have top pair with my Queen, my seven kicker provides no additional security. Minni's bet of 600 into a pot of 800 (75% pot) is a polarized sizing that typically represents either a very strong made hand (value) or a high-equity draw. Given that the board now contains three hearts and multiple straight possibilities, my hand has effectively been demoted to a mediocre bluff-catcher. In a heads-up match against a chip leader, it is important not to bleed chips in spots where the opponent's range has significantly improved relative to mine. I will fold and wait for a more favorable board texture.

I am on the button with Ace-four offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. My current stack is significantly smaller than Minni's, so I need to play assertively to rebuild. A standard opening raise to 300 is consistent with my previous play and allows me to take the initiative in the hand.

Heads-up in the big blind with K♥Q♥ — a very strong hand against a button open. Flash opened to 300 (3x), which is a wide/standard button raise; I want to take the initiative and charge weaker holdings while narrowing his calling range. I’ll 3-bet to 1,200 total (about 4x his open). That size extracts value from Ax/Kx/Qx and suited connectors, keeps plenty of room for postflop play, and doesn’t commit too much against his ~6,550 stack. If he 4-bets big or shoves I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold or call depending on sizing); if he calls I’ll continue with pressure on favorable flops.

I am currently the short stack with about 65 big blinds. I opened on the button with Ace-Four offsuit, and Minni has responded with a large 4x 3-bet to 1,200. While Ace-Four is a decent starting hand in heads-up play, calling such a large raise out of my remaining stack (about 18% of my total chips) creates a difficult post-flop scenario with a low stack-to-pot ratio. Minni has been very aggressive recently, frequently 3-betting my opens. While I could consider a 4-bet shove as a bluff given my Ace blocker, 65 big blinds is a bit too deep to shove with A4o comfortably against a player who has me significantly covered. I will fold and wait for a more favorable opportunity to take a stand or find a better hand to call or 4-bet with.

Heads-up on the button with K♦4♥ — a hand I want to play aggressively rather than limp. Pot is 150 and to call is 50, but taking the initiative with a standard 3x BB open (to 300 total) applies pressure to the shorter-stacked BB and lets me play the flop in position. The raise is small relative to my 33.4k stack and can pick up the blinds or isolate weaker ranges; if Flash 3-bets large or shoves, I can fold this marginal hand. If called I'll use position to continue or control pot size postflop.

I have Ace-Ten suited, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Minni has been raising frequently from the button. I should 3-bet here for value and to take back the initiative. A raise to 900 is a standard size that puts pressure on the opener's range while maintaining a healthy stack-to-pot ratio.

Flash has 3‑bet me to 900 after my 300 open. Calling 600 to win a 1,200 pot requires ~33% equity. K♦4♥ offsuit is frequently dominated by a standard 3‑bet range (lots of Aces, Kings and broadways) and won’t have the equity or post‑flop playability I’d want even in position. I could 4‑bet bluff to 1,500 using the King as a blocker, but the min 4‑bet is small relative to my stack and Flash can easily call or shove, putting me in an uncomfortable spot with a weak holding. I’m the chip leader and should wait for a better, higher‑expected‑value spot to apply pressure — folding preserves my stack and avoids a marginal confrontation.

I have K9o on the button in a heads-up situation. King-high hands are generally strong in this format, and K9o is well within the top 25% of starting hands. Given that I am short-stacked compared to Minni, I need to look for opportunities to build my stack. Raising to 300 is a standard open from the small blind to exert pressure and take control of the pot early.

Heads-up vs a standard 3x open from the button. With Ks4c in the big blind the hand is marginal but worth defending — K‑high will have decent equity against a wide raising range and I can realize equity cheaply. I don’t want to 3‑bet out of position with a weak kicker and risk getting isolated by stronger Kx/AK hands; folding is too tight here. I'll call the additional 200 (300 total committed) to see a flop, keep the pot manageable, and proceed based on the flop texture (raise/lead if I hit top pair or strong draw, fold or pot‑control if I miss and face aggression).

I flatted preflop to a wide button open and the flop came 4♠ J♣ 2♠. I have K♠4♣ — a pair of fours with a King kicker (bottom pair) and only a backdoor spade possibility. Against a standard button opening range this is a marginal but real made hand: ahead of many pure overcards and bluffs, behind most Jx, overpairs and many pocket pairs. Out of position I want to control the pot size and let the bettor in position take the lead. I will check here. Plan: if Flash continuation-bets a small-to-medium amount (typical c-bet), I will call to extract value and re-evaluate on the turn; if they fire big or shove (committing a large portion of their 6.6k stack) I will fold most of the time since my equity versus a value-heavy shove is poor and pot odds are not attractive. If Flash checks back, I can take a free card or fire a small turn bet for thin value depending on card texture.

On a dry flop like 4s-Jc-2s, as the pre-flop aggressor, I should follow through with a continuation bet. My King-high has some showdown value, but betting 300 (50% of the pot) allows me to take the pot down immediately if Minni has missed, while still leaving me room to navigate if called. Minni's check is standard for the big blind here.

I checked the flop and Flash c‑bet a small 300 into a 900 pot on a dry 4♠ J♣ 2♠ board. I hold bottom pair (4s) with a decent K kicker and only a very slim backdoor possibility. Against a wide heads‑up opening/c‑betting range this hand has >25% equity and the price to call is favorable (300 into a 1200 pot after my call -> ~25% break‑even). Raising OOP with a marginal bottom pair is speculative — I have little fold equity and will often be crushed by better Jx/overpairs and committed OOP. Plan: call to realize equity and extract value from worse hands, keep the pot manageable, and re‑evaluate on the turn (fold to a large shove or heavy action; call small/medium turns to see showdown).

Board 4♠ J♣ 2♠ 7♦ with K♠4♣: I have bottom pair (4s) with a decent K kicker. Flop I called a small 300 c-bet; the turn check from Flash looks like weakness or pot control. My backdoor spade draw is effectively dead and I have very limited fold equity OOP. Betting now would either fold out most worse hands (low EV) or induce a shove/call situation vs Jx/stronger pairs where I'm usually behind. With the pot at 1,200 and the opponent's 6,250 stack, I prefer to keep the pot small and take a free river. I'll check back the turn and re-evaluate the river — calling small river bets for thin value but folding to large shoves or heavy value lines.

On the turn, the board is 4s Jc 2s 7d. I currently hold King-high with K9o. Minni called my continuation bet on the flop, which indicates she likely has a piece of the board (such as a Jack, a small pair, or a spade flush draw) or an Ace-high hand. The 7d on the turn is relatively inconsequential. Since Minni checked to me again, I have the option to check behind and take a free card or see a cheap showdown. Given that I have some showdown value against her missed draws but likely won't fold out a Jack or a better pair with a single bet, checking is a prudent way to control the size of the pot and preserve my stack.

I hold K♠4♣ — bottom pair (4s) with a decent king kicker on a 4♠ J♣ 2♠ 7♦ Q♦ board. Flash opened preflop and fired a small flop c‑bet (I called); both players checked the turn, which looks like pot control or weakness. The river Q♦ does improve some of their value combos (Qx) but also doesn’t change the fact that my hand mainly beats missed Kx and pure bluffs while losing to Jx, Qx and most pocket pairs. Betting risks getting called by all better hands and folding out only a few marginal worse hands for small gain. Given the line, the texture and my lack of fold equity OOP, pot control is best — take the free showdown and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand. I will check to see the showdown.

On the river, the board is 4s-Jc-2s-7d-Qd. I have King-high (Kd 9h). Minni has checked for the third time this hand. After she called the flop C-bet and we both checked the turn, the Queen on the river is a card that could have potentially improved her range if she was floating with hands like AQ or KQ, though she might have 3-bet those pre-flop. Given her check, she likely has a weak pair (4x, 2x, 7x), a Jack, or a missed draw. While my King-high is weak, betting here as a bluff is risky because she has a massive stack and has shown a willingness to call down with marginal hands in previous rounds. Checking behind allows me to win against her missed spade draws and is the most prudent way to preserve my stack as the shorter player.

Heads-up on the button (acts last postflop) — K4o is marginal but has good playability in position. With a huge stack advantage over Flash I want the initiative and to deny him cheap flops, but I don’t want to commit too much with a dominated hand. A standard open to 2xBB (raise to 200 total) keeps the pot manageable, puts pressure on his wide defending range, and lets me c-bet favorable flops or fold to a strong 3-bet. If he 3-bets I’ll fold K4o; if he calls I’ll play aggressively in position. Raise to 200.

I am in the Big Blind with J3s. Minni, who has a significant chip lead, has min-raised to 200. The cost to call is only 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 pot odds. While J3s is a weak hand, in a heads-up scenario, it has sufficient equity and playability to defend against a min-raise. Folding would be overly tight given the price, and 3-betting is unnecessary given our stack depths and the weakness of the hand. I will call and see a flop.

I have flopped bottom two pair (Jacks and Threes) on a very coordinated monotone board. While two pair is a strong hand heads-up, the presence of three clubs means any single club in my opponent's hand has significant equity, and any completed flush currently beats me. Since Minni was the pre-flop aggressor, I will check to her to see how she interacts with this texture. This allows me to control the pot size and decide how to proceed based on her betting action.

Flop: Kc 3c Jc — I have top pair (Kd) but a very weak 4-kicker and the board is extremely wet (three clubs). Flash checked, and his preflop calling range will include a lot of club cards, Jx, and weaker Kings. I want to get value from worse Kx/Jx hands and charge club draws while keeping the pot manageable given his shorter stack. A continuation bet of ~300 (about 3/4 pot) accomplishes protection and value extraction without committing my deep stack. If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I re-evaluate on the turn (be cautious if a club or a Jack/King arrives). If he raises large I’ll give strong consideration to folding — I’m not forced to gamble a big pot with a marginal kicker against a likely flush or better Kx.

I have bottom two pair (Jacks and Threes) on a monotone board. This is a strong hand heads-up, but the three clubs are very concerning as they make any club in the opponent's hand a significant draw and any two clubs a completed flush. Minni has bet 300 into a pot of 400. While I could raise for value/protection against a lone club, calling is more disciplined here. It allows me to keep the pot manageable, see a turn card, and potentially catch a bluff or a worse hand, while minimizing losses if she already has the flush or a higher two pair like KJ.

On the turn, the board pairs the three, giving me a Full House (Threes full of Jacks). This is an incredibly strong hand, especially in a heads-up match. The board is still very coordinated with three clubs, meaning my opponent could easily have a flush or a King that she will continue to bet for value. Since I checked and called on the flop, checking again on the turn is the most deceptive play. It allows Minni to continue her aggression with bluffs, made flushes, or hands like K-X, which I can then either call to keep her bluffs in or check-raise for value. Given her significant chip lead and history of aggressive betting, I expect her to lead out here frequently.

Heads‑up on the button with Kd4c on Kc 3c Jc 3d. I have top pair but a very weak kicker and the board is club‑heavy; Flash’s call on the flop represents a wide range that includes one‑club draws, Jx, weaker Kx and occasional small pairs (and rarely a 3). The turn (3d) is not a club or a King/Jack, so it hasn’t given big additional equity to his calling range, but it did add the possibility of trips if he held a 3 (low frequency). I want to charge club draws and extract value from worse Kx/Jx while keeping the pot controlled given his short stack. A substantial turn bet (~3/4 pot) accomplishes this: it denies correct odds to most river draws, gets value from worse pairs, and still gives me a clear decision if he jams. If Flash jams for his ~5750, my extra call (5750 - my bet) would be about 5000 and the total pot would be ~12500, so I need roughly 40% equity to call — against a shove range full of draws and worse K/J hands I comfortably have that equity, so I’m prepared to call a jam. I’ll bet to charge and extract thin value rather than check and give a free river.

On the turn, the board pairs the three, giving me a Full House (Threes full of Jacks). This is an exceptionally strong hand, especially in heads-up play. The board currently shows three clubs, which makes it highly likely that Minni, given her aggressive betting, has either a flush, a strong King, or is semi-bluffing. While checking and calling the turn is a deceptive option to keep her bluffs in, the board is quite 'wet' and several river cards could kill the action or make it harder to get paid (like a fourth club or an Ace). By raising now, I can extract value from her made flushes and Kings. Since I have a relatively small stack compared to hers, I want to build the pot now to ensure I can get my remaining chips in on the river. I will raise to 2250, which is a strong value raise that forces her to commit more with the second-best hands.

I call. I have top pair (Kd) but a very weak 4‑kicker on an extremely club‑heavy board (Kc 3c Jc 3d). Flash’s big check‑raise to 2250 on the turn is polarized — it can be the nuts (flopped two‑club flushes, sets of 3s) or strong semi‑bluffs/single‑club draws and worse K/J hands. Folding is too nitty given I beat a lot of his check‑raise bluffs/draws; jamming/raising myself bloats the pot and forces a cooler scenario against the nut portion of his range. Pot = 4000; I must add 1500 now (2250 total committed on the turn) which gives me ~27% immediate pot‑odds to see the river; if he shoves his remaining 1250 after my call my total risk would be 2750 to win ~8000 (break‑even ~34.4%). Versus his likely polarized raise range I expect enough equity to justify calling and I’ll re‑evaluate only if the river changes texture or he shoves (in which case I’m prepared to call).

I have a Full House (Threes full of Jacks) on a board of Kc-3c-Jc-3d-Th. My turn check-raise to 2250 was called by Minni, which strongly suggests she has a high-value hand like a Flush, a strong King, or potentially a smaller three-of-a-kind. With the pot at 5500 and only 3500 remaining in my stack, the most effective move to maximize value is to move all-in. Minni's large stack and past behavior indicate she is likely to call with a wide range of second-best hands, such as flushes or Kings, and checking risks her checking behind with those same hands. I am only beaten by K3, TT, or the unlikely KK/JJ, making this a clear value shove.

Call 3500. Pot is 9000 and Flash shoved/bet 3500 on the river; calling 3500 would make the final pot 16,000, so I only need ~21.9% equity (3500/16000) to break even. I have top pair (Kd) with a very weak 4-kicker on a three-club, very wet board — the line (check-call flop, large check-raise on turn, all-in/river bet) is polarized toward nut flushes and trips but will also contain Jx/Kx and some bluffs/semi-bluffs. Given Flash’s shown aggression in this match and the relatively cheap price to call, my hand retains enough showdown value vs his non-flush portion (worse K’s, Jx, missed draws and bluffs) to justify the call. Raising would be irresponsible with a weak kicker and folding is too nitty against a polarized, aggressive line when pot odds are favorable. Therefore I call the 3500 to reach showdown.

I am on the button with K2s. This is a standard opening hand in heads-up play, especially given the stack depths and my need to accumulate chips to catch up to Minni. While Minni has been quite aggressive recently (winning a large pot in Hand #118), opening for a standard 3x raise maintains pressure and allows me to play the hand in position.

I have K♠2♦ in the big blind facing a button open to 300. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 more to call (total 300). Heads-up the button's opening range is wide — Flash has been opening to 300 frequently — so K2o has reasonable equity vs that wide range. The pot odds are ~2:1 (need ~33% equity), and versus a typical wide HU open my hand will often clear that threshold. 

I don't want to inflate the pot out of position with a marginal hand by making a larger 3-bet. A minimum 3-bet to 500 is too small to credibly fold out a lot of his range and a bigger 3-bet would commit me with a weak holding. Folding is too tight here. So I will call, keep the pot manageable, and play the flop (look to pot-control, realize equity, or fold to heavy aggression on unfavorable textures).

Out of position (BB) on a J♦-4♦-7♠ flop with K♠2♦ — I have no pair and only a very weak backdoor diamond (needs both turn and river). Flash opened wide preflop so his checking range can include lots of hands that beat K2 (Jx, Ax, Kx, mid pairs) or decent draw/air, but I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with a marginal holding. Pot-control and realization of equity is the right plan: check and fold to significant aggression, call small bets cheaply to see a turn, and only invest more if I improve or pick up clear fold equity.

On a dry board like 4d-7s-Jd, as the pre-flop aggressor, I have the range advantage. A continuation bet (C-bet) of 50% of the pot is standard here to put pressure on Minni's wide defending range from the big blind. While I only have King-high, this bet can often take down the pot immediately if she missed the board, which is likely for a significant portion of her range. If called, I still have two overcards to the 4 and 7 and a backdoor flush draw to provide some equity on the turn.

Call 300. Flash has been opening wide and c‑betting small; I only need ~20% equity here (call 300 to win ~1500). K♠2♦ has an overcard (K) and a small backdoor diamond possibility and will have reasonable equity vs a wide HU c‑bet range that contains a lot of air and worse holdings. I want to keep the pot controlled out of position with a marginal hand — folding is too tight given the price, and raising OOP with K2o is spewy and often dominated. Plan: call to see a turn, re‑evaluate on the next street — fold to significant raise/shove if I miss, but continue or extract value if I pair my K or pick up a meaningful draw.

Heads‑up vs Flash (SB). Preflop he opened to 300 and I defended the BB; he c‑bet the flop (~300) and I called. Turn is 4♥, pairing the board, and he checks to me. I hold K♠2♦ and currently have no pair; my backdoor diamond flush route from the flop is now dead (I needed both turn and river diamonds). I only have 6 clean outs (3 Ks + 3 2s) to improve on the river — roughly ~13% equity — so I have very little equity to continue as a bluff. The turn pairing also increases the chance he has a 4x or a slow‑played Jx; betting out of position with K2 is spewy and will often be called or check‑raised by better hands. I will check to keep the pot controlled, avoid committing chips with a marginal hand, and preserve the option to fold to big river aggression or extract value if I actually pair on the river.

The turn card is the four of hearts, pairing the bottom card of the flop. This card is generally better for Minni's defending range, as she could easily have combinations of 4x in the big blind. I have King-high with no draws. While I could continue to represent a strong hand, Minni has shown stickiness in previous hands. Checking back allows me to control the size of the pot, realize my showdown value if the river is a King, or potentially win against her missed draws if the hand goes to showdown cheaply. Given the stack disadvantage, I want to avoid being check-raised on this board texture.

I have K♠2♦ and completely missed the river. After calling his flop c‑bet and checking the turn, Flash checked the 9♦ river — which actually completes a lot of diamond combos (4♦, J♦, 9♦ on board) and leaves my hand with no showdown value. Betting as a bluff here is too risky: many Jx/Kx, any 4x (trips) or hands containing diamonds now call or beat me, and the pot (1,200) is small relative to the stacks so I don’t need to manufacture action. Pot control and mine‑and‑fold is the best line — check and fold to a bet. I’ll check and preserve my stack rather than bluff into a likely calling range.

On a river board of 4d-7s-Jd-4h-9d, I hold King-high. The 9d completes a potential diamond flush and several straight draws. Minni has checked the river after checking the turn, which indicates weakness or a marginal hand intended for showdown. While I have very little showdown value with King-high, betting here would likely only be called by better hands (a Jack, a 7, or a 9) and wouldn't force a fold from a strong range. Checking back is the most prudent play to see the showdown cheaply and preserve my stack, as King-high occasionally wins against missed straight draws like 56 or 68 in heads-up play.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening very wide — QcTs is a strong, playable hand in this spot. I have position and a large stack (27,450) versus Flash’s 12,400, so I want the initiative and to isolate. A standard 2x BB open to 200 is appropriate: it builds the pot, applies pressure to the BB’s wide defending range, and keeps the pot manageable postflop. Postflop plan: c-bet most dry boards for fold equity and value, slow-play or check behind when I hit marginally (top pair) against heavy resistance, and call small 3-bets to play postflop while folding or re-evaluating against very large 3-bets that commit too much of the effective stacks.

I am holding 7d 3c in the Big Blind. This is one of the weakest hands in poker. Minni has raised to 200 from the Button/Small Blind. While it only costs me 100 more to call with pot odds of 3:1, 73o is extremely difficult to play out of position and rarely connects with the board in a way that allows me to win a significant pot. Given that Minni has a significant chip lead and has been playing aggressively, I should conserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity to defend or counter-attack rather than bleeding chips with garbage hands.

I am on the button with J4s. This is a reasonable hand to open with in a heads-up situation, although it is on the lower end of the range. Given the current stack sizes and the tournament progress, a standard 3x raise is appropriate to apply pressure on Minni. If she calls, I have position and a hand that can flop well. Raising to 300 total is a standard play here.

Facing a standard 3x open to 300 from the button with 5♣4♥ in the big blind. I already have 100 posted and need to call 200 more (300 total) — pot odds ≈ 3:1 (~33%), which is acceptable against a wide heads‑up opening range. 5‑4 off is a playable, connected hand with decent equity and postflop playability; a light 3‑bet OOP with this holding is suboptimal (small raise sizes won’t fold enough hands and I’d be out of position). I’ll call to see a flop, keep the pot controlled, and fold to heavy aggression if I miss, or press when I hit draws/pairs.

Check. 5♣4♥ on 6♥2♥7♣ is a clean open‑ender (3s or 8s = 8 outs, ~31% to hit by the river) with a tiny backdoor heart possibility. Out of position I want to keep the pot small and realize equity rather than barreling; a lead or a light raise here is a poor use of chips against a wide but aggressive button. Plan: check and call a normal c‑bet — break‑evens: 300→~25%, 400→~28.6%, 500→~31.25% — so I’ll call small/medium bets up to roughly 500 and fold to much larger pressure or raises. If he checks back I take a free card and proceed depending on turn improvement (value/raise when I hit, fold or pot‑control when I don’t).

On a low board of 6h-2h-7c, I have two overcards with Jack-Four of spades. Although I missed the flop, as the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard to try and take down the pot immediately, especially since many of Minni's calling hands (high cards like KQ, AT) will have also missed this board. A bet of 300 (half the pot) is a balanced sizing that applies pressure without overcommitting chips against a significantly larger stack.

I have 5♣4♥ on 6♥2♥7♣ — an open‑ended straight draw (8 outs) plus a tiny backdoor heart possibility. Pot is 900 and Flash bets 300; calling 300 to win ~1200 gives ~25% pot odds. My equity to the river with the OESD is ~31% (plus ~4% backdoor flush), so a call is +EV. Flash is a frequent/button aggressor and this small c‑bet represents a wide range, so I’ll realize equity and keep the pot controlled. Plan: call now, evaluate the turn — fold to heavy continued pressure if I miss, but if I hit my straight (or a strong pair/draw improvement) look to extract value or raise depending on his sizing and tendencies.

Call 300. I have 5♣4♥ on 6♥2♥7♣10♥ — an open‑ended straight draw (3s/8s) plus a backdoor→now near‑flush draw (I hold 4♥ while the board has 6♥2♥10♥). Unique outs ≈ 15, so my equity to improve on the river is roughly ~32–33%. The turn bet is small (300 into the ~900 pot), so calling 300 to potentially win ~1500 gives me excellent pot odds (break‑even ≈20%). Flash is an aggressive, wide button player, so this is a clear +EV call to realize equity and keep the pot controlled; raising OOP as a semi‑bluff with only draw equity is suboptimal. Plan: call and see the river — if I complete a straight/flush I will look to extract value; if I miss and face heavy river aggression I’ll fold unless river odds/implied odds justify a call.

On this turn (Ten of Hearts), the board has become more coordinated with potential flushes and straights. After my flop continuation bet was called, Minni's check on the turn presents an opportunity to check back. I have Jack-high with no draws, and given Minni's significant chip lead and tendency to call, a second barrel is unlikely to succeed without more equity. Checking back allows me to see the river for free and control the size of the pot.

I just completed a heart flush on the river (A♥,10♥,6♥,4♥,2♥ using my 4♥). It’s a weak flush — the board gives A,10,6 as the top hearts so any opponent heart higher than 4 (K♥,Q♥,J♥,9♥,8♥,7♥,5♥) will beat me. Flash is a wide, aggressive opener who c‑bet the flop and then slowed on the turn, so his range contains a lot of hearts and hands that still beat my weak flush. With the pot only 1,200 and no bet to call, the safest +EV line is to check and induce: I can capture value from worse A/x or single pair hands that will bluff or call small bets, and I avoid bloating the pot and getting raised by dominating heart combos. If Flash fires a small river bet I can call for value; if he jams big I should strongly consider folding (he likely has a higher heart). Therefore: check now.

On this river, the board shows four hearts, including the Ace of Hearts. I have Jack-high with no hearts, meaning I have zero showdown value. While Minni checked, indicating she might not have a flush, she is a loose-calling player who has a significant chip lead. Attempting a bluff into a four-flush board against such an opponent is high-risk, as she likely calls with any heart or even a mid-pair. Checking back is the most prudent play to preserve my stack, conceding the pot when I have no realistic chance of winning at showdown or through a bluff.

Heads-up on the button with A♠ J♦ — a very strong opening hand in this spot. I want the initiative and to avoid playing a marginal pot out of position, so I’ll raise rather than just call the extra 50. I choose a standard 3xBB-sized open to 300 (total committed preflop), which is big enough to gain fold equity and get value from worse Aces/broadways but not so large that I’m overly committed if Flash 3-bets. If Flash 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and his tendencies; if he calls I’ll play the flop on the button with position and a high-card Ace.

In a heads-up situation, Jh 6h is a solid hand to defend the Big Blind against a standard 3x raise from the button. With 2:1 pot odds on the call and a suited hand that has decent playability and high-card value compared to a typical button range, calling is the most standard and effective play here. This allows me to see a flop and realize my equity without over-committing against a larger stack.

On a paired board of Kc-Td-Ks, my hand (Jh 6h) has very little showdown value but does possess a backdoor flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen). As the player out of position against a frequent pre-flop raiser like Minni, the standard and most prudent play is to check. This allows me to gauge Minni's interest in the pot and decide whether to continue based on the size of a potential continuation bet. Leading into the pre-flop aggressor on this specific board texture would likely be met with a raise or a call from a range that currently crushes mine.

I missed this flop. Kc Td Ks leaves me with only Ace-high (A♠J♦) and a board that heavily favors a lot of Flash’s calling range (Kx, TT, other pocket pairs). Flash checked, which can be weakness or a disguised strong hand — either way many hands that beat me will not fold to a c-bet and a bet here simply bloats the pot and can get me raised by hands I can’t continue against. I have position and a large stack advantage, so pot control and taking a free card is the prudent line. Plan: check and reevaluate on the turn — if Flash checks again I can take another free card or make a controlled bet on a safe turn; if he leads I’ll judge by sizing (likely fold to a large bet, consider calling small to see a card, and only continue aggressively if I pair up or improve). For now: check to keep the pot manageable and avoid being bluff-raised out of the hand.

Minni checked back on a flop of Kc-Td-Ks, which suggests she doesn't have a King and likely has a marginal hand or air. My J-high has no value and no real draws on this board. Since I'm out of position, checking again is the most prudent move. It allows me to see the river for free if she checks back again, or fold cheaply if she decides to bet. There's no reason to turn this into a bluff yet, as the board is very static and she might call with any Ten or mid-pair.

I have A♠J♦ on K♣ T♦ K♠ 4♦. I completely missed — no pair and no realistic draw — and the board with two Kings heavily favors Flash’s calling range (any K for trips, TT, many pocket pairs, AT/KT/etc.). Flash checked flop and turn, which can be either a slow-play or a cautious/value line, but given his sticky calling tendencies from earlier hands a bet here will mostly get called by better hands or induce a raise I can’t profitably continue against. I have position and a large stack advantage, so pot control is the prudent line: take a free river card, keep the pot small, and re-evaluate on the river. If Flash leads on the river I’ll judge by sizing (fold to a large shove, call small/medium bets to see a showdown or when I pair up); if the river gives me a pair (A or J) I’ll seek value. For now: check.

On a board of Kc-Td-Ks-4d-8d, my Jack-high holds almost no showdown value. However, Minni's decision to check both the flop and the turn indicates significant weakness or a marginal hand that is content to see a cheap showdown. Since I am out of position and the pot is relatively small, I will check. If Minni checks back, I hope my Jack-high is somehow good against total air, though it's unlikely. If she bets, I will fold, as I have no reason to bluff-catch with this hand.

Board Kc Td Ks 4d 8d, I hold A♠ J♦ — no pair, only Ace‑high and one diamond (no flush). Flash checked flop and turn and checked the river as well. Given the paired kings on board and the river completing a diamond draw, many hands in his calling range (Kx, pocket pairs like TT, 44, 88, even sets/full houses/quads) will beat me and will not fold to a river bluff; a bet here has very little fold equity and is likely to either get called by better or induce a raise I can’t profitably continue against. Pot is small (600) relative to both stacks, so pot control and risk management favor taking the free showdown rather than bluffing thin. I check to go to showdown and preserve my stack; if he had bet a small amount I would consider a call depending on sizing and read, but with no bet the correct line is to check.

I am on the button with 9s 2d, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. While I have been aggressive from the button in previous hands, 92o has very little post-flop playability and equity. Given the significant stack disadvantage against Minni, I should avoid wasting chips on hands this weak and wait for a better spot to apply pressure or value bet.

Heads-up on the button I should be wide and aggressive — I have positional advantage and both stacks are deep, so stealing the BB is profitable. 6♠4♣ is marginal but fine to open here; I’ll raise to the minimum allowed (200 total = 2bb) to get fold equity and keep the pot manageable if called. Postflop I’ll c-bet favorable, low/uncoordinated flops and check/control against scary boards. If Flash 3-bets to a meaningful size I’ll fold this hand rather than bloat the pot with a weak holding.

I am in the Big Blind with 5c 7d, a very weak offsuit hand. Minni has raised to 200 from the button. While the pot odds are 3:1 (requiring 100 more to call a 300 pot), 57o is difficult to play out of position and has poor equity realization. Given that I am currently trailing in chips and approaching the final hands of the tournament, I prefer to wait for a better spot rather than defending with such a marginal hand.

I am on the button with T2o. In a heads-up situation, T2o is a weak hand but often playable from the button to keep pressure. However, looking at the recent history, Minni has been quite active and has a significant chip lead. Folding is a very safe option to wait for a better spot, but a standard 3x raise is the usual aggressive play here to steal the blinds. Given the stack depths and the hand strength, folding is better than calling, but raising is better if I want to stay aggressive. However, with T2o, it's a clear fold against an opponent who has been calling or re-raising frequently.

Heads-up on the button (SB) I should be opening a very wide range to seize initiative and steal the BB. 2♣4♠ is a weak holding but fine to raise from the button — a min-raise to 200 achieves fold equity while keeping the pot small relative to both stacks. I’ll raise to 200 to take the lead; if Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this hand, and if called I’ll proceed cautiously postflop and c-bet only favorable boards or with appropriate equity.

I am holding Ace-Queen Offsuit, which is a premium hand in a heads-up situation. Minni has been very active from the button, and my hand is well ahead of her standard opening range. I will 3-bet here to take the initiative and build a pot with a superior hand. A raise to 700 (3.5x her open) is a standard sizing to put pressure on her wider range while extracting value.

I opened wide from the button with 2♣4♠ to seize initiative, but Flash’s 3‑bet to 700 is a clear sign of strength or a polarised 3‑betting range. This hand has almost no showdown or bluff equity vs that range, is frequently dominated, and will play terribly out of position. Calling 500 for ~36% break‑even equity isn’t justified and a 4‑bet bluff here would be too risky and thin. I’ll fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with 6s 3s. In heads-up play, the button has position on all post-flop streets. While 63s is a weak hand, it has some potential for flushes and straights. Completing the small blind for an additional 50 chips is a low-cost way to see a flop in position, especially given the current stack depths.

Heads-up, I’m in the big blind with A♠Q♠ — a premium heads-up hand. Flash (SB) merely completed to the big blind, so his preflop range is wide (suited connectors, broadway, small pairs, some Axs/Kxs). I want the initiative and to isolate him with a hand that dominates a lot of that range. A standard 3x-ish raise is appropriate: it builds the pot for value, pressures his weakest holdings, and keeps the pot size manageable given both stacks (I have 28,400 vs his 11,400). Plan: if called I’ll c-bet favorable flops and re-evaluate against aggression; if he 3-bets big I’ll re-assess based on sizing and his tendencies. Raise to 300 total to open the hand aggressively but prudently.

I am in position with a speculative hand (6s 3s). Minni raised to 300 after I completed the small blind. I need to put in 200 more to see a flop in a 400-chip pot, giving me 2:1 immediate pot odds. Given that I have position for the remainder of the hand and 63s has some connectivity and flush potential, I will call to see a flop and look for opportunities to outplay the opponent or hit a strong draw.

Heads‑up I was the preflop raiser with A♠Q♠ and this flop (2♦ J♣ 3♣) misses a big portion of Villain’s calling range (lots of Axs/Kxs/suited connectors and small pairs). As the aggressor I should take the initiative: deny equity to club draws, fold out weaker unpaired hands, and extract thin value from worse A/K/Qx and small pairs. The board is relatively dry except for clubs, so a standard ~½ pot continuation bet is the right play. I’ll bet 300. If he raises big I’ll give it up (he’s likely strong: Jx/sets/strong club combos); if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and barrel selectively on safe cards or control the pot against resistance.

I have bottom pair (threes) with a 6 kicker on a J-3-2 rainbow board. In heads-up play, any pair holds significant value, especially in position. Minni's bet of 300 into a 600-chip pot is a standard continuation bet that she could be making with a wide range of hands, including many that I currently beat or have equity against. With a 3:1 price on the call and the advantage of being in position for the rest of the hand, I will call to see how the turn develops.

I’m the preflop raiser with A♠Q♠ and c‑bet the dry flop (2♦ J♣ 3♣); villain just called. The turn 5♠ is a relatively harmless card — it doesn’t complete the club backdoor and my range is still strong (lots of A‑x/broadways), so I should keep the initiative. I want to extract thin value from worse A/K/Qx and small pairs and deny equity to any lingering backdoors, while keeping the pot controlled versus resistance. I’ll continuation‑bet roughly half the pot (600 into 1200). If he folds I take it down; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate the river and give credit to big check‑raises (Jx/sets/club made hands) and proceed cautiously versus clear strength.

On the turn, the board is 2d-Jc-3c-5s. I hold 6s 3s for bottom pair (threes) and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4). Minni bets 600 into a 1200 pot, offering me 3:1 odds to call. In heads-up play, holding any pair plus a draw while in position is generally strong enough to continue, especially given the pot odds and the potential to improve to a straight or two pair on the river. I will call to see the final card.

I opened with A♠Q♠ and c‑bet the dry flop then barreled the turn; Flash called both streets which strongly weights his range to at least a pair (Jx, small/medium pocket pairs), some club draws and the occasional slow‑played set. The river pairs the deuce (2s) which actually helps any 2x holdings (and can give trips) and does nothing for my Ace‑high. I have very little showdown value and, given how sticky he’s been, my bluff has low fold equity — a river stab will get called too often by better and simply loses to the bulk of his calling range. With a 2400 pot and healthy stacks behind, the lowest‑variance +EV play is to check and either get to showdown against bluffs/missed draws or fold if he leads big. I check.

I have bottom pair (threes) on a board that paired the deuces on the river. My final hand is two pair, threes and deuces, with a Jack kicker. Minni led pre-flop, on the flop, and on the turn, but checked the river. This check often indicates a hand that has some showdown value but isn't strong enough to bet for value a third time, or a total air hand that is giving up. While my hand is weak, it beats all missed club draws and many high-card hands (like AK, AQ, or KQ) which Minni might have been aggressive with. There is little value in betting as a better hand (like a Jack, a 5, or a higher pocket pair) is unlikely to fold, and a worse hand is unlikely to call. Therefore, checking back to realize my showdown equity is the most prudent play.

Heads-up on the button with K♦7♦ — a playable, suited king that does well in position. Limping is too passive in heads-up; I want to take the initiative, build the pot when I'm ahead of BB's calling range and get fold equity. A standard open to 3x the big blind (to 300 total) balances value and pressure without overcommitting the stack. If Flash 3-bets big I can fold this marginal holding; if he calls I play postflop in position to probe and extract value or take the pot away.

I am holding Queen-Deuce offsuit in the Big Blind. Minni has raised from the Button, which is a standard move for her, but my hand is quite weak to defend out of position. With a stack of 126 big blinds, there is no need to commit chips with such a marginal hand that performs poorly post-flop and lacks high-card strength or connectivity. I will fold and wait for a more favorable opportunity to engage.

In a heads-up situation, Td 5d is a reasonable hand to open from the Button/Small Blind. It has some flush and straight potential. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure and take the initiative in the hand, aiming to pick up the blinds or play a pot with a range advantage.

Heads-up against Flash (button) who opens to 300 — he’s been opening wide and playing aggressively. I’m on the big blind with K♠5♣: a marginal but defensible hand heads-up. Calling 200 keeps the pot manageable, realizes equity against a wide opening range, and lets me see a flop cheaply rather than bluff‑3‑betting out of position with a thin hand. I’ll proceed cautiously postflop: continue if I spike a King/5 or get good draw equity, fold to heavy aggression on dry Ace/King boards where I’m likely dominated. A min 3‑bet to 500 would be too weak and a larger 3‑bet is too thin with K5o, so call and evaluate the flop.

Heads‑up against an aggressive, wide opener on the button. K♠5♣ on J♦7♣6♥ is a missed hand: one overcard (K), no pair, only weak backdoor club/runner straight possibilities. I want pot control and to let the preflop aggressor continue — checking keeps options open. Plan: check and fold to heavy aggression unless it’s a small c‑bet I can reasonably float (call small bets to see a turn with the nut overcard), but if the turn pairs my K or 5 I’ll take a more aggressive line. Checking preserves stack and avoids getting barreled off with very little equity.

As the pre-flop aggressor, I'm making a standard continuation bet on this flop after Minni checked. The board of 7c-Jd-6h is somewhat coordinated, but I have a backdoor flush draw and an overcard to the 6 and 7. Betting half the pot (300) allows me to maintain initiative and potentially fold out Minni's air or weak hands.

Heads‑up vs an aggressive, wide opener on the button. I checked the flop with K♠5♣ to keep the pot small and see a turn — this is a missed hand but the flop bet is small (300 into 900, ~1/3 pot). Calling is the best line: the price is reasonable (≈3:1 on the call) against a wide range, lets me realize equity and possibly float the turn, and preserves pot control. Raising would be a thin, high‑variance play with little fold equity vs this opponent; folding would be too tight given the cheap price. I will call and re‑evaluate on the turn (look to fold to big barrels unless I pair or pick up meaningful equity).

I never improved on the turn (K♠5♣ on J♦7♣6♥4♠) and Flash has been opening/playing aggressively and c‑betting wide. With no pair and almost no draw, I have minimal showdown value and very little fold equity if I bet. Pot control is the priority — check back, see a river, and only invest more if I actually pair or pick up real equity. If Flash fires a large barrel on the river I’ll fold most of the time; if he checks again I can show down K‑high or consider a small bluff on a favourable river. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot.

I have an open-ended straight draw with my 5 connecting with the 4, 6, and 7 on the board. After Minni called my flop continuation bet and checked the turn, I have the option to semi-bluff or check behind. Given that Minni was sticky on the flop, she likely has a piece of the board (a Jack, 7, or 6) and is unlikely to fold to a single turn bet. By checking back, I ensure I see the river for free and can realize my equity. If an 8 or 3 lands, I can bet for value; otherwise, I can evaluate the river based on her action and the final card.

No improvement on the river — K♠5♣ on 7♣ J♦ 6♥ 4♠ 9♦ is just K‑high. I checked the turn to keep the pot small and didn’t pick up any real equity on the river, so there’s no value bet to be had and a bluff here is thin: Flash opened wide and is capable of folding some missed hands, but he also calls down light and checked the turn (showing pot‑control or a real hand). With 26.8k behind and only 1.2k in the pot, preserving the stack and taking the showdown is lower variance and higher EV than firing a marginal river bluff. I’ll check and show down K‑high; if Flash bets a meaningful size I’ll fold most of the time (call only very small bluffs).

On the river, the board is 7c-Jd-6h-4s-9d. I have Ten-high and almost certainly no showdown value. Minni has checked to me three times. While this might look like weakness, her check-call on the flop suggests she has a piece of the board, such as a Jack, a 7, a 6, or a straight draw. The 9 on the river likely improved some of her range (like 8-9 or 9-10). Given her sticky nature in previous hands, it is unlikely she will fold a pair to a single river bet. I will check behind and concede the pot rather than risking more chips on a low-frequency bluff.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with K5o — marginal but perfectly playable in this spot. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call, but opening to the min-raise (to 200 total) is standard: it uses position, gains fold equity, and defines the hand while keeping the pot manageable. Flash has shown preflop aggression but will also defend wide; I’ll raise to 200 to steal the blind and take the initiative. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, call smaller defenses and c-bet favorable flops or control the pot when I miss.

I am dealt pocket fives in the big blind. In a heads-up match, any pocket pair is a strong hand. Minni has been opening a wide range of hands from the button, often with a min-raise. While calling is an option to keep the pot controlled, raising (3-betting) allows me to take the initiative, define Minni's range, and potentially take the pot down immediately. If she calls, I have a solid hand that can flop a set or remain best on low-card boards. I will raise to 600, which is a standard 3-bet size against a 2x open.

Facing a standard heads-up 3-bet to 600 after I opened to 200. The pot is 800 and I need to add 400 more (600 total committed). Heads-up ranges are wide and Flash is an aggressive preflop player, plus I have position — K5o is marginal but playable in position against a wide 3-bet. Calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity and use positional advantage to c‑bet favorable flops (or check/control when I miss). If Flash responds with a large 4‑bet/all‑in I’ll fold this hand. Decision: call and see a flop.

On a board of As-Qh-9h, my pocket fives are in a difficult spot. This texture heavily favors the ranges that typically call a 3-bet, as it includes many Aces, Queens, and heart draws. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor, I could consider a continuation bet, but given the coordination of this board and the fact that most of Minni's calling range connects with it, a check is more prudent. This allows me to see how she reacts without committing more chips to a pot where I am likely behind. If she checks back, I get a free card; if she bets, I can evaluate, but I am leaning towards a fold if faced with significant aggression.

Pot is 1,200 and Flash (the preflop 3-bettor) checked to me on A♠ Q♥ 9♥. I have K♦5♣ — no pair and no hearts, but I’m in position and have a big stack advantage. Versus a fairly wide 3-bet range, a small continuation bet will fold plenty of missed hands (small pairs, Kx, floats) and wins the pot often. I’ll make a low-frequency, low-variance bluff: a ~1/3-pot c-bet (400). This applies pressure while keeping the pot manageable if called. If he folds I pick up the pot; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously (check/control or fold to heavy action unless I improve).

On a board of As-Qh-9h, my pocket fives are extremely vulnerable. This flop contains two overcards to my pair and a flush draw, which aligns very well with the range of hands Minni would use to call a pre-flop 3-bet (such as suited broadways, suited connectors, or Ax hands). Minni has bet 400 into a 1200 pot. While the pot odds are 4:1, the equity of my hand is very low against her likely range. If she has any Ace, Queen, or Nine, I am drawing to only two outs. Even against her semi-bluffs (like heart draws), I am often a coin-flip or worse. Given that I am out of position and the board is highly coordinated, it is unlikely I can profitably reach a showdown. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

In a heads-up situation, 9-6 offsuit is a marginal hand. While standard strategy often dictates playing a wide range from the button, Minni has shown significant aggression and a large chip lead. Given my current stack size and the desire to avoid unnecessary confrontations with sub-par hands, I will fold this hand and wait for a better opportunity to contest a pot in position.

Heads‑up on the button with Q2o — a weak hand but playable in position. I want to open a wide range from the button to take the initiative and pick up the blind rather than play passively. Flash (BB) has a shorter stack, and a standard 3x open to 300 is common at this table and will fold a lot of worse holdings or let me play post‑flop on my terms. If he calls I can c-bet favorable flops or proceed cautiously on dangerous textures; if he 3-bets large I'll fold this hand. Raising to 300 balances aggression and stack preservation and maximizes expected value here.

A-9o is a strong hand in heads-up play, well ahead of the typical button raising range. While calling is an option, I prefer to 3-bet to 900 for value and to take the initiative, especially since I've had success with this sizing in previous hands (like Hand #116).

Heads‑up on the button with Q♦2♥ facing a standard 3‑bet to 900 after my 300 open. Q2o is marginal, but in heads‑up position it’s worth seeing a flop — I keep initiative and use positional advantage to play post‑flop. A 4‑bet bluff to 1500 is too thin here (I don’t have the right blockers and it bloats the pot out of position against a shorter effective stack). Folding is overly nitty and gives up equity for no good reason.
