Calling 600 more (900 total) is a small portion of my stack, preserves maneuvering room, and gives me reasonable pot odds and post‑flop options. Plan: play the flop in position, c‑bet favorable textures, continue with top pair or meaningful draws, and fold to heavy action when completely missed.

I have top pair (Aces) on the flop. As the pre-flop aggressor who 3-bet, I have a significant range advantage on this Ace-high board. I will lead with a standard 1/3 pot continuation bet of 600 to extract value from Jack-x, Eight-x, or mid-pocket pairs while maintaining control of the hand.

Heads‑up on the button with Q♦2♥ — I completely missed this flop (A♦ J♠ 8♥) and only have a weak pair-outside chance and a single heart backdoor. Flash 3‑bet preflop, so his range is weighted toward A‑x, broadways and strong hands, but his flop sizing is very small (600 into a 2400 pot), which can be a wide probe or a thin c‑bet. Folding here is too nitty given the cheap price to see another card (calling 600 to potentially win ~3000 needs only ~16–17% equity). A raise/bluff is too risky: I don’t block Aces or Jacks and could easily get raised/shoved or called by many hands that dominate me. Best line is to call and use position — see a turn, and then either take it down if he shows weakness or fold to significant continued aggression unless I improve (Q/2/heart or favorable running cards). Call 600.

I have improved to top two pair (Aces and Nines) on the turn. While the 9d completes some potential straights (QT, T7) and opens a diamond draw, my hand remains extremely strong in a heads-up 3-bet pot. I will bet 1800 to extract value from remaining Ace-x, Jack-x, or flush and straight draws, maintaining my initiative.

Fold. Flash’s line is textbook value-heavy: a preflop 3‑bet, a flop raise and now a larger turn raise. The math is simple — I must call 1800 to win a 4800 pot, so I need ~27% equity. With Q2o my only realistic river outs are three Qs and three 2s (≈6 outs → ≈13% raw equity), and several of those outs will still lose to Ax, two‑pair or better. Given Flash’s range (strong A‑x, Jx, sets, two‑pairs, and many dominated broadway combinations from the 3‑bet/raise line), plus the fact he has a shorter stack and can apply pressure, calling or bluff‑raising is not profitable enough. Preserve a big stack and fold to this turn raise — pick a better spot to apply aggression.

In a heads-up situation, Queen-Eight offsuit is a solid hand to open from the Button/Small Blind. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) allows me to take the initiative and potentially take down the blinds or see a flop with a range advantage.

Flash (button/SB) opened to 300 heads-up. From the big blind with K6o (Ks6h) this is a clear defend — heads-up open ranges are wide and calling is cheap. I need to put 200 more into a 400 pot (total commitment 300), giving ~3:1 pot odds (need ~25% equity). K6o can make top-pair or usable Kx and has enough showdown value and implied odds with deep stacks to continue postflop. A 3‑bet isn’t attractive with this marginal hand and folding is too tight heads-up. I’ll call to see a flop and plan to continue when I hit (top pair or strong draws) or check/fold to heavy aggression on dry misses.

Heads-up out of position on a very dry Q♣7♦2♥ flop. I hold K♠6♥ — missed the flop (only K-high) with no realistic draws (flush impossible by river with only one heart in hand and one on board). I defended preflop expecting to see a flop and planned to continuation-check/fold on dry misses. Facing an aggressive opener on the button, leading out as a bluff OOP is high-variance and likely to be met with resistance. I’ll check to control the pot and either take a free turn or fold to meaningful pressure; only if I pair my King or pick up a strong read will I continue aggressively. For now: check.

On a dry flop of Queen-Seven-Deuce, holding top pair with Queen-Eight is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. Since Minni checked, I should place a continuation bet for value. A bet of 300 (half the pot) is appropriate to extract value from middle pairs, small pairs, or drawing hands, while protecting my equity against overcards like King-Ten or Ace-Jack.

Heads‑up on a very dry Q♣7♦2♥ board I checked expecting to defend cheaply but fold to heavy pressure. Flash’s 300 bet into a 900 pot is only ~1/3 pot, so calling 300 gives me ~4:1 pot odds — cheap to see a turn. K6o missed but has some showdown value and blocks some Kx combos; raising OOP as a bluff here is high variance and folding surrenders too much equity against a wide c‑betting button. I’ll call the 300 to see a turn and then plan: if I pair my King or pick up a good read/draw I can proceed, but I’ll fold to significant turn aggression when still unimproved.

The turn (Qc 7d 2h Qs) pairs the board and makes many of Flash’s c-bet/raise hands (any Qx) extremely strong. I hold K♠6♥ — no pair, no realistic draw, and very little showdown equity versus a wide but aggressive HU opener. With the pot ~1200 and facing no bet right now, I should not bloat the pot out of position with a thin bluff that’s easily called by Qx, pair+kicker, or even Kx occasionally. Check to control the pot and fold to meaningful aggression. If Flash checks back I can see a cheap river; if he bets I’ll fold. Betting or raising OOP here is high-variance and unnecessary with essentially air.

I have improved to trips on the turn. With the board being very dry (Q-7-2-Q), it is unlikely my opponent is on a draw. I will bet 600 into the 1200 pot to extract value from any 7, small pocket pair, or sticky Ace-high hands that Minni may be holding. This sizing is intended to keep her range wide while building the pot.

I hold K♠6♥ on Q♣7♦2♥Q♠. Flash opened pre, barreled the flop (300) and now turns up another bet of 600. The board pairing the queen makes Flash’s line strongly skewed toward Qx and other value hands. I have no pair and no real draw — only 6 outs (three kings + three sixes) to make a single pair on the river, roughly 6/46 ≈ 13% equity. Calling 600 here would make the pot ~2,400, so I’d need about 25% equity to break even. My raw equity (~13%) is well below that, so a call is -EV. Raising OOP as a bluff with K6 is high-variance and unlikely to fold enough of Flash’s value-heavy range. With a healthy stack and many hands left, preserving chips is correct. I will fold to this turn aggression.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with Js6d — this is a marginal but playable hand and I want to take the initiative. In heads-up play I should open a wide range and apply pressure; raising gains fold equity and lets me play postflop in position. A standard open to the min-raise (200 total, 2x BB) is size-efficient: it folds many worse hands, keeps the pot manageable against a 3-bet, and preserves my deep-stack postflop flexibility (I have 26500 vs Flash's 13350). Calling 50 is too passive and folding is too weak. I will raise to 200 and plan to continue in position; re-evaluate if Flash 3-bets large.

With 9c7c, I have a decent suited connector in the Big Blind. Minni's raise to 200 is a standard small raise, and I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call. This hand has good playability post-flop, making it a clear defend in a heads-up situation despite being out of position.

The flop is extremely unfavorable for my hand, consisting of three spades and two high cards (Ace and King) that likely hit Minni's pre-flop raising range. With 9c7c, I have no pair, no draws, and no spades. The standard play in the Big Blind on this texture against the pre-flop aggressor is to check. I have zero equity and will fold to almost any bet size.

Flop: A♠ K♠ 5♠. I hold J♠6♦ — no pair but a strong spade draw (9 outs, ~19% to hit on the turn, ~35% to the river). Heads‑up I opened preflop and Flash checked, which often indicates weakness or pot control. I want to apply pressure with a semi‑bluff: a half‑pot c‑bet continues my range (representing A/K or a made spade), folds a lot of air, but keeps the pot manageable if called. Bet 200 into the 400 pot (50% pot). If Flash folds I take it down; if called I still have good equity and can reassess on the turn; if he raises large I will respect it and fold most of my non‑made hands.

The flop of 5s As Ks is extremely coordinated and hits Minni's pre-flop raising range much harder than mine. With 9c7c, I have absolutely no equity on this board—no pair, no draws, and no spades. Even though the bet size is small (200 into 400), there is no justification for continuing with a hand that has zero ways to improve to a winner. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 8d6s, a speculative hand. With a stack of 13,200 and blinds at 50/100, I have a healthy stack. In heads-up play, 86o is strong enough to open or call most of the time from the small blind to see a flop. Since Minni has a significant lead, I want to keep the pots manageable. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand or potentially take it down pre-flop.

I’m in the big blind with 8c7s facing a button open to 300. Heads-up this is a playable, well-connected hand with good equity and postflop playability against a wide opening range. The pot is 400 and it costs 200 to call (total commitment 300 including my posted BB), which offers favourable pot/implied odds and keeps the pot manageable out of position. I’ll call to see a flop and play aggressively when I connect (pairs, two-pair, strong draws); if I miss and face heavy pressure I can fold. A small 3-bet here would bloat the pot and risk unnecessary commitment versus an unknown opener, so calling is the +EV line.

Out of position on A‑3‑2 with 8c7s I missed — no pair, only a weak backdoor club and very little equity vs a wide but Ace‑heavy opening range. Betting OOP with pure air just bloats the pot and is exploitable against a typical button c‑bet frequency. I want pot control and the option to see a turn for free (or fold to a sizable C‑bet). Plan: check and re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to a large bet, call small probes sometimes to float versus an aggressive opponent or continue if I pick up a club/pair.

On an Ace-high board like 3c-2c-Ad, the pre-flop aggressor typically has a range advantage. Even though 8d6s has no connection to this board, a continuation bet is a standard play to represent the Ace and force folds from Minni's non-Ace hands like K-high or small pocket pairs. I will bet 300 into the 600 pot to keep the pressure on.

Flash has c-bet 300 into ~900 (small ~1/3-pot) on A‑3‑2 with two clubs. I missed the flop with 8c7s — no pair, only a backdoor club line (I have 3 clubs total, need both turn+river to make a flush — very low runout) and 6 simple pairing outs. Raising out of position with pure air would bloat the pot and be exploitable. Folding here surrenders good pot odds: calling 300 to win ~1200 requires ~25% equity. Calling keeps the pot manageable, gives me the option to float and apply pressure if Flash shows weakness on the turn, and preserves stack flexibility. Plan: call the 300, re-evaluate on the turn — fold to heavy turn aggression if I don’t improve, attack if I pick up a club or a pair.

Board: 3c 2c Ad 9c. My hand: 8c7s — no pair but I picked up a four-card club draw (and still have pair-outs). That’s roughly 14 unique outs (~30% to improve to a pair/flush on the river). Pot is 1200. I’ll semi-bluff and apply pressure as I’ve improved on the turn: bet ~½ pot to fold out weaker A‑highs, Kx and small pairs, deny a free river, and build the pot when I hit. If Flash raises big I’ll respect the raise and fold (don’t want to get stacked as a draw); I can call smaller resistance to realize equity or fold to heavy aggression.

On the turn, the board now shows three clubs and an Ace. I have 8-high with no clubs and no straight draws. Minni's lead of 600 into the 1200 pot after check-calling the flop suggests she either has an Ace and is betting for protection/value against the flush draw, or she has completed a flush or a straight. Since I have no equity in this pot and a bluff would be extremely expensive and unlikely to succeed against a lead on this board texture, I must fold.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening extremely wide. 2♣3♥ is weak but has some play (wheel/straight potential) and, more importantly, raising takes the initiative and puts pressure on Flash’s wide BB defense. The min-raise to 200 is the standard heads-up open size here — it’s small enough to keep the pot controllable with deep stacks but large enough to discourage cheap equity for the BB. Plan: fold to a sizeable 3‑bet; call only very small 3‑bets where implied odds justify it; if called, continuation-bet frequently on flops I can represent and check or pot-control on dry, overcard-heavy boards. Objective is to steal the blind and play position. I will raise to 200.

I am holding Jack of Diamonds and Ten of Hearts in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind, meaning I need to call 100 more to see a flop. My hand is a decent middle-broadway connector and is definitely strong enough to defend the Big Blind against a 2x open, especially with the pot odds being 3-to-1. I will call and see a flop.

On a board of Ah-Qh-Jh, I have bottom pair (Jacks) and an inside straight draw to the nuts (needing a King). This is a very coordinated board that hits a pre-flop raiser's range hard. As the Big Blind, I am first to act post-flop. I should check here to see how Minni reacts. My hand has some showdown value and good equity to improve, but it is not strong enough to lead out into a range that contains many Aces and Queens.

I opened wide preflop (200) and got called; flop is J♥ Q♥ A♠. I hold 2♣3♥ — no pair, no real straight draw and only a single heart (so no meaningful flush draw). This board heavily favors the caller’s defending range (many Ax, Qx, Jx, broadways and some heart combos). A c-bet here would be a high-variance bluff with limited fold equity against a wide, often-Ace-heavy range, and with deep stacks I don’t want to build a big pot with essentially nothing. I’ll check to control the pot size, preserve my stack, and see how Flash reacts. Plan: fold to a sizable bet or check‑raise; if Flash checks back I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and look for a safe bluffing card or a chance to take it away on later streets only if the texture becomes more favorable or I pick up additional equity.

On a board of Jh-Qh-As-Kd, my Jd-Th completes the broadway straight (A-K-Q-J-10). This is currently the absolute nuts, as there is no possibility for a flush or a full house yet. After both players checked the flop, it is important to start building the pot for value. Minni's check on the flop suggests she likely doesn't have a monster, but she could easily have an Ace, a King, or a straight draw that will call a bet. I will lead out with a bet of 300 into the 400 pot to extract value and protect against potential heart draws on the river.

I opened wide preflop and checked the flop to control the pot. The board is J♥ Q♥ A♠ K♦ and I hold 2♣3♥ — no pair, no meaningful draw (the backdoor heart is now dead after the K♦). Flash checked the flop and now bets 300 into a 700 pot; calling 300 requires roughly 23% equity (300/(700+300+300) ≈ 23%), which I do not have. Flash’s line (check then a small turn bet) is consistent with many value hands here (A, K, Q, J) and some thin bluffs, but not with enough bluff frequency to make a call or a bluff-raise profitable. Raising as a bluff is high variance and unnecessary given my large stack advantage — I can pick better spots to apply pressure. Fold and preserve chips for better opportunities.

I am on the button with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. With only 14 hands remaining in the tournament and being significantly behind in chips, I need to play aggressively to maximize value and potentially close the gap. Raising to 300 is a standard open-raise size that builds the pot while maintaining positional control.

Heads-up facing a button open to 300. I’m in the big blind with 5♦6♣ — a marginal, connected hand with straight potential but offsuit and out of position. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 more to see a flop (min‑raise to 500).

Reasoning: heads‑up I should defend a wide range, and 5‑6 has decent equity against a typical button opening range and can flop strong draws/pairs. Calling 200 into the 400 pot gives ~25% break‑even equity, which is attractive. I don’t want to 3‑bet here with this offsuit connector OOP — the min 3‑bet would commit me to difficult postflop spots and make me vulnerable to 4‑bets. Folding is too nitty heads‑up and misses good implied‑odds spots.

Plan: call to see a flop, play cautiously out of position. If I hit a pair or strong draw I’ll continue; on dry, high card flops I’ll pot‑control or fold to heavy aggression. Preserve stack and realize equity rather than bloat the pot with a weak bluff.

Flop checked to me heads‑up. With 5♦6♣ on J♥ Q♦ 2♥ I have no pair and only weak backdoor draws (straight/flush require running cards). Flash checking narrows their range to both air and medium strength hands; betting here would be a pure bluff with limited fold equity and could commit me OOP with little equity if raised. I prefer to pot‑control and realize equity — check and see a turn card. If I improve (pair or a strong draw) I can take the initiative; if he bets on the turn I can re‑evaluate then.

I have flopped top pair with the best possible kicker (Ace) on a Queen-Jack-Deuce board. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up match. The board is somewhat coordinated with straight and flush draws possible, so I need to bet for both value and protection. Minni has checked to me, so I will make a continuation bet of 400, which is approximately two-thirds of the pot, to build value against weaker Queens, Jacks, or various draws.

Heads‑up I defended preflop with 5♦6♣ intending to realize equity and pot‑control. The pot is 1000 after Flash’s 400 c‑bet; calling 400 gives me 1400 to win so I need ~28.6% equity to break even. On J♥ Q♦ 2♥ my clean outs are limited (three 5s + three 6s ≈ 6 outs → ~24% to make a pair by river, plus tiny runner‑runner chances), so pure raw equity is a bit below the break‑even threshold. However this is heads‑up against a wide button opening/c‑betting range that contains many bluffs and broadway hands I can still outdraw, and my stack depth makes calling inexpensive relative to potential implied odds. Raising as a bluff OOP with no pair or relevant blockers is a losing line and folding is too tight given the dynamics. I’ll call to see a turn, commit further only if I improve or get a favorable spot — otherwise fold to heavy turn aggression.

Board is 2♥ Q♦ J♥ T♥ and I hold 5♦6♣ — I have no pair, no hearts and only six clean outs (the remaining 5s and 6s) — roughly ~13% to improve by the river. The turn also brought a third heart, so many hands in Flash’s open/c-bet range (two‑heart combos, Qx/Jx, Kx with straight/flush possibilities) now dominate me. Pot control OOP is the prudent line: I defended preflop and called a small flop c-bet to realize equity, but with almost no showdown value on this turn I don’t want to bloat the pot or bluff into a range that has many made hands. I’ll check and plan to fold to meaningful turn aggression; if Flash checks back we can re-evaluate the river cheaply.

On the turn, the board has become extremely coordinated with the Ten of Hearts, completing several potential straights (like K-9 or 9-8) and making a flush possible. While I have top pair with an Ace kicker and the nut flush draw (Ace of Hearts), checking back is the most prudent play. This allows me to control the pot size and realize my significant equity for free. If the river completes my flush or straight, I can bet for value. If the river is a blank, I still have showdown value with top pair. Checking also avoids facing a check-raise from Minni, which would put me in a difficult position given the tournament situation and stack sizes.

I have 5♦6♣ on J♥ Q♦ T♥ 2♥ / 2♦ — no pair, no flush/straight, essentially zero showdown value. Flash opened and c‑bet the flop (I called) then checked the turn, which signals weakness or pot‑control; the river pairing the deuce doesn't improve me and doesn't credibly give me extra fold equity because I don't block 2s, Qs, Js or Ts. Many hands in his range (Qx, Jx, Tx, and made straights like K9/98) will call a river bluff, while the hands I might fold out are a relatively small fraction. Pot is 1400 and it costs me nothing to check to showdown; risking a bluff here is unnecessary and likely -EV. I’ll check and take the showdown line — fold to any river raise or large bet.

I have top pair with the best possible kicker on a board that ended up quite coordinated. I flopped top pair (Queens) and bet for value. The turn brought the Ten of Hearts, completing various straights (K-9, 9-8) and making a flush possible. I checked the turn for pot control and to realize my equity. The river paired the Deuce, which is mostly a blank unless Minni has exactly a Deuce. Minni has checked for the third time. While I might get thin value from a Jack or a weaker Queen, the board is very 'wet' and I have significant showdown value. Given the tournament situation where I am behind in chips, I prefer to check back, ensure I win the pot if I have the best hand, and avoid a potentially disastrous check-raise on such a connected board.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening a very wide range. T7o is marginal but perfectly acceptable in heads-up; raising takes the initiative and preserves my positional advantage postflop. I’ll open to a standard 3xBB (300) — big enough to generate fold equity against weaker big blind defenses and consistent with my usual sizing at this table, but small enough not to overcommit with a marginal hand. If Flash 3-bets I can fold or proceed cautiously given his stack and tendencies; if he calls I’ll C-bet favorable flops and use position to realize equity.

I am in the Big Blind with J3o. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Given the current tournament situation (Hand 138 of 150) and the fact that I am significantly behind in chips (13,450 vs 26,150), I need to be selective about the pots I enter. J3o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. While I only need to call 200 more into a pot of 400, I prefer to conserve my stack for a better spot to double up or reclaim chips, rather than defending with a marginal holding against a player who has shown aggression throughout the match.

With only 11 hands remaining in the tournament and being significantly behind in chips, I need to find better spots than 63 offsuit to accumulate chips. In heads-up play, 63o is a weak hand even from the button, and Minni has been playing fairly aggressively. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

Heads-up on the button with Qc6d — a hand that plays well in position against a wide big-blind range. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50, but open-raising to 200 (total) is standard: it takes the initiative, folds out many worse hands, and builds a pot I can play postflop from position. Stacks are deep so the sizing is small relative to effective stacks and keeps options open. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll give up Q6o; if he just calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops (Q/6/low, dry boards) and proceed based on texture and his reaction.

With only 11 hands remaining in the tournament and holding a significant stack deficit compared to Minni, I need to be selective about the hands I defend from the Big Blind. While the price to call is only 100 chips (offering 3:1 pot odds), Queen-Deuce offsuit is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position. I prefer to preserve my stack for more favorable spots or potential all-in opportunities with hands that have better equity or playability, rather than 'leaking' chips in marginal post-flop situations.

I am dealt a pocket pair of sixes (6s 6h) on the button. In a heads-up situation, a pocket pair is a very strong hand. My opponent Minni has a significant chip lead, but with 10 hands remaining in the tournament, I need to look for opportunities to increase my stack without taking excessive risks. I will start by making a standard raise to 300 to take control of the hand and build the pot with a hand that is likely ahead pre-flop.

Heads-up in the BB with pocket 5s facing a standard SB open to 300. Pot is 400 and I only need to call 200 (300 total committed). With deep stacks (26,600 vs 13,000) I get good pot odds and excellent implied odds to set-mine. I'm out of position, so I prefer pot control and seeing a flop rather than bloating the pot with a 3-bet OOP. A raise is a reasonable alternative to take the initiative, but it needlessly enlarges the pot and risks a bigger commitment against an aggressive button. Folding is too tight. I will call and re-evaluate postflop.

I'm OOP with pocket 5s on 4♦ 9♠ 6♣ — a vulnerable middle/small pair with only two outs to a set. The board is relatively dry but 9 and 6 are overcards; Flash opened wide from the button so he'll c‑bet frequently. With deep stacks I want pot control and to realize equity — checking keeps the pot manageable and lets me call small continuation bets (and fold to large raises). Plan: check here; call a standard c‑bet (~≤1/2 pot) and reevaluate on the turn, fold to big raises or heavy pressure since my hand is marginal. If Flash checks back I reach showdown cheaply.

I have flopped a set of sixes, which is a powerhouse on this 9-6-4 rainbow board. My opponent, Minni, has checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise. To maximize value from 9x hands, straight draws like 78 or 57, and potential overcards, I will place a continuation bet. A bet of 400 into a 600 pot is a standard value-building size that keeps her range wide and encourages a call.

I have 5♣5♦ on 4♦ 9♠ 6♣ — a medium/weak pair OOP against the button. Flash raised wide pre and is c‑betting a small amount (400 into a 1000 pot ≈40% pot), which often represents a wide range that misses this dry board. With deep stacks I prefer to control the pot and realize equity rather than inflate the pot OOP with a raise. Calling 400 keeps worse hands and bluffs in, gives good implied odds to hit a set, and lets me reevaluate on the turn. Plan: call the small c‑bet, fold to extreme turn pressure on scary cards (big overcards or coordinated turns), and continue vs. checks or small turn bets.

Check. The turn 9 pairs the board and my pocket fives are now a marginal, easily dominated hand — I’m behind any 9, any 6 or 4, and most pocket pairs. I called the small flop c‑bet to realize equity and control the pot; there are only two outs to a full house on the river, so this is not a hand I want to inflate OOP. With a 2:1 chip advantage I don’t need to gamble to win this pot—checking keeps the pot small and lets me fold versus real strength. Plan: check now, see Flash’s action on the river, fold to a meaningful bet, and only call very small river bluffs or river bets that credibly can be missed c‑bet bluffs.

On the turn, the board is 4d-9s-6c-9d and I am holding 6s-6h. I have improved from a set of sixes on the flop to a full house (sixes full of nines). This is an extremely strong hand that beats all 'trips' (9x) hands, which are a major part of Minni's calling range given the board texture. The only hands that beat me are 9-4, 9-6, or pocket nines, all of which are statistically unlikely. Since Minni has checked again, I will place a value bet to build the pot. A bet of 800 into the 1400 pot is a strong sizing that targets her 9x hands and overpairs, while still potentially getting called by draws that are now drawing dead.

Board: 4♦ 9♠ 6♣ 9♦. I hold 5♦5♣. Pot is 2,200 and I must call 800 (need ≈26.7% equity to justify a call). My only clean outs are the two remaining 5s (~4.3% to hit a set on the river), so calling purely to hit is grossly -EV. Flash opened pre, c‑bet the flop and barreled the turn — that line is very consistent with value (any 9, hands with a 6/4, or pocket pairs), and while some two‑street bluffs exist they’re unlikely to be frequent enough here to overcome my lack of equity. Raising OOP with a vulnerable middle pair into this line would bloat the pot with little fold equity and is too risky. I checked the turn intending to fold to meaningful pressure; that’s the right play now. Fold and preserve my deep stack for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with A2o in the small blind — a very strong heads-up hand. I want initiative and to use position postflop, so I’ll open-raise rather than limp/call. The min-raise to 200 is a standard, efficient size here: it gives fold equity against the BB, keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks (25.95k vs 13.9k), and avoids letting the opponent see cheap flops with a wide range. If Flash 3-bets large I can fold or reevaluate depending on sizing; if he calls I’ll plan a continuation bet on favorable, dry flops and pot-control on coordinated boards. Raise to 200 to seize initiative and play position with a top heads-up hand.

In Hand #142, I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Clubs and Four of Diamonds. Minni, who holds a significant chip lead, has min-raised to 200. Although I am getting 3:1 odds to call (100 more into a 300 pot), T4o is a very weak hand with poor playability out of position. Given that we are at hand 142 of 150, I need to be selective about the pots I enter to avoid bleeding chips against a larger stack. Folding here preserves my stack for better opportunities in the final few hands.

I am in the Small Blind/Button position with 8d 5s. While heads-up play requires a wide opening range, 85o is quite weak. Given that I am currently trailing Minni by a significant margin and there are only 8 hands remaining in the tournament, I need to be selective about the pots I enter to avoid bleeding chips. 85o does not navigate well post-flop, and Minni has shown a willingness to defend the Big Blind and play aggressively. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips in the final few hands.

Heads-up on the button with A♠9♣ — a strong, positionally advantaged hand. With deep stacks (26.1k vs 13.75k) I want to open for value and fold equity while keeping postflop options. Flash has been active, so I take a standard 3x BB sizing to 300 total: builds the pot, prices out worse hands, and still allows me to fold or call a meaningful 3-bet without being pot-committed. If called I’ll use my positional advantage to c-bet or steer the pot; if met with a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold or proceed cautiously depending on sizing).

I am in the Big Blind with 5s 3s. Minni, who has a significantly larger stack and has been very aggressive from the button, has raised to 300. I need to call 200 more into a total pot that will be 600, giving me 3:1 pot odds. While 53s is a weak hand, in a heads-up match, suited gappers have enough playability to defend against a standard open, especially with the favorable price. I will call to see a flop and look for opportunities to outplay Minni or hit a strong draw, but I will remain disciplined if the board does not improve my hand.

On a board of 8s 6d Td, I have 5-high with only a backdoor spade flush draw. This board texture is quite connected and likely favors the pre-flop raiser's range or hits middle-card combinations that Minni frequently plays. There is no reason to lead out with a bet here given I have no made hand and very little equity. I will check and most likely fold to any significant bet from Minni.

Heads-up on the button with A♠9♣ after opening preflop to 300 and getting called. Flop is T♦ 8♠ 6♦ (pot 600) and Flash checks. I have the best single-card (Ace-high) and a usable backdoor/gutshot-style draw (a 7 completes 6-7-8-9-10), plus position — good reasons to take the initiative. A c-bet pressures many of Flash's floating/call-down hands, charges diamond draws (two diamonds on board), and gets value from weaker pairs/overcards that will often fold. I also prefer a sizing that gives decent fold equity without bloating the pot with a marginal hand. Bet ~2/3 pot (400): if he folds we take it; if he calls I can re-evaluate on the turn (continue with equity or fold vs big aggression). If he check-raises large, that usually indicates a ten/strong made hand and I will proceed cautiously or fold depending on sizing.

On a board of 8s-6d-Td, I have 5-high with only a backdoor spade flush draw. Minni has bet 400 into a 600 pot. This board texture is very coordinated and likely hits many parts of a standard opening range. With no made hand and very little equity beyond a thin backdoor draw, calling is unprofitable. I will fold and wait for a better spot to contest a pot in the remaining hands.

We are currently on hand 145 of 150. With only 6 hands remaining, I am trailing Minni by approximately 13,000 chips. My current hand, 8-5 offsuit, is extremely weak even for heads-up play. While I want to close the gap, trying to force a play with junk cards against a larger stack is more likely to result in further losses than a significant gain. Given that I am in the small blind and the cost to fold is only 50 chips, I will choose to preserve my stack and wait for a more favorable hand or a better opportunity to bluff in the remaining five hands.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with 8♥7♠ — a playable, connected hand that benefits a lot from position. The pot is 150 and it only costs me 50 to complete, but I’d rather take the initiative and apply pressure with a standard heads-up open. Raising to 200 (2x BB) achieves that: it gives fold equity, keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks, and lets me play postflop in position where 87o has good equity and straight/board-coverage potential. If Flash 3-bets big I can release this hand; if he calls, I’ll use position to continue or probe on favorable flops.

We are currently in the big blind with Js 5h. Minni, on the button, has raised to 200, which is a min-raise. The pot is offering us 3:1 odds to call (100 more to call a 300 total pot). While our hand is weak, we have a significant stack and are nearing the end of the tournament (hand 146 of 150). However, J5o is very weak against a standard button opening range, and out of position, it's difficult to play profitably. Given our goal is to maximize the final stack and we are already trailing significantly behind Minni, we shouldn't bleed chips on marginal defended blinds. That said, a fold is also reasonable, but at 3:1 odds, calling to see a flop is often the standard professional play in heads-up to prevent the button from stealing with 100% of hands. But looking at the hand history, Minni has been quite aggressive and successful. I will choose to fold this weak hand and wait for a better spot in the final 4 hands.

Holding Ten-Six offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation, I'll put in a standard raise to 300. This hand is well within a typical opening range for the small blind. Given that we are nearing the end of the tournament (Hand 147/150) and I'm currently trailing in chips, I need to stay active to look for opportunities to close the gap, while also being prepared to fold if Minni shows significant strength.

Heads-up in the big blind with A♥Q♥ facing a 300 open from the button. This is a top-tier heads-up hand — I want to take the initiative rather than letting a wide SB range see a cheap flop out of position. My stack (26,500 ≈132bb) vs Flash (13,100 ≈65bb) lets me apply pressure without committing stacks. I 3-bet to 1,000 (about 3.3x the open): big enough to get value from worse aces, broadways and pairs and to fold out many speculative hands, but small enough to keep postflop playability and preserve fold equity. If Flash 4-bets big/all-in I’ll re-evaluate (AQs is good but not invincible vs a polarized 4-bet).

In Hand 147 of 150, I am trailing Minni by approximately 12,500 chips. While I need to find opportunities to gain ground, Ten-Six offsuit is a very weak hand to defend against a 3-bet, even in a heads-up situation. Minni's 3-bet to 1000 indicates significant strength or a willingness to apply pressure as the chip leader. Calling 700 more to see a flop with a marginal hand and a disadvantageous stack size is not a winning long-term strategy. With only three hands remaining after this, I prefer to fold and preserve my 12,800 chips to look for a better spot to double up or pick up a pot in the final few hands.

Heads-up on the button with 7♦5♣: this is a weak but perfectly playable hand in position. I want to apply pressure and use position rather than passively calling 50. The minimum (200) is small and can invite weird squeezes, so I open to a standard, credible size (300 total) to get fold equity and build a pot when I have position. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this hand; if he calls I’ll c-bet or use position to realize equity on favorable flops. Overall this raise maximizes my EV vs a wide BB defending range while risking only a small portion of the effective stacks.

With only three hands remaining in the tournament, I am currently trailing Minni's stack significantly (13,000 to 26,600). In this hand, I am in the Big Blind with 8c 6d, which is a weak starting hand. Minni has opened for 300 from the button. While I am getting 3:1 odds to call, 86o is difficult to play out of position, and Minni has shown consistent aggression post-flop. Given the stack disparity and the limited time left, I prefer to fold this marginal hand and look for a better spot or a higher equity hand in the final two rounds to try and close the gap.

Hand #149 of 150. I am on the button with Jack-Seven offsuit. In heads-up play, this is a standard hand to open. Given the tournament is ending very soon, I want to continue playing a standard aggressive strategy to try and chip up. Minni has been active, but J7o is strong enough to justify a standard 3x open raise.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I have Q♦ J♥ — a strong heads-up defending hand but not a premium to bloat the pot out of position. 3-betting from the big blind would leave me OOP postflop and could commit chips unnecessarily against a wide but often calling opening range. Plan: proceed aggressively on favorable flops (Q/J/straight draws), exercise pot control and fold to heavy action on A/K heavy boards, and re-evaluate if Flash shows significant strength pre- or post-flop.

I have Q♦ J♥ on 8♥ 2♥ 4♣ — two clean overcards and a backdoor heart but no pair. I’m out of position and this is a low, disconnected board, so I want pot control and to realize equity rather than bloat the pot OOP. If Flash checks back I take a free turn and can barrel or stab if a Q/J or a favorable heart/texture appears.

On a rag board of 8-2-4 after raising pre-flop, I have a standard continuation bet opportunity. Minni checked to me, and while I only have Jack-high, this board doesn't favor the big blind's calling range significantly unless they have a small pair or hit an 8. I will bet 300 (half pot) to try and take the pot down now.

Call 300. Heads-up on a dry 8♥-2♥-4♣ flop vs a button who opened pre and now c‑bet small (~1/3 pot) — a range that’s often wide. Q♦J♥ has two clean overcards plus a backdoor heart possibility; folding is too tight and check‑raising/raising OOP with just overcards is unnecessarily bloating the pot. Calling realizes equity cheaply, keeps pot under control OOP, and lets me re-evaluate on the turn: if I hit a Q/J or pick up a heart I can become aggressive; if I miss and face big turn pressure I’ll fold. Stack sizes and pot odds support a call here.

Check. The turn A♥ drastically changes the board dynamic — it spikes a card that sits squarely in the button’s opening/c-bet range (Ax, paired hands, and plenty of hearts). I have Q♦J♥: my flop overcards are now under an Ace and I only have a one-card heart draw (9 hearts ≈19–20% to hit on the river) plus the usual Q/J pairing outs. That gives me reasonable but not great equity (~30% to make something), and many of those outs will still be second-best if Flash holds an Ace or a higher heart.

I’m out of position and want pot control. Checking keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity on the river, and lets Flash show more of his strength. Plan: if Flash checks back I can bet a Q/J or a heart on the river for value; if he bets a small-to-moderate sized bet (≈≤½ pot) I’ll call to see the river; if he pressures with a large bet or a raise I’ll fold — the turn Ace makes folding to heavy action correct.

The Ace of Hearts on the turn is a significant scare card that heavily favors my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor. Since I followed up with a continuation bet on the flop, a second barrel here is highly credible, representing either a top pair of Aces or a completed heart flush. Minni has checked to me again, suggesting she likely holds a marginal hand like a pair of eights or a pocket pair that will find it very difficult to call a substantial bet on this board. With only two hands remaining in the tournament, I need to capitalize on these board textures to increase my stack. I will bet 800 into the 1200 pot to maximize the pressure on her mid-range holdings.

Call 800. The turn A♥ significantly improves Flash’s range (Ax, A with a heart, and any two hearts), so my Q♦J♥ is behind to many value hands, but the sizing is moderate. I’m getting ~28.6% pot‑odds to call (800 to win ~2800). My realistic improvement outs on the river (9 hearts + Q♣/Q♠ + J♣/J♦/J♠ = ~14 unique cards) give roughly ~30% to hit a pair/flush, so a flat call is marginally +EV. I’m OOP and don’t want to bloat the pot with a raise holding only overcards and a one-card heart draw; folding is too passive given the odds. Plan: see the river — if I pair or make the flush I’ll go for value; if I miss and face heavy pressure I’ll fold, and if Flash checks I can take the pot or stab small depending on the river card.

Check. I have Q♦J♥ and missed — the river pairs the board (2s on board now) and my only live outs are Q/J which are unlikely to be good given Flash’s line. Flash opened, c‑bet the flop and then fired a large turn — that line is heavily weighted to value (Ax, 2x, 8x, or two‑heart combos that had turn equity). Betting as a bluff here has very little fold equity against that line and I have no value to extract, so the lowest‑variance +EV play is to check and go to showdown (preserve stack and avoid throwing good chips away on a thin bluff).

On the river, the board pairs deuces. I have been representing a strong hand (Ace or flush) with my bets on the flop and turn. Minni has called both, which indicates she likely has a piece of the board—most likely an 8, a pocket pair, or potentially an Ace she is playing passively. Since she called the turn bet when the Ace (a major scare card) hit, she is unlikely to fold to a river bluff. I have Jack-high, which has almost no showdown value, but at this stage of the tournament (Hand 149 of 150), preserving my remaining stack of 11,600 for the final hand is more strategic than risking a large portion of it on a bluff that is likely to be called by her range. I will check and hope to see a missed draw, but I am prepared to lose this pot to maintain my stack for the final hand.

Heads-up with Ad8h on the button (I posted the SB). A8o is a strong heads-up opening hand — worth playing aggressively. The pot is 150 and calling 50 is passive and gives the BB cheap equity. I’ll raise to 300 total (standard ~3x BB, above the 200 minimum) to seize initiative, leverage my positional advantage on the flop, build the pot with a strong Ace, and deny good odds to speculative hands. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold given a big 3-bet with this kicker); if he calls I’ll c-bet appropriate textures.

This is the final hand of the tournament. I am significantly behind Minni in total chips, making it nearly impossible to overtake the lead in a single hand unless an all-in situation occurs and I win. However, my goal is still to maximize my final stack. Queen-Six offsuit is a standard defending hand in the Big Blind against a 3x open in heads-up play. I will call the additional 200 to see the flop and play out the final hand.

On this final hand of the tournament, I have flopped middle pair (Queens) on a highly coordinated board of Kc-Qc-Jd. This board texture is very favorable for the pre-flop raiser's range, as it contains many cards that connect with high-card hands. With middle pair and a weak kicker, my hand has some showdown value but is vulnerable to any King, two-pair, or straight. I will check to the aggressor to control the size of the pot and see how Minni responds, aiming to reach a showdown or navigate the turn safely.

I opened preflop and have position with A♦8♥ — the Ace gives me a high-card advantage and blocks some Ax combos. The flop K♣ Q♣ J♦ is very coordinated, but as the preflop aggressor I should continuation-bet to take the initiative and fold out weaker holdings (small pairs, random broadways, some backdoor draws). A c-bet of ~2/3 pot applies meaningful pressure without overcommitting — if Flash check-raises large I’ll respect it and fold this marginal Ace; if he calls I can reevaluate on the turn (continue with A or favorable runouts, or control the pot on blanks). Given our chip lead I want to leverage fold equity and keep him guessing.

In the final hand of the tournament, I have middle pair (Queens) on a board that is extremely coordinated. Minni's 400-chip bet is a standard continuation bet on a board that hits her opening range well. While my kicker is weak, middle pair in a heads-up scenario is too strong to fold to a single bet on the flop. I will call the bet to see a turn card and evaluate. Raising would likely only get called by hands that have me beaten (Kings or better Queens), so calling is the most efficient way to keep the pot manageable while retaining showdown value.

On the turn, the Deuce of Spades is a complete brick that does not change the board texture. I currently hold middle pair (Queens) with a weak kicker on a very coordinated board (King-Queen-Jack). Since Minni was the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, and this board hits a Button opening range very hard, I will continue to check to her. My goal is to keep the pot manageable and reach a showdown in this final hand of the tournament, as my hand has some value but cannot stand a large amount of pressure given the board connects with so many overcards and straights.

I opened preflop and continued on the flop to take the initiative. The turn 2♠ is a blank that doesn't give many new made hands and Flash checked — a clear sign of weakness or pot-control. My A♦8♥ has showdown value vs pure bluffs and blocks A10 straights, but it’s not a made hand. I want to use my position and chip advantage to take the pot now and fold out single-pair and many turn/river floats. A bet around 2/3 pot applies meaningful pressure without overcommitting. If Flash check-raises big I’ll strongly consider folding (I’m likely behind vs K/Q/J or a made straight), if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the river and proceed cautiously for value or to pick a better bluff spot.

On the turn, Minni continues with another bet of 900 into a pot of 1400. The board is Kc-Qc-Jd-2s, and I hold middle pair (Queens) with a 6 kicker. This is the final hand of the tournament. The board texture is very heavy with broadway cards, making it likely Minni has a piece of this or is bluffing with high-card equity. While my kicker is weak, middle pair in a heads-up match is generally worth at least two streets of calling on most boards. Given that I am significantly behind in the overall chip count and this is the last hand, I will call to see the river and attempt to reach a showdown to maximize my final stack. Folding now would be too tight given the strength of middle pair in heads-up play.

On the river, the Ten of Diamonds completes a possible four-card straight on the board (K-Q-J-T). Any player holding an Ace or a Nine now has a straight. My pair of Queens is significantly devalued because I am beaten by any King, any two-pair combo, and any straight. Minni has shown aggression on every street, suggesting she has a strong piece of this board or a high-equity draw that may have just arrived. Since I am first to act and my hand has some showdown value but will not be called by worse hands if I bet, checking is the best option. I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown in this final hand of the tournament.

The river T♦ completes 10‑J‑Q‑K‑A — with A♦8♥ I have the nut Broadway straight. Flash checked to me after calling my flop and turn bets, which implies a sticky/calling range (Kx/Qx/Jx/two pair/sets) or a blocker. This is a pure value spot: I want to extract from two‑pair and sets that will call. A pot‑sized bet applies maximum pressure and should get called often given his earlier calls; I block the Ad so ties by an ace are unlikely. Bet pot to maximize EV.

On the river, the board has coordinated into a four-card straight (K-Q-J-T). Holding only a pair of Queens with a weak kicker, I am beaten by any Ace, any Nine, any two-pair, and any set. Minni has been the aggressor on every street, and her pot-sized bet on the river strongly indicates she has a piece of this board that outdraws a single pair. Since my goal is to maximize my final stack in the last hand of the tournament, calling a large bet with a hand that is almost certainly behind is counterproductive. I will fold to preserve my remaining chips.
